topics.title: Study of 741 Op-Amp and Implementation of Basic Circuits Using 741 - Inverting, Non-Inverting, Voltage Follower
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCICL408 - ANALOG CIRCUITS LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Study of 741 Op-Amp and Implementation of Basic Circuits Using 741 - Inverting, Non-Inverting, Voltage Follower ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 The 741 Operational Amplifier (IC 741) The **µA741** (commonly called the **741 Op-Amp**) is a general-purpose, internally compensated, monolithic operational amplifier built on a single silicon chip using bipolar junction transistor (BJT) technology. It was introduced by Fairchild Semiconductor and is housed in an **8-pin DIP (Dual In-line Package)**. The 741 has become the *de facto* industry standard workhorse for analog circuit design and forms the foundation of virtually all undergraduate analog electronics laboratories worldwide. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCICL408):** The 741 is the primary device studied in the Analog Circuits Lab. Students must be able to identify the **pinout by heart**, measure key DC parameters (offset voltage, bias current), and build the three fundamental closed-loop configurations: **Inverting Amplifier**, **Non-Inverting Amplifier**, and **Voltage Follower (Unity Gain Buffer)**. #### 1.1.1 Pin Configuration of IC 741 (8-Pin DIP) | Pin Number | Symbol | Function | |:----------:|:------:|:---------| | 1 | OFFSET NULL | Offset voltage adjustment terminal A | | 2 | INVERTING INPUT ($V^-$) | Inverting signal input | | 3 | NON-INVERTING INPUT ($V^+$) | Non-inverting signal input | | 4 | $V_{CC}^-$ (or $V^-$) | Negative power supply (typically $-15$ V) | | 5 | OFFSET NULL | Offset voltage adjustment terminal B | | 6 | OUTPUT | Amplified signal output | | 7 | $V_{CC}^+$ (or $V^+$) | Positive power supply (typically $+15$ V) | | 8 | NC | No Connection | > [!NOTE] > **Memory Trick:** A useful mnemonic for the left side of the chip is **"1-Null, 2-Inverting, 3-Non-Inverting, 4-VEE"** and the right side mirrors with **5-Null, 6-Output, 7-VCC, 8-NC**. #### 1.1.2 Ideal vs. Practical 741 — Key DC Parameters | Parameter | Symbol | Ideal Value | Typical 741 Value | |:----------|:------:|:-----------:|:-----------------:| | Open-Loop Voltage Gain | $A_{OL}$ | $\infty$ | **$2 \times 10^5$** (≈ 106 dB) | | Input Impedance | $Z_{in}$ | $\infty$ | **$2 \text{ M}\Omega$** | | Output Impedance | $Z_{out}$ | $0$ | **$75 \text{ }\Omega$** | | Input Bias Current | $I_{B}$ | $0$ | **$80$ nA** | | Input Offset Voltage | $V_{OS}$ | $0$ | **$2$ mV** | | Unity Gain Bandwidth | $f_T$ | $\infty$ | **$1$ MHz** | | Slew Rate | $SR$ | $\infty$ | **$0.5 \text{ V/}\mu\text{s}$** | | Common-Mode Rejection Ratio | CMRR | $\infty$ | **$90$ dB** | | Power Supply Rejection Ratio | PSRR | $\infty$ | **$76$ dB** | ### 1.2 The Three Basic Op-Amp Circuits — Conceptual Overview #### Inverting Amplifier The input signal is applied to the **inverting terminal** through a resistor $R_1$. The output is **$180^\circ$ out of phase** with the input. The non-inverting terminal is grounded. > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Analogy — The "Lever with a Reversed Hinge":** Imagine pushing a stick attached to a seesaw. When you push down on your end, the other end goes up. The output moves in the **opposite direction** of the input, hence the name "inverting." The ratio of the resistor $R_f$ to $R_1$ acts like the gear ratio of the seesaw — bigger $R_f$ means a stronger output swing. #### Non-Inverting Amplifier The input signal is applied to the **non-inverting terminal**. The output is **in phase** with the input. The inverting terminal is connected to a feedback network. > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Analogy — The "Cooperative Partner":** Think of a pal who repeats what you say but louder and clearer, agreeing with your tone. The output is a faithful, **same-direction amplified copy** of the input. The gain is always $\geq 1$, never less than unity. #### Voltage Follower (Unity-Gain Buffer) This is a special case of the non-inverting amplifier where the feedback resistor is **zero** ($\,R_f = 0\,$) and the ground resistor is **infinite** ($\,R_1 = \infty\,$, i.e., open). The output voltage exactly equals the input voltage, but the op-amp provides **current gain** and **impedance isolation**. > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Analogy — The "High-Fidelity Translator":** Imagine a diplomat who speaks a message verbatim but in a louder, more powerful voice to a much larger audience. The *words* (voltage signal) are unchanged, but the *delivery power* (current capability) is dramatically increased. This is the secret to isolating a high-impedance source from a low-impedance load. ### 1.3 Why Are These Three Circuits Foundational? These three configurations form the **building blocks** of nearly all analog signal processing: - **Inverting** → Sums, scales, integrates, differentiates, and creates virtual ground. - **Non-Inverting** → High-input-impedance amplification, instrumentation front-ends. - **Voltage Follower** → Impedance matching, isolation, sample-and-hold, ADC drivers. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Applications of 741 Circuits:** > - **Audio pre-amplifiers** in microphones and musical instruments (non-inverting). > - **Active filters** (low-pass, high-pass, band-pass). > - **Sensor signal conditioning** in biomedical instrumentation (ECG, EEG). > - **ADC/DAC buffer stages** in data acquisition systems. > - **Precision rectifiers** and **peak detectors** in measurement equipment. ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Two Golden Rules of Ideal Op-Amp Analysis Before deriving any circuit, you must internalize the **two golden rules** that govern every negative-feedback op-amp analysis: > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rule 1 — Virtual Short:** When the op-amp is in negative feedback with high open-loop gain, the differential input voltage is essentially zero: $V^+ = V^-$. The two input terminals appear to be *electrically shorted*, even though no current flows between them. > > **Rule 2 — Virtual Open (Infinite Input Impedance):** No current flows into either input terminal of an ideal op-amp: $I^+ = I^- = 0$. These two rules, combined with **Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL)**, allow us to derive *every* op-amp circuit equation by inspection. ### 2.2 Inverting Amplifier — Step-by-Step Logical Analysis **Circuit Topology:** - Input voltage $V_{in}$ is applied through resistor $R_1$ to the **inverting** input (pin 2). - Resistor $R_f$ (feedback) connects the output (pin 6) back to the inverting input. - Non-inverting input (pin 3) is connected directly to **ground**. **Step 1 — Apply Virtual Short Rule:** Since the non-inverting input is grounded, $V^+ = 0$. By Rule 1, the inverting input also becomes $V^- = 0$. This node is called the **virtual ground** (a ground in voltage but not physically connected). **Step 2 — Apply KCL at the Inverting Node:** The current flowing from input to node equals the current flowing from node to output (no current enters the op-amp due to Rule 2): $$\frac{V_{in} - 0}{R_1} = \frac{0 - V_{out}}{R_f}$$ **Step 3 — Solve for the Gain:** $$\frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = -\frac{R_f}{R_1}$$ ### 2.3 Non-Inverting Amplifier — Step-by-Step Logical Analysis **Circuit Topology:** - Input voltage $V_{in}$ is applied directly to the **non-inverting** input (pin 3). - Resistor $R_1$ connects the inverting input to ground. - Resistor $R_f$ provides feedback from output to the inverting input. **Step 1 — Apply Virtual Short Rule:** $V^- = V^+ = V_{in}$. **Step 2 — Voltage Divider Across $R_f$ and $R_1$:** The voltage at the inverting node, as seen from the output, follows a standard voltage divider: $$V^- = V_{out} \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_f}$$ **Step 3 — Equate and Solve:** $$V_{in} = V_{out} \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_f}$$ $$\frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}$$ ### 2.4 Voltage Follower — Step-by-Step Logical Analysis **Circuit Topology:** - The output is **directly connected** back to the inverting input (i.e., $R_f = 0$). - The resistor $R_1$ that normally goes from inverting input to ground is **removed** (i.e., $R_1 = \infty$, open circuit). - $V_{in}$ is applied to the non-inverting input. **Step 1 — Recognize the Special Case:** By taking the limit of the non-inverting gain formula as $R_f \to 0$ and $R_1 \to \infty$: $$\frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = 1 + \frac{0}{\infty} = 1$$ **Step 2 — Apply Virtual Short Directly:** Since pin 2 (inverting) is wired straight to pin 6 (output), and Rule 1 demands $V^- = V^+$, we get: $$V_{out} = V_{in}$$ ### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **The following table contains the must-memorize formulas for KTU Lab Exam, ESE, and competitive exams like GATE. KTU examiners frequently test these exact expressions.** | Circuit Type | Closed-Loop Gain $A_{CL} = \dfrac{V_{out}}{V_{in}}$ | Input Impedance $Z_{in}$ | Output Impedance $Z_{out}$ | Phase Relationship | |:-------------|:------------------------------------------------------:|:------------------------:|:--------------------------:|:------------------:| | **Inverting** | $A_{CL} = -\dfrac{R_f}{R_1}$ | $Z_{in} = R_1$ | $Z_{out} \approx 0$ | $180^\circ$ out of phase | | **Non-Inverting** | $A_{CL} = 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}$ | $Z_{in} \to \infty$ | $Z_{out} \approx 0$ | $0^\circ$ (in phase) | | **Voltage Follower** | $A_{CL} = 1$ | $Z_{in} \to \infty$ | $Z_{out} \approx 0$ | $0^\circ$ (in phase) | #### Supplementary High-Yield Formulas (For 741 + General Op-Amp) | Formula | Expression | Engineering Meaning | |:--------|:----------:|:--------------------| | Differential Input | $V_d = V^+ - V^-$ | The "error" voltage amplified by $A_{OL}$ | | Output Voltage | $V_{out} = A_{OL} \cdot (V^+ - V^-)$ | Open-loop behavior | | Closed-Loop Bandwidth | $f_{CL} = \dfrac{f_T}{\vert A_{CL} \vert}$ | **Gain-Bandwidth Product** (GBP) is constant | | Slew-Rate Limit | $SR \geq \pi \cdot f_{max} \cdot V_{out,pk}$ | Maximum undistorted frequency | | CMRR (dB) | $\text{CMRR} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{A_{d}}{A_{cm}}\right)$ | Common-mode noise rejection | | Virtual Ground Current | $I_1 = \dfrac{V_{in}}{R_1}$ | Input bias current in inverting topology | | Output Saturation | $V_{out} \approx \pm 13$ V (with $\pm 15$ V supply) | 741 cannot swing rail-to-rail | ### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility The **Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBP)** is the single most important spec for the 741 in practice. For IC 741, $f_T \approx 1 \text{ MHz}$. Therefore: $$f_{CL} = \frac{1 \text{ MHz}}{\vert A_{CL} \vert}$$ If you build a non-inverting amplifier with $A_{CL} = 10$, your usable bandwidth is only **$100$ kHz**. This is why the 741 cannot be used in RF or video applications, but remains ideal for audio and instrumentation. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Inverting Amplifier **Given:** An ideal op-amp in the inverting configuration with $V_{in}$ applied through $R_1$ to the inverting node, $R_f$ as the feedback element, and the non-inverting input grounded. **Step 1 — Define the node voltages.** Let $V_a$ be the voltage at the inverting input node (node $a$). Then $V_a = V^-$ and the non-inverting input voltage is $V^+ = 0$ (grounded). **Step 2 — Apply the virtual short principle.** From Rule 1 of the golden rules: $$V_a = V^+ = 0$$ This establishes node $a$ as a **virtual ground**. **Step 3 — Express currents using Ohm's Law.** The current entering node $a$ from the source $V_{in}$ through $R_1$: $$I_1 = \frac{V_{in} - V_a}{R_1} = \frac{V_{in} - 0}{R_1} = \frac{V_{in}}{R_1}$$ The current leaving node $a$ toward the output $V_{out}$ through $R_f$: $$I_f = \frac{V_a - V_{out}}{R_f} = \frac{0 - V_{out}}{R_f} = -\frac{V_{out}}{R_f}$$ **Step 4 — Apply KCL at node $a$ and the virtual-open rule.** All current entering node $a$ must equal all current leaving (no current enters the op-amp input): $$I_1 + I_f = 0 \quad \text{(since } I^- = 0\text{)}$$ $$\frac{V_{in}}{R_1} + \left(-\frac{V_{out}}{R_f}\right) = 0$$ **Step 5 — Solve algebraically for $V_{out}/V_{in}$.** $$\frac{V_{in}}{R_1} = \frac{V_{out}}{R_f}$$ $$V_{out} = -\frac{R_f}{R_1} \cdot V_{in}$$ $$\boxed{A_{CL} = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = -\frac{R_f}{R_1}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Why the negative sign?** The negative sign appears because the op-amp output must drive the inverting node to *zero* (virtual ground). If $V_{in}$ is positive, current flows *into* node $a$ through $R_1$. To sink this current without letting node $a$ rise, the output must go **negative**, drawing current *out* of node $a$ through $R_f$. This polarity inversion is the heart of the inverting topology. ### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — Non-Inverting Amplifier **Given:** $V_{in}$ at the non-inverting terminal, $R_1$ from inverting terminal to ground, $R_f$ from output to inverting terminal. **Step 1 — Apply the virtual short principle.** $$V^- = V^+ = V_{in}$$ **Step 2 — Identify the voltage divider.** The inverting node $V^-$ is formed by the voltage divider between $R_1$ (to ground) and $R_f$ (to output): $$V^- = V_{out} \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_f}$$ This comes from the standard voltage divider formula $V_x = V_{out} \cdot \dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_f}$, where the divider is *unloaded* because no current flows into the op-amp's inverting input (Rule 2). **Step 3 — Equate the two expressions for $V^-$.** $$V_{in} = V_{out} \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_f}$$ **Step 4 — Cross-multiply to isolate $V_{out}$.** $$V_{out} \cdot R_1 = V_{in} \cdot (R_1 + R_f)$$ **Step 5 — Solve algebraically.** $$V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{R_1 + R_f}{R_1} = V_{in} \cdot \left(1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}\right)$$ $$\boxed{A_{CL} = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Why is the gain always $\geq 1$?** Physically, the op-amp must produce an output that is *larger* than $V_{in}$ in order to maintain the inverting node at the same voltage as $V_{in}$ (via the voltage divider). The smallest possible output is $V_{in}$ itself, which occurs only when $R_f = 0$ (a direct wire feedback). This degenerates to the voltage follower with unity gain. ### 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation — Voltage Follower **Given:** Output directly wired to the inverting input; $V_{in}$ at the non-inverting input; no resistors in the feedback path. **Step 1 — Apply virtual short.** The output node is the same physical node as the inverting input: $$V^- = V_{out}$$ By Rule 1: $$V^- = V^+$$ **Step 2 — Substitute $V^+ = V_{in}$.** $$V_{out} = V^+ = V_{in}$$ **Step 3 — Compute the gain.** $$A_{CL} = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = 1$$ $$\boxed{V_{out} = V_{in}, \quad A_{CL} = 1}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Why use a unity-gain buffer if the voltage is unchanged?** Because of the **impedance transformation**. The input impedance is essentially the op-amp's input impedance ($\approx 2 \text{ M}\Omega$ for 741), while the output impedance is near zero ($\approx 75 \text{ }\Omega$). This decouples a high-impedance source (e.g., a sensor) from a low-impedance load (e.g., a speaker) without disturbing the source voltage — a principle called **impedance bridging**. ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Performance Summary | Performance Metric | Inverting | Non-Inverting | Voltage Follower | |:-------------------|:---------:|:-------------:|:----------------:| | **Gain Range** | $-\infty < A < 0$ (any negative value) | $1 \leq A < \infty$ (positive, $\geq 1$) | $A = 1$ exactly | | **Gain Polarity** | Inverted ($-$ sign) | Non-inverted ($+$ sign) | Non-inverted | | **Input Impedance** | Finite ($= R_1$) | Very high ($\approx 2$ M$\Omega$) | Very high ($\approx 2$ M$\Omega$) | | **Output Impedance** | Low ($\approx 0$) | Low ($\approx 0$) | Very low ($\approx 0$) | | **Virtual Ground?** | Yes (at inverting node) | No | No | | **Suitable for Summation?** | Yes (easy — connect more $R$ at the node) | No (complex) | No | | **Bandwidth at $\vert A \vert = 10$** | $100$ kHz | $100$ kHz | $1$ MHz (full) | | **Common Use Case** | Mixers, integrators, summers | General-purpose amplifier | Buffer, line driver, isolation | ### 3.5 Worked Numerical Examples #### Example 1: Inverting Amplifier Design > **Problem:** Design an inverting amplifier with a gain of $-5$ using $R_1 = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$. Find the output voltage for $V_{in} = 0.4$ V. **Solution:** $$R_f = \vert A_{CL} \vert \cdot R_1 = 5 \times 10 \text{ k}\Omega = 50 \text{ k}\Omega$$ $$V_{out} = -5 \times 0.4 \text{ V} = -2.0 \text{ V}$$ > [!NOTE] > Since $\pm 15$ V supplies are used, the saturation limit is $\pm 13$ V. With $V_{in} = 0.4$ V and $A = -5$, $V_{out} = -2$ V is well within the linear range. #### Example 2: Non-Inverting Amplifier Design > **Problem:** Design a non-inverting amplifier with gain of $+11$ using $R_1 = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$. Find the output voltage for $V_{in} = 0.5$ V. **Solution:** $$R_f = (A_{CL} - 1) \cdot R_1 = (11 - 1) \times 1 \text{ k}\Omega = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$$ $$V_{out} = 11 \times 0.5 \text{ V} = 5.5 \text{ V}$$ #### Example 3: Bandwidth Check Using GBP > **Problem:** For the non-inverting amplifier of Example 2 with $A_{CL} = 11$ using IC 741 ($f_T = 1$ MHz), find the maximum usable input frequency. **Solution:** $$f_{CL} = \frac{f_T}{\vert A_{CL} \vert} = \frac{1 \text{ MHz}}{11} \approx 90.9 \text{ kHz}$$ > [!WARNING] > If you feed a $100$ kHz signal into this amplifier, the op-amp's open-loop gain will be insufficient to maintain the closed-loop gain. The output amplitude will **roll off** (decrease) and the phase will distort. ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 IC 741 Internal Block Architecture The Mermaid diagram below illustrates the high-level internal functional architecture of the µA741 Op-Amp. It contains four cascaded gain stages, a class AB push-pull output stage, and biasing networks. ```mermaid flowchart LR A["V+ Pin 3"] --> S1["Input Differential
Stage"] B["V- Pin 2"] --> S1 S1 --> S2["Second Gain
Stage Darlington"] S2 --> S3["Third Gain
Stage Level Shift"] S3 --> S4["Class AB
Output Stage"] S4 --> O["Vout Pin 6"] P["V+ Pin 7
+15V"] --> BIAS["Bias Network
and Current Mirror"] N["V- Pin 4
-15V"] --> BIAS BIAS --> S1 BIAS --> S2 BIAS --> S3 BIAS --> S4 N1["Pin 1
Offset Null"] --> S1 N2["Pin 5
Offset Null"] --> S1 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** The signal enters through pins 2 and 3, is amplified through three high-gain stages, level-shifted, and then delivered via the class-AB complementary output stage to pin 6. The internal compensation capacitor (~30 pF) provides **Miller compensation**, ensuring stability and rolling off the open-loop gain at $-20$ dB/decade above ~10 Hz. ### 4.2 741 Pin Configuration (Top View of the DIP Package) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph DIP["IC 741 - 8 Pin DIP Top View"] direction TB P1["Pin 1
OFFSET NULL A"] P2["Pin 2
INVERTING INPUT"] P3["Pin 3
NON INVERTING INPUT"] P4["Pin 4
VEE NEGATIVE SUPPLY"] P5["Pin 5
OFFSET NULL B"] P6["Pin 6
OUTPUT"] P7["Pin 7
VCC POSITIVE SUPPLY"] P8["Pin 8
NC NO CONNECTION"] end P1 --- P8 P2 --- P7 P3 --- P6 P4 --- P5 ``` > [!NOTE] > Pin numbering follows the standard convention: hold the chip with the **notch on the left** and count counter-clockwise starting from the bottom-left pin (which becomes **Pin 1**). The notch is the small semicircular indentation on one end of the DIP. ### 4.3 Functional Block Diagram — Three Closed-Loop Configurations This diagram shows the *functional signal flow* for the three basic circuits: ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph INV["Inverting Amplifier"] direction LR VS1["Vin"] --> R1A["R1"] --> NA["Virtual Ground
at Inverting Node"] NA --> RFA["Rf"] --> OP1["Op-Amp 741"] OP1 --> VO1["Vout = -Rf/R1 x Vin"] NA -.feedback.-> OP1 end subgraph NON["Non-Inverting Amplifier"] direction LR VS2["Vin"] --> NB["V+ Node"] NB --> OP2["Op-Amp 741"] OP2 --> VO2["Vout = 1 + Rf/R1 x Vin"] R1B["R1"] --> NB RFB["Rf"] --> OP2 NB -.feedback.-> OP2 end subgraph FOL["Voltage Follower"] direction LR VS3["Vin"] --> NC["V+ Node"] NC --> OP3["Op-Amp 741"] OP3 --> VO3["Vout = Vin"] W["Direct Wire
Feedback"] --> NC OP3 -.feedback.-> NC end ``` ### 4.4 Inverting Amplifier — Circuit Schematic (Block-Level) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph SCHEMATIC["Inverting Amplifier Topology"] direction LR SRC["Signal Source
Vin AC Coupled"] --> R1["R1
Input Resistor"] R1 --> NA["Node A
Inverting Input Pin 2"] RF["Rf
Feedback Resistor"] --> NA NA --> OP["IC 741
Triangle Symbol"] G1["Ground"] --> P3["Pin 3
Non-Inverting"] OP --> OUT["Vout Pin 6
Inverted Output"] G2["Ground"] --> P4["Pin 4 VEE -15V"] G3["Ground"] --> P7["Pin 7 VCC +15V"] end ``` ### 4.5 Non-Inverting Amplifier — Circuit Schematic (Block-Level) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph SCHEMATIC2["Non-Inverting Amplifier Topology"] direction LR SRC2["Signal Source
Vin"] --> P3B["Pin 3
Non-Inverting Input"] P3B --> OP2B["IC 741"] R1B2["R1
Resistor to Ground"] --> P2B["Pin 2
Inverting Input"] OP2B --> RFB2["Rf
Feedback Resistor"] RFB2 --> P2B OP2B --> OUT2["Vout Pin 6
In-Phase Output"] G4["Ground"] --> R1B2 G5["Ground"] --> P4B["Pin 4 VEE -15V"] G6["Ground"] --> P7B["Pin 7 VCC +15V"] end ``` ### 4.6 Voltage Follower — Circuit Schematic (Block-Level) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph SCHEMATIC3["Voltage Follower Topology"] direction LR SRC3["Signal Source
Vin High Z"] --> P3C["Pin 3
Non-Inverting Input"] P3C --> OP3C["IC 741"] OP3C --> P6C["Pin 6 Output"] WIRE["Direct Wire
100 percent Feedback"] --> P2C["Pin 2
Inverting Input"] P6C -.short.-> WIRE P2C --> WIRE OP3C --> LOAD["Low Impedance
Load"] G7["Ground"] --> P4C["Pin 4 VEE -15V"] G8["Ground"] --> P7C["Pin 7 VCC +15V"] end ``` ### 4.7 Practical Lab Test Setup ```mermaid flowchart LR FG["Function Generator
1 kHz Sine Wave"] -->|"Vin"| PCB["Breadboard with
741 Circuit Under Test"] PSU["Dual Power Supply
+15V and -15V"] --> PCB PCB -->|"Vout"| CRO["Cathode Ray
Oscilloscope CH1"] PCB -->|"Vout"| DMM["Digital Multimeter
for DC Measurement"] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Lab Tip:** When probing the 741 on an oscilloscope, always connect the scope probe's ground clip to the **circuit common ground**, not to a floating point. The 741 is sensitive to power-supply noise; a **decoupling capacitor** of $0.1 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$ should be placed between pins 7 and 4 as close to the IC as possible to prevent high-frequency oscillations. ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** **Define the term "virtual ground" in the context of an inverting op-amp configuration. Why does it occur?** > [!NOTE] > **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > > **Virtual ground** is a node in an op-amp circuit that is at *approximately zero potential* with respect to ground, even though it is not physically connected to ground. (1 mark) > > It occurs in the **inverting amplifier** configuration because the non-inverting input is grounded ($V^+ = 0$). Due to the **virtual short principle** in negative feedback, the inverting input is forced to the same potential: $V^- = V^+ = 0$. (1 mark) > > This node is *not* a real ground because no current actually flows to the chassis ground at that point. However, it behaves as one for the purpose of circuit analysis. The high open-loop gain of the op-amp ensures the differential input is vanishingly small. (1 mark) #### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** **Why is the input impedance of a non-inverting amplifier much higher than that of an inverting amplifier?** > [!NOTE] > **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > > In the **non-inverting configuration**, the input signal is applied directly to the non-inverting terminal of the op-amp, which has a very high input impedance (typically $2 \text{ M}\Omega$ for the 741, or close to $\infty$ for an ideal op-amp). (1 mark) > > In the **inverting configuration**, the input signal is applied through an external resistor $R_1$ to the inverting node. The input impedance as seen by the source is therefore limited to $Z_{in} = R_1$ (typically $10 \text{ k}\Omega$ to $100 \text{ k}\Omega$). (1 mark) > > This makes the non-inverting topology preferable when the source impedance is high (e.g., from sensors or transducers) because it loads the source negligibly. (1 mark) --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks and typically has internal sub-division. You are provided with two alternative choices (Or option). Both questions are presented here for exhaustive practice. #### ✦ QUESTION A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Model 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** Draw the circuit diagram of an **inverting amplifier** using IC 741. Derive the expression for its **closed-loop voltage gain** and **input impedance**. Mention the phase relationship between input and output. **(7 marks)** **(b)** An inverting amplifier using IC 741 has $R_1 = 2 \text{ k}\Omega$ and $R_f = 20 \text{ k}\Omega$. A sinusoidal input of $V_{in} = 50 \text{ mV (peak)}$ at $f = 5 \text{ kHz}$ is applied. Calculate the **output voltage**, **input impedance**, and **output impedance**. State whether the op-amp operates in the **linear region** when supplies are $\pm 15 \text{ V}$. **(7 marks)** ##### Model Solution for Question A: **Part (a) — Circuit Diagram and Derivation (7 marks):** > **Step 1:** Draw the circuit. The inverting input (pin 2) is connected through $R_1$ to $V_{in}$, the non-inverting input (pin 3) is grounded, and $R_f$ provides feedback from pin 6 to pin 2. Power supplies $\pm 15$ V at pins 7 and 4. (2 marks for diagram) > **Step 2 — Apply the virtual short principle:** > The non-inverting input is at $0$ V (grounded). Therefore, by the virtual short rule: > $$V^- = V^+ = 0 \text{ V (virtual ground)}$$ > **Step 3 — Apply KCL at the inverting node:** > $$\frac{V_{in} - 0}{R_1} = \frac{0 - V_{out}}{R_f}$$ > $$V_{out} = -\frac{R_f}{R_1} V_{in}$$ > **Step 4 — Derive the input impedance:** > The current entering the inverting amplifier is $I_{in} = V_{in} / R_1$. Therefore, the input impedance is: > $$Z_{in} = \frac{V_{in}}{I_{in}} = R_1$$ > **Step 5 — Phase relationship:** > The negative sign in the gain expression indicates that the output is **$180^\circ$ out of phase** with the input. (1 mark for phase, 2 marks for derivation, 2 marks for impedance) **Part (b) — Numerical Calculation (7 marks):** > **Step 1 — Compute the gain:** > $$A_{CL} = -\frac{R_f}{R_1} = -\frac{20 \text{ k}\Omega}{2 \text{ k}\Omega} = -10$$ > **Step 2 — Compute the output voltage:** > $$V_{out} = A_{CL} \cdot V_{in} = -10 \times 50 \text{ mV} = -500 \text{ mV (peak)}$$ > **Step 3 — Input impedance:** > $$Z_{in} = R_1 = 2 \text{ k}\Omega$$ > **Step 4 — Output impedance:** > For an ideal op-amp, $Z_{out} = 0$. For IC 741, the open-loop output impedance is $75 \text{ }\Omega$, but in closed-loop with negative feedback, it is reduced by a factor of $(1 + A_{OL}\beta)$ and is therefore **approximately zero** in practice: > $$Z_{out, CL} \approx 0 \text{ }\Omega$$ > **Step 5 — Check linear operation:** > The peak output is $\vert V_{out} \vert = 0.5$ V. The saturation voltage of the 741 with $\pm 15$ V supplies is approximately $\pm 13$ V. Since $0.5 \text{ V} \ll 13 \text{ V}$, the op-amp operates well within the **linear region**. (2 marks for conclusion, 1 mark for each of the calculations: $V_{out}$, $Z_{in}$, $Z_{out}$, linear check) --- #### ✦ QUESTION B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Model 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** Draw the circuit diagram of a **non-inverting amplifier** using IC 741. Derive the expression for its **closed-loop voltage gain**. Compare it with the **voltage follower** circuit in terms of gain, input impedance, and bandwidth. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Design a **non-inverting amplifier** with a gain of $+21$ using IC 741 (with $f_T = 1 \text{ MHz}$). Choose standard resistor values. Calculate the **maximum usable input frequency** and the **output voltage** for $V_{in} = 100 \text{ mV}$ (peak). **(7 marks)** ##### Model Solution for Question B: **Part (a) — Circuit, Derivation, and Comparison (7 marks):** > **Step 1 — Circuit diagram:** $V_{in}$ at pin 3 (non-inverting), $R_1$ from pin 2 (inverting) to ground, $R_f$ from pin 6 (output) to pin 2. (2 marks for diagram) > **Step 2 — Derive the gain:** > By the virtual short principle, $V^- = V^+ = V_{in}$. > The voltage divider gives: > $$V^- = V_{out} \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_f} = V_{in}$$ > $$A_{CL} = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}$$ > **Step 3 — Voltage Follower Special Case:** When $R_f = 0$ and $R_1 = \infty$ (open), the formula yields $A_{CL} = 1$. > **Step 4 — Comparison Table:** (3 marks) | Parameter | Non-Inverting Amplifier | Voltage Follower | |:----------|:-----------------------:|:----------------:| | Gain $A_{CL}$ | $1 + R_f/R_1$ (variable, $\geq 1$) | Exactly 1 | | Input Impedance $Z_{in}$ | Very high ($\approx 2$ M$\Omega$ for 741) | Very high ($\approx 2$ M$\Omega$ for 741) | | Bandwidth ($-3$ dB) | $f_T / A_{CL}$ | $f_T = 1$ MHz (full unity-gain bandwidth) | > **Note:** The voltage follower has the **maximum possible bandwidth** because its gain is unity, so the gain-bandwidth product constraint is least restrictive. **Part (b) — Numerical Design (7 marks):** > **Step 1 — Choose $R_1$ and compute $R_f$:** > Let $R_1 = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$ (standard E12 value). > $$R_f = (A_{CL} - 1) \cdot R_1 = (21 - 1) \times 1 \text{ k}\Omega = 20 \text{ k}\Omega$$ > **Step 2 — Maximum usable input frequency:** > Using the gain-bandwidth product: > $$f_{max} = \frac{f_T}{A_{CL}} = \frac{1 \text{ MHz}}{21} \approx 47.6 \text{ kHz}$$ > **Step 3 — Output voltage:** > $$V_{out} = A_{CL} \cdot V_{in} = 21 \times 100 \text{ mV} = 2.1 \text{ V (peak)}$$ > **Step 4 — Verification of linear operation:** > $V_{out, peak} = 2.1$ V. With $\pm 15$ V supplies, the saturation limit is $\approx \pm 13$ V. Since $2.1 \text{ V} \ll 13 \text{ V}$, the op-amp operates in the **linear region**. (1 mark for design, 2 marks for $f_{max}$, 2 marks for $V_{out}$, 2 marks for verification and standard resistor selection) --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Lab/ESE Exams:** > > 1. **Forgetting the negative sign in the inverting amplifier gain.** Students often write $V_{out}/V_{in} = R_f/R_1$ instead of $-R_f/R_1$. The negative sign is **always there** in the inverting configuration. Marks are deducted for omitting it. > > 2. **Confusing the inverting and non-inverting pin numbers.** Pin 2 is the **inverting** input, Pin 3 is the **non-inverting** input. Swapping these is a circuit-fatal error. Use the mnemonic: "**2 is minus ($-$)**, **3 is plus ($+$)**" or "**2 is IN, 3 is NIN**" (Inverting and Non-INverting). > > 3. **Not mentioning the virtual short / virtual ground in derivations.** KTU examiners expect students to *state* the two golden rules before applying them. Omitting this step loses 1–2 marks. > > 4. **Ignoring power supply connections.** The 741 will not work without $\pm 15$ V at pins 7 and 4. A circuit drawn without these is incomplete and loses marks. > > 5. **Assuming rail-to-rail output.** The 741 saturates at approximately $\pm 13$ V with $\pm 15$ V supplies, *not* $\pm 15$ V. This is a common error when calculating maximum input signal amplitudes. > > 6. **Not computing the bandwidth.** KTU 2024 scheme questions often ask "up to what frequency will this circuit work?" Always compute $f_{CL} = f_T / \vert A_{CL} \vert$ when designing a closed-loop amplifier. > > 7. **Omitting offset null adjustment.** In precision applications, the $10 \text{ k}\Omega$ potentiometer between pins 1 and 5 (with wiper to $V_{EE}$) is needed to null the input offset voltage. Forgetting this in lab practicals causes DC offset at the output. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU PCICL408 Exam:** > > **General 741 Facts:** > - 8-pin DIP IC, **invented by Dave Fullagar at Fairchild Semiconductor**. > - Pin **2** = Inverting input ($V^-$); Pin **3** = Non-inverting input ($V^+$). > - Pin **6** = Output; Pin **7** = $V_{CC}^+$ ($+15$ V); Pin **4** = $V_{CC}^-$ ($-15$ V). > - Pins **1 & 5** = Offset Null (use $10 \text{ k}\Omega$ pot for nulling). > - Pin **8** = NC (no connection). > - Open-loop gain $A_{OL} \approx 2 \times 10^5$ (106 dB). > - Unity-gain bandwidth $f_T \approx 1$ MHz. > - Slew rate $SR = 0.5$ V/µs. > - Saturation $\approx \pm 13$ V (not rail-to-rail). > > **Circuit Formulas (Instant Recall):** > - **Inverting:** $A_{CL} = -R_f/R_1$; $Z_{in} = R_1$; phase $= 180^\circ$. > - **Non-Inverting:** $A_{CL} = 1 + R_f/R_1$; $Z_{in} \approx Z_{in, opamp}$; phase $= 0^\circ$. > - **Voltage Follower:** $A_{CL} = 1$; $Z_{in} \approx Z_{in, opamp}$; phase $= 0^\circ$. > > **Two Golden Rules:** > - $V^+ = V^-$ (virtual short). > - $I^+ = I^- = 0$ (virtual open / infinite input impedance). > > **Key Design Principles:** > - **Gain-Bandwidth Product** is constant: $f_{CL} \cdot \vert A_{CL} \vert = f_T = 1$ MHz. > - **Slew Rate Limit:** $f_{max} \leq \dfrac{SR}{2 \pi V_{out, peak}}$. > - **Saturation Limit:** $V_{out} \leq 13$ V (with $\pm 15$ V supplies). > > **Lab Practical Points:** > - Always use **decoupling capacitors** ($0.1 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$) between supply pins. > - Use a **dual power supply** ($\pm 15$ V) for proper operation. > - **Verify with CRO** — observe the input and output waveforms simultaneously on two channels. > - **Phase check:** If input and output are inverted, the inverting circuit is correct. If in phase, the non-inverting or follower is correct. > - **Measure gain** by computing $\vert V_{out} \vert / \vert V_{in} \vert$ at midband frequency (e.g., 1 kHz).

topics.title: Measurement of Op-Amp Parameters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICL408 - ANALOG CIRCUITS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Measurement of Op-Amp Parameters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔬 Module Experiment 2: Measurement of Op-Amp Parameters

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> *Op-Amp Parameter Measurement* refers to the experimental determination of the non-ideal characteristics of an operational amplifier (such as the **µA741**, **LM358**, or **TL081**) under DC and small-signal AC conditions. These parameters quantify the *deviation* of a real op-amp from the ideal one and are essential for predicting circuit behavior in precision analog design.

In the KTU 2024 scheme, this experiment is performed in the laboratory to characterize the device under test (DUT) and validate manufacturer datasheet values (e.g., from Texas Instruments or ON Semiconductor).

### 🎯 Conceptual Analogy — The "Imperfect Weighing Scale"

Imagine an **ideal weighing scale** that:
- Reads exactly **zero** when nothing is placed on it.
- Weighs with **infinite precision** regardless of the temperature in the room.
- Does not draw any **current from the object** being weighed (impossible in reality, but ideal).

A **real op-amp** is exactly like a slightly imperfect scale:
- It does **not read zero** when both inputs are grounded → this is the **Input Offset Voltage ($V_{IO}$)**.
- It draws a tiny **leakage current** from the source connected to its inputs → this is the **Input Bias Current ($I_B$)**.
- It **rejects common disturbances** (like the scale being tilted) only up to a finite limit → this is the **CMRR**.
- It cannot respond **infinitely fast** to a step input → this is limited by the **Slew Rate (SR)**.

### 📋 List of Standard Op-Amp Parameters Measured

The following non-ideal parameters are the standard measurement targets in the KTU Analog Circuits Lab:

| # | Parameter | Symbol | Typical Magnitude (µA741) |
|---|-----------|--------|---------------------------|
| 1 | Input Offset Voltage | $V_{IO}$ | 1 – 6 mV |
| 2 | Input Bias Current | $I_B$ | 80 – 500 nA |
| 3 | Input Offset Current | $I_{OS}$ | 20 – 200 nA |
| 4 | Open-Loop Voltage Gain | $A_{OL}$ | $2 \times 10^{5}$ (≈ 106 dB) |
| 5 | Common-Mode Rejection Ratio | CMRR | 70 – 90 dB |
| 6 | Slew Rate | SR | 0.5 V/µs |
| 7 | Power Supply Rejection Ratio | PSRR | 76 – 90 dB |
| 8 | Gain-Bandwidth Product | GBP | 1 MHz |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why are these parameters measured?**
> In the KTU 2024 NEP-aligned curriculum, this experiment is mapped to **Course Outcome CO2**: *"Design and analyze analog electronic circuits using op-amps."* The student is expected to **quantify** these non-idealities so that error budgets can be computed for instruments, filters, and ADCs.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation of Each Parameter

### ⚙️ (a) Input Offset Voltage ($V_{IO}$)

The **input offset voltage** is the small DC voltage that must be applied between the two input terminals to make the output exactly **zero** when no input signal is present. It arises from **mismatches in the input differential pair transistors** inside the op-amp.

- **Why it exists:** No two transistors are perfectly matched at the silicon level.
- **Consequence:** A DC error appears at the output, scaling by the closed-loop gain.
- **Measurement technique:** Configure the op-amp in a **high-gain inverting/non-inverting amplifier** (e.g., $A_{CL} = 1000$). The output offset is divided back to the input side.

### ⚙️ (b) Input Bias Current ($I_B$) & Input Offset Current ($I_{OS}$)

- **Input Bias Current ($I_B$):** The average of the DC currents flowing into the two input terminals.
$$I_B = \frac{I_{B+} + I_{B-}}{2}$$
- **Input Offset Current ($I_{OS}$):** The difference between the two bias currents.
$$I_{OS} = I_{B+} - I_{B-}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** The op-amp's input stage is a BJT or FET base/gate. Even at zero input, the device must be "fed" a tiny current (BJT: base current; FET: leakage current). If the two currents are not equal, the output drifts.

### ⚙️ (c) Open-Loop Voltage Gain ($A_{OL}$)

The gain of the op-amp with **no feedback** applied. It is a very large number (typically $10^5$ to $10^6$). Measured by configuring the op-amp in an open-loop configuration with a small AC input and measuring $V_{out}/V_{in}$.

### ⚙️ (d) Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR)

Defined as the ratio of **differential-mode gain** to **common-mode gain**, expressed in decibels.
$$\text{CMRR (dB)} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{A_D}{A_{CM}}\right)$$

- **Intuition:** A perfect op-amp ignores signals that appear on **both** inputs (e.g., a 50 Hz hum picked up on a long cable). A real op-amp only partially rejects this.

### ⚙️ (e) Slew Rate (SR)

The **maximum rate of change** of the output voltage with respect to time, when a large step input is applied.

$$\text{SR} = \left.\frac{dV_{out}}{dt}\right|_{\max} \;\; (\text{V/µs})$$

- **Cause:** Internal compensation capacitor and current-limiting inside the op-amp.
- **Consequence:** Limits the maximum undistorted sine-wave frequency:
$$f_{\max} = \frac{\text{SR}}{2\pi V_{p}}$$

### ⚙️ (f) Power Supply Rejection Ratio (PSRR)

The ability of the op-amp to reject **ripple or noise** on the power supply rails ($+V_{CC}$, $-V_{EE}$).
$$\text{PSRR (dB)} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{\Delta V_{supply}/\Delta V_{out}}{1}\right)$$

---

## 2.2 📘 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Table — Memorize for KTU University Exam**

| Parameter | Formula | Typical Range (µA741) | Unit |
|-----------|---------|------------------------|------|
| Input Offset Voltage | $V_{IO} = \dfrac{\vert V_{out}\vert}{A_{CL}}$ | 1 – 6 | mV |
| Input Bias Current | $I_B = \dfrac{\vert V_{out}\vert}{R_B \cdot A_{CL}}$ | 80 – 500 | nA |
| Input Offset Current | $I_{OS} = \dfrac{\vert V_{out}\vert}{R \cdot A_{CL}}$ | 20 – 200 | nA |
| Open-Loop Gain | $A_{OL} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{out}}{V_{in}}\right)$ | $10^5$ – $10^6$ | V/V or dB |
| CMRR | $\text{CMRR} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{A_D}{A_{CM}}\right)$ | 70 – 90 | dB |
| Slew Rate | $\text{SR} = \dfrac{\Delta V_{out}}{\Delta t}$ | 0.5 | V/µs |
| PSRR | $\text{PSRR} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\Delta V_{supply}}{\Delta V_{out}}\right)$ | 76 – 90 | dB |
| Gain-Bandwidth Product | $\text{GBP} = A_{CL} \times f_{-3dB}$ | 1 | MHz |
| Max. Sine Frequency | $f_{\max} = \dfrac{\text{SR}}{2\pi V_{p}}$ | depends on $V_p$ | Hz |

### 🛠️ Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Instrumentation Amplifiers (Medical ECG/EEG):** Require very high **CMRR (>100 dB)** to reject 50/60 Hz mains hum.
- **Audio Preamplifiers:** Demand low **$V_{IO}$** to avoid audible DC clicks.
- **High-Speed Data Acquisition (ADC Drivers):** Demand high **Slew Rate** to avoid distortion.
- **Sample-and-Hold Circuits:** Require low **Input Bias Current** so that the hold capacitor does not discharge.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of $V_{IO}$ from a Closed-Loop Measurement

**Setup:** The op-amp is wired in a **non-inverting amplifier** with gain $A_{CL} = 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}$. Both inputs are tied to ground through equal resistors. The output is measured with a high-impedance DMM.

Let the **internal** offset voltage source be modeled as $V_{IO}$ placed in series with the non-inverting input.

Applying superposition and KVL at the inverting node ($V^-$):

$$V^- = \frac{R_1 \cdot V_{out} + R_f \cdot 0}{R_1 + R_f} = \frac{V_{out}}{A_{CL}}$$

The op-amp output equation with the internal offset is:

$$V_{out} = A_{OL}\,(V^+ - V^- + V_{IO})$$

Substituting $V^+ = 0$ (grounded) and $V^-$ from above:

$$V_{out} = A_{OL}\!\left(0 - \frac{V_{out}}{A_{CL}} + V_{IO}\right)$$

Solving for $V_{out}$:

$$V_{out} = A_{OL}\,V_{IO} - \frac{A_{OL}}{A_{CL}}\,V_{out}$$

Rearranging:

$$V_{out}\!\left(1 + \frac{A_{OL}}{A_{CL}}\right) = A_{OL}\,V_{IO}$$

$$V_{out} = \frac{A_{OL}\,V_{IO}\,A_{CL}}{A_{CL} + A_{OL}}$$

Since $A_{OL} \gg A_{CL}$ (typical $A_{OL} = 2 \times 10^5$, $A_{CL} = 1000$):

$$V_{out} \approx V_{IO} \cdot A_{CL}$$

Therefore, the input offset voltage is **back-calculated** as:

$$V_{IO} = \frac{V_{out}}{A_{CL}}$$

**Worked Numerical Example:**

Given: $R_1 = 1\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_f = 99\,\text{k}\Omega$ → $A_{CL} = 100$.
Measured $V_{out} = 320\,\text{mV}$.

$$V_{IO} = \frac{320\,\text{mV}}{100} = 3.2\,\text{mV}$$

This is within the µA741 datasheet range.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of CMRR from a Practical Test

**Setup:** Apply a known **common-mode** sine wave $V_{cm} = 10\,V_{p-p}$ at 1 kHz to **both** inputs. Measure the resulting output $V_{out,cm}$.

The common-mode gain is:

$$A_{CM} = \frac{V_{out,cm}}{V_{cm}}$$

The differential-mode gain is the closed-loop gain of the configuration used (e.g., $A_D = A_{CL} = 100$).

CMRR in dB:

$$\text{CMRR (dB)} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{A_D}{A_{CM}}\right)$$

**Worked Numerical Example:**

Given: $A_D = 100$, $V_{cm} = 10\,V_{p-p}$, measured $V_{out,cm} = 0.316\,V_{p-p}$.

$$A_{CM} = \frac{0.316}{10} = 0.0316$$

$$\text{CMRR} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{100}{0.0316}\right) = 20\log_{10}(3162.3) = 70\,\text{dB}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Slew Rate from a Step Response

**Setup:** Apply a large square wave (e.g., 1 kHz, $\pm 10\,V_{p-p}$) to the input of a unity-gain follower. Observe the output on an oscilloscope.

The output cannot rise infinitely fast; it ramps at a finite slope. From the oscilloscope:

$$\text{SR} = \frac{\Delta V_{out}}{\Delta t} \quad (\text{V/µs})$$

**Worked Numerical Example:**

Observed: Output rises from $-10\,V$ to $+10\,V$ in $40\,\mu s$.

$$\text{SR} = \frac{10 - (-10)}{40 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{20}{40 \times 10^{-6}} = 0.5 \times 10^{6}\,\text{V/s} = 0.5\,\text{V/µs}$$

**Maximum undistorted sine-wave frequency** for a desired peak output $V_p$:

$$f_{\max} = \frac{\text{SR}}{2\pi V_p}$$

For $V_p = 5\,V$, SR = 0.5 V/µs:

$$f_{\max} = \frac{0.5 \times 10^{6}}{2\pi \times 5} \approx 15.9\,\text{kHz}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation of Input Bias Current ($I_B$)

**Setup:** A large feedback resistor $R_f$ (e.g., 1 MΩ) is used. With both inputs grounded, the output voltage $V_{out}$ is measured. The bias current flowing through $R_f$ creates a measurable offset.

$$I_B = \frac{\vert V_{out}\vert}{R_f \cdot A_{CL}}$$

**Worked Numerical Example:**

$R_f = 1\,\text{M}\Omega$, $A_{CL} = 100$, $V_{out} = 8\,mV$.

$$I_B = \frac{8 \times 10^{-3}}{1 \times 10^{6} \times 100} = 80 \times 10^{-12}\,\text{A} = 80\,\text{pA}$$

This matches the order of magnitude for FET-input op-amps (e.g., TL081).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> Since accurate schematic capture of analog circuits is best done in SPICE tools, the diagrams below represent the **Block-Level Functional Architecture** of the measurement setups, mapped step-by-step. This is the standard pedagogical approach used in KTU lab manuals.

## 4.1 Block Diagram — General Op-Amp Parameter Measurement Setup

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcA[DC Power Supply +/- 15V] --> dutA[Op-Amp Under Test]
    srcB[Function Generator Input Signal] --> dutA
    dutA --> ampA[High Gain Amplifier Stage]
    ampA --> meterA[Digital Multimeter or CRO]
    meterA --> calcA[Parameter Calculator Block]
    calcA --> resultA[Display Vio Ib IOS CMRR SR PSRR]
    dutA --> compA[Compensation Capacitor 30pF]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — $V_{IO}$ Measurement Test Bench

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    p1[Ground Reference 0V] --> r1a[Resistor R1 equals 1k]
    p1 --> r1b[Resistor Rf equals 99k]
    r1a --> nodeX[Inverting Input Node]
    r1b --> nodeX
    nodeX --> opamp1[Op-Amp DUT]
    opamp1 --> out1[Output Node Vout Measured]
    r1a --> opamp1
    p1 --> opamp1
    out1 --> dmm1[DMM Reading mV]
    dmm1 --> form1[Compute Vio equals Vout divided by ACL]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — CMRR Measurement Test Bench

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    fgA[Function Generator 1kHz Sine] --> invA[Inverting Buffer]
    fgA --> noninvA[Non-Inverting Buffer]
    invA --> nodeY[Inverting Input]
    noninvA --> nodeZ[Non-Inverting Input]
    nodeY --> opamp2[Op-Amp DUT configured as diff amp]
    nodeZ --> opamp2
    opamp2 --> out2[Output Node Vout CM]
    out2 --> croA[CRO Display]
    croA --> cmrrCalc[CMRR equals 20 log AD divided by ACM]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Slew Rate Test Bench

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    fg2[Function Generator Square Wave 1kHz +/- 10V] --> buf2[Unity Gain Buffer]
    buf2 --> opamp3[Op-Amp DUT in Voltage Follower]
    opamp3 --> out3[Output Node Ramp Signal]
    out3 --> cro2[Oscilloscope Time Domain View]
    cro2 --> srCalc[SR equals delta V divided by delta t]
    srCalc --> fmax[Compute Fmax equals SR divided by 2 pi Vp]
```

## 4.5 Complete Measurement Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Start Lab Session]) --> step1[Power On Dual Supply Set to +/- 15V]
    step1 --> step2[Insert Op-Amp IC into Breadboard]
    step2 --> step3[Wire Circuit for Parameter 1 Vio]
    step3 --> step4[Measure Vout with DMM]
    step4 --> step5[Calculate Vio and Tabulate]
    step5 --> step6[Rewire Circuit for Parameter 2 Ib]
    step6 --> step7[Measure Vout and Compute Ib]
    step7 --> step8[Repeat for IOS CMRR SR PSRR Aol]
    step8 --> step9[Compare with Datasheet]
    step9 --> step10[Plot Graphs SR vs Vp CMRR vs Freq]
    step10 --> end1([Submit Lab Report with Viva])
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**Define Input Offset Voltage of an operational amplifier. Mention its typical value for a µA741.**

**Model Answer:**
The Input Offset Voltage ($V_{IO}$) is the small differential DC voltage that must be applied between the inverting and non-inverting input terminals of an op-amp to make the output voltage exactly zero. It arises due to **mismatches** in the input differential pair transistors and load resistors inside the IC.

For a **µA741**, the typical value of $V_{IO}$ at room temperature is in the range of **1 mV to 6 mV**, with a nominal datasheet value of **2 mV**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Point to Remember:** $V_{IO}$ is multiplied by the closed-loop gain at the output. So a small 2 mV offset with $A_{CL} = 1000$ gives a 2 V DC error at the output!

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What is CMRR? Why is it expressed in decibels (dB)?**

**Model Answer:**
**CMRR (Common-Mode Rejection Ratio)** is defined as the ratio of the differential-mode gain ($A_D$) to the common-mode gain ($A_{CM}$) of an op-amp:

$$\text{CMRR} = \frac{A_D}{A_{CM}}$$

It is expressed in **decibels** because the ratio $A_D/A_{CM}$ is typically very large (e.g., $10^4$ to $10^5$). Using the decibel scale, this is expressed as:

$$\text{CMRR (dB)} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{A_D}{A_{CM}}\right)$$

This makes the values convenient to handle (e.g., **90 dB** instead of **31,623**). A higher CMRR in dB means better rejection of common-mode signals such as mains hum and ground noise.

---

## 📝 Part B — 14-Mark Module Questions (ESE Pattern with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**(a)** With the help of a circuit diagram, explain the experimental procedure to measure the **Input Offset Voltage ($V_{IO}$)** and **Input Bias Current ($I_B$)** of a given op-amp IC. (7 marks)

**(b)** In an experiment on a µA741 op-amp wired as a non-inverting amplifier with $R_1 = 1\,\text{k}\Omega$ and $R_f = 99\,\text{k}\Omega$, the measured output offset is $V_{out} = 250\,\text{mV}$. Calculate the input offset voltage. (3 marks)

**(c)** If the same op-amp is reconfigured with $R_f = 1\,\text{M}\Omega$ and the input bias current measurement yields $V_{out} = 8\,\text{mV}$, compute the input bias current. (4 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Procedure (7 marks):**

**For $V_{IO}$ Measurement:**
1. Construct a **non-inverting amplifier** using the op-amp with $R_1 = 1\,\text{k}\Omega$ and $R_f = 99\,\text{k}\Omega$ so that the closed-loop gain is:
$$A_{CL} = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1} = 1 + 99 = 100$$
2. Connect both inputs to ground (0 V) through equal resistors (typically 1 kΩ each) to provide a DC return path for the input bias currents.
3. Power the op-amp with $\pm 15\,V$ DC dual supply.
4. Measure the output voltage $V_{out}$ using a high-impedance **digital multimeter** (DMM).
5. Compute:
$$V_{IO} = \frac{\vert V_{out}\vert}{A_{CL}}$$

**For $I_B$ Measurement:**
1. Modify the circuit to use a **large feedback resistor** $R_f = 1\,\text{M}\Omega$ and a small input resistor $R_1 = 100\,\Omega$ (or short the input).
2. Ensure both inputs are still grounded through **equal bias-compensation resistors**.
3. Measure the output $V_{out}$.
4. Compute:
$$I_B = \frac{\vert V_{out}\vert}{R_f \cdot A_{CL}}$$

**(b) Calculation of $V_{IO}$ (3 marks):**
$$A_{CL} = 1 + \frac{99\,\text{k}\Omega}{1\,\text{k}\Omega} = 100$$
$$V_{IO} = \frac{250\,\text{mV}}{100} = 2.5\,\text{mV}$$

**(c) Calculation of $I_B$ (4 marks):**
$$A_{CL} = 1 + \frac{1\,\text{M}\Omega}{100\,\Omega} \approx 10000 \;\; \text{(approx.)}$$
$$I_B = \frac{8 \times 10^{-3}}{1 \times 10^{6} \times 10000} = 0.8 \times 10^{-12}\,\text{A} = 0.8\,\text{pA}$$
*(Note: If $R_1$ is omitted/short, $A_{CL} = 1$ and $I_B = 8\,\text{mV} / 1\,\text{M}\Omega = 8\,\text{nA}$, which is a typical bias current range for BJT-input op-amps like µA741.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Explain the **Slew Rate** parameter of an op-amp. Describe the experimental method to measure it using a square-wave input. (7 marks)

**(b)** The slew rate of a certain op-amp is measured to be **0.5 V/µs**. Determine the maximum frequency at which the op-amp can produce an undistorted sine wave of peak amplitude **$V_p = 10\,V$**. (4 marks)

**(c)** Define **PSRR** in one sentence and state one method to improve it in a real circuit. (3 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Slew Rate Explanation & Method (7 marks):**

**Definition:** The **Slew Rate (SR)** of an op-amp is defined as the maximum rate of change of the output voltage with respect to time, when a large-amplitude step input is applied. It is expressed in **V/µs**.

**Cause:** Slew rate limitation arises from the maximum current that can be supplied to the **internal frequency compensation capacitor** (typically 30 pF inside the op-amp).

**Measurement Procedure:**
1. Wire the op-amp as a **unity-gain voltage follower** (output tied directly to the inverting input).
2. Apply a **large-amplitude square wave** (e.g., $\pm 10\,V_{p-p}$ at 1 kHz) at the non-inverting input.
3. Observe the output on a **cathode-ray oscilloscope (CRO)**.
4. The output, instead of a perfect square wave, shows **trapezoidal edges** with finite slope.
5. Measure the slope of the rising edge:
$$\text{SR} = \frac{\Delta V_{out}}{\Delta t} = \frac{V_{max} - V_{min}}{t_{rise}}$$

**(b) Maximum Frequency Calculation (4 marks):**

The maximum undistorted sine-wave frequency is given by:
$$f_{\max} = \frac{\text{SR}}{2\pi V_p}$$

Substituting $\text{SR} = 0.5 \times 10^{6}\,\text{V/s}$ and $V_p = 10\,V$:

$$f_{\max} = \frac{0.5 \times 10^{6}}{2\pi \times 10} = \frac{0.5 \times 10^{6}}{62.83} \approx 7.96\,\text{kHz}$$

**(c) PSRR Definition & Improvement (3 marks):**
**Definition:** PSRR (Power Supply Rejection Ratio) is the ability of the op-amp to reject variations or noise present on the power supply rails.

**Improvement Method:** Use **decoupling capacitors** (typically 0.1 µF ceramic in parallel with 10 µF electrolytic) close to the power supply pins ($+V_{CC}$ and $-V_{EE}$) of the op-amp IC. This shunts high-frequency noise on the supply rails to ground.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **CMRR Formula Mix-Up:** Many students write $\text{CMRR} = 20\log_{10}(A_{CM}/A_D)$. This is **WRONG** and costs full marks. The correct order is $A_D / A_{CM}$ (differential on top).
> 2. **Slew Rate Units:** Always convert to **V/µs** for reporting. If your answer is $5 \times 10^5$ V/s, write it as **0.5 V/µs**.
> 3. **$V_{IO}$ Calculation:** Forgetting to divide $V_{out}$ by the **closed-loop gain** $A_{CL}$ is the single most common mistake. The measured output is **amplified**, not the input.
> 4. **Input Bias Current Resistor Mismatch:** If the two bias-compensation resistors at the inputs are not equal, the **offset current** (not bias current) gets measured.
> 5. **Lab Report Graph:** For slew rate, examiners expect a **graph of SR vs. peak output voltage** — not a single number. Always tabulate and plot.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Eight standard op-amp parameters** are measured: $V_{IO}$, $I_B$, $I_{OS}$, $A_{OL}$, CMRR, SR, PSRR, GBP.
- ✅ $V_{IO}$ is back-calculated as $V_{out} / A_{CL}$; always uses a **high-gain** closed-loop configuration.
- ✅ $I_B$ requires a **large feedback resistor** (1 MΩ typical) so the small bias current produces a measurable output.
- ✅ **CMRR (dB)** = $20\log_{10}(A_D / A_{CM})$; higher is better; critical for instrumentation applications.
- ✅ **Slew Rate** is measured with a **large square wave** at the input of a unity-gain follower; the output's rising edge slope = SR.
- ✅ **Maximum undistorted sine frequency** is governed by $f_{max} = \text{SR} / (2\pi V_p)$.
- ✅ **PSRR** can be improved externally with **decoupling capacitors** on the supply pins.
- ✅ For a **µA741** (BJT-input): expect $V_{IO} \approx 2\,\text{mV}$, $I_B \approx 80\,\text{nA}$, SR $\approx 0.5\,\text{V/µs}$, CMRR $\approx 90\,\text{dB}$.
- ✅ For a **TL081** (JFET-input): expect much lower $I_B$ (in pA) but similar SR and CMRR.
- ✅ Always use a **dual power supply** ($\pm 15\,V$) and place the op-amp IC on a **breadboard** with proper **bypass capacitors** for stable measurements.
- ✅ Compare measured values with the **manufacturer's datasheet** in your lab viva — this is a favourite examiner question.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Summing and Difference Amplifiers Using Op-Amp
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICL408 - ANALOG CIRCUITS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Summing and Difference Amplifiers Using Op-Amp

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Summing Amplifier** (also called an *Adder* or *Weighted Summer*) is a linear op-amp configuration that produces an output voltage which is a **weighted algebraic sum** of two or more input voltages. It is realized by feeding multiple inputs through separate input resistors to a virtual-ground node of an inverting op-amp topology.

A **Difference Amplifier** (also called a *Subtractor*) is a single-op-amp configuration that produces an output voltage proportional to the **difference between two input voltages** applied to its inverting and non-inverting terminals simultaneously. It is the fundamental building block of instrumentation and bridge-signal conditioning circuits.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Lab Definition (Verbatim Expectation):**
> The inverting summing amplifier and the difference amplifier are the two mandatory sub-experiments that constitute *Experiment 2* of the PCICL408 Analog Circuits Lab. The lab record must contain the **circuit diagram, design calculation table, expected output table, and measured output table** for both configurations.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

**Analogy for the Summing Amplifier — The "Water Mixing Tank":**
Imagine a large cylindrical tank with a fixed-capacity outlet at the bottom, while three inlet pipes pour water into the top at controlled flow rates. The *inlet pipes* are your **input resistors (R₁, R₂, R₃)** — the smaller the resistor, the more "current" (water) it allows for a given input "pressure" (voltage). The op-amp acts as the **sump pump** that drains the mixed water out the bottom (output) at a rate determined by the feedback resistor Rf. Because the mixing node is held at *virtual ground* (zero pressure), each input contributes independently to the output. The minus sign simply means the output is *inverted* — the tank is below ground level, so flow appears negative.

**Analogy for the Difference Amplifier — The "Balance Scale":**
Picture a two-pan balance. The op-amp continuously adjusts the *fulcrum support* (its output) until the **non-inverting (+) input** and the **inverting (−) input** carry identical weights (voltages). When a heavier voltage is placed on the + side (V₂), the output swings positive to "lift" the − side; when V₁ dominates the − side, the output swings negative. The op-amp reports the *imbalance* between the two pans, scaled by the resistor ratio.

### 1.3 Standardised Metrics & Conventions

- **Standard Op-Amp IC used in KTU Labs:** **μA741** (single op-amp) or **LM358** (dual op-amp).
- **Standard Supply Rails:** **+Vcc = +15 V** and **−Vee = −15 V** (dual symmetrical supply).
- **Virtual-Ground Assumption:** Valid only when the op-amp operates in the **linear region** with **negative feedback** present.
- **Ideal Op-Amp Parameters:** Open-loop gain A → ∞, Input impedance Z_in → ∞, Output impedance Z_out → 0, Input bias current I_B = 0, Offset voltage V_io = 0.

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick Mental Hook for Exams:**
> - *Summer* → "**S**um at the inverting node, **S**ign flips" → Output is **inverted weighted sum**.
> - *Subtractor* → "**S**ubtract because both terminals see the inputs" → Output is **(+) input minus (−) input scaled**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principles — Step-by-Step Logic

#### (A) Inverting Summing Amplifier — Working Logic
1. The non-inverting (+) input is tied to **ground** through a **balance resistor R_b** (typically R_b = R₁ ∥ R₂ ∥ R₃ ∥ Rf) to minimise offset due to input bias currents.
2. Multiple input voltages (V₁, V₂, V₃, …, V_n) are applied through their individual **input resistors (R₁, R₂, R₃, …, R_n)** to the inverting (−) node.
3. A single **feedback resistor Rf** connects the output back to the inverting (−) node.
4. Because of negative feedback, the inverting node sits at **virtual ground (V⁻ ≈ 0 V)**.
5. By **KCL at the inverting node**, the algebraic sum of input currents equals the feedback current:
   (V₁ / R₁) + (V₂ / R₂) + (V₃ / R₃) = −(V_out / Rf).
6. Solving for V_out yields the **weighted negative sum**.

#### (B) Difference Amplifier — Working Logic
1. V₁ is applied through **R₁** to the inverting (−) terminal.
2. V₂ is applied through **R₂** to the non-inverting (+) terminal.
3. **Rf** provides negative feedback from output to the inverting terminal.
4. **R_g** (sometimes called R₃) connects the non-inverting terminal to ground, forming a voltage divider for V₂.
5. The op-amp output drives the circuit such that V⁺ = V⁻ (virtual short).
6. Superposition is applied: the final V_out is the contribution from V₁ (acting through the inverting path) **plus** the contribution from V₂ (acting through the non-inverting path with the divider).

#### (C) Non-Inverting Summing Amplifier — Working Logic
1. All input voltages (V₁, V₂, …) feed directly into the **non-inverting (+) terminal** through individual resistors.
2. A single resistor **R_g** ties the non-inverting terminal to ground.
3. The standard non-inverting feedback network (Rf and R₁) sets the **non-inverting gain (1 + Rf/R₁)**.
4. The non-inverting node is **NOT at virtual ground** — the algebra is more involved and is derived in Section 3.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Configuration | Output Voltage Equation (General) | Output Equation (Matched / Balanced Case) | Engineering Use-Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Inverting Summer (n inputs) | $V_{out} = -R_f \left( \dfrac{V_1}{R_1} + \dfrac{V_2}{R_2} + \cdots + \dfrac{V_n}{R_n} \right)$ | When R₁ = R₂ = … = R_n = R : $V_{out} = -\dfrac{R_f}{R} (V_1 + V_2 + \cdots + V_n)$ | Audio mixers, DAC output summing, multi-channel signal combination. |
| 2 | Non-Inverting Summer (2 inputs, equal gains) | $V_{out} = \left(1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}\right) \cdot \dfrac{V_1 R_2 + V_2 R_1}{R_1 + R_2 + R_g}$ | When R₁ = R₂ = R_g = R : $V_{out} = V_1 + V_2$ | Non-inverting adders where polarity preservation is critical. |
| 3 | Difference Amplifier (Subtractor) | $V_{out} = \left(\dfrac{R_3}{R_2 + R_3}\right) \left(1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}\right) V_2 - \left(\dfrac{R_f}{R_1}\right) V_1$ | When R₁ = R₂ and R_f = R₃ : $V_{out} = \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}(V_2 - V_1)$ | Bridge amplifiers, ECG/EEG front-ends, instrumentation, error amplifiers in SMPS. |
| 4 | Balance Resistor (Inverting Summer) | $R_b = R_1 \parallel R_2 \parallel R_3 \parallel \cdots \parallel R_n \parallel R_f$ | Used to cancel input bias current induced offset. | Mandatory in precision DC summing applications. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Tip — Saturation Check:**
> Always verify that the calculated $V_{out}$ lies within the **op-amp saturation limits**, i.e., $\vert V_{out} \vert \le V_{sat} \approx \pm 13.5 \text{ V}$ (for ±15 V supplies with a typical 741 op-amp). Marks are deducted in KTU valuation if this check is missing.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Biomedical Instrumentation:** ECG signal channels sum limb-electrode potentials (Wilson Lead Network) using precision summers before differential amplification.
- **Audio Mixing Consoles:** Inverting summers combine multiple microphone/line inputs with individual gain (fader) control — the input resistor acts as the fader.
- **Digital-to-Analogue Converters (R-2R Ladder & Summing DAC):** Weighted-input summers form the output stage of binary-weighted DACs where each bit controls a current source weighted by 2ⁿ.
- **Active Filters:** Multiple summing nodes build multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) filter banks.
- **Process Control & SMPS:** The error amplifier in switched-mode power supplies is, in essence, a difference amplifier comparing the feedback voltage with a reference.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Inverting Summing Amplifier (3-Input Case)

Consider a 3-input inverting summer with inputs V₁, V₂, V₃ feeding through R₁, R₂, R₃ respectively into the inverting node (−). The non-inverting input (+) is grounded. Feedback resistor is Rf.

**Step 1:** Apply the **virtual-ground concept**.
Since the op-amp is in linear region with negative feedback, the differential input is zero:
$$V^{+} = V^{-} = 0 \text{ V (Virtual Ground)}$$

**Step 2:** Apply **Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL)** at the inverting node (V⁻).
Sum of currents entering the node = Sum of currents leaving the node.

$$I_1 + I_2 + I_3 = -I_f$$

**Step 3:** Express each current using Ohm's law (V⁻ = 0).
$$I_1 = \dfrac{V_1 - V^{-}}{R_1} = \dfrac{V_1}{R_1}$$
$$I_2 = \dfrac{V_2 - V^{-}}{R_2} = \dfrac{V_2}{R_2}$$
$$I_3 = \dfrac{V_3 - V^{-}}{R_3} = \dfrac{V_3}{R_3}$$
$$I_f = \dfrac{V_{out} - V^{-}}{R_f} = \dfrac{V_{out}}{R_f}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute into the KCL equation.
$$\dfrac{V_1}{R_1} + \dfrac{V_2}{R_2} + \dfrac{V_3}{R_3} = -\dfrac{V_{out}}{R_f}$$

**Step 5:** Solve algebraically for V_out.
$$-\dfrac{V_{out}}{R_f} = \dfrac{V_1}{R_1} + \dfrac{V_2}{R_2} + \dfrac{V_3}{R_3}$$

Multiplying both sides by (−Rf):
$$V_{out} = -R_f \left( \dfrac{V_1}{R_1} + \dfrac{V_2}{R_2} + \dfrac{V_3}{R_3} \right)$$

**Step 6 (Generalisation):** Extending to n inputs:
$$V_{out} = -R_f \sum_{k=1}^{n} \dfrac{V_k}{R_k}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation — Difference Amplifier (Standard Subtractor)

The classic difference amplifier uses four matched resistors: R₁, R₂ (input path resistors) and Rf, R₃ (feedback and ground-reference path). The balanced condition is **R₁ = R₂** and **Rf = R₃**.

**Step 1:** Identify the voltages at the op-amp input terminals.

The non-inverting input (+) sees a **voltage divider** formed by R₂ and R₃, with V₂ applied at the top of R₂ and ground at the bottom of R₃. Since the op-amp draws no input current, the divider is unloaded:
$$V^{+} = V_2 \cdot \dfrac{R_3}{R_2 + R_3}$$

**Step 2:** Apply virtual short (V⁻ = V⁺).
$$V^{-} = V^{+} = V_2 \cdot \dfrac{R_3}{R_2 + R_3}$$

**Step 3:** Apply KCL at the inverting node (V⁻).
Current from V₁ through R₁ = Current leaving through Rf to V_out:
$$\dfrac{V_1 - V^{-}}{R_1} = \dfrac{V^{-} - V_{out}}{R_f}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute V⁻ from Step 2 into the KCL equation.
$$\dfrac{V_1 - V_2 \cdot \dfrac{R_3}{R_2 + R_3}}{R_1} = \dfrac{V_2 \cdot \dfrac{R_3}{R_2 + R_3} - V_{out}}{R_f}$$

**Step 5:** Cross-multiply by R_f to clear the right-side denominator.
$$R_f \left[ \dfrac{V_1 - V_2 \cdot \dfrac{R_3}{R_2 + R_3}}{R_1} \right] = V_2 \cdot \dfrac{R_3}{R_2 + R_3} - V_{out}$$

**Step 6:** Isolate V_out.
$$V_{out} = V_2 \cdot \dfrac{R_3}{R_2 + R_3} - \dfrac{R_f}{R_1} \left[ V_1 - V_2 \cdot \dfrac{R_3}{R_2 + R_3} \right]$$

**Step 7:** Distribute and collect V₂ terms.
$$V_{out} = V_2 \cdot \dfrac{R_3}{R_2 + R_3} - \dfrac{R_f}{R_1} V_1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1} \cdot V_2 \cdot \dfrac{R_3}{R_2 + R_3}$$

$$V_{out} = \left( 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1} \right) \left( \dfrac{R_3}{R_2 + R_3} \right) V_2 - \dfrac{R_f}{R_1} V_1$$

This is the **general difference amplifier equation**.

**Step 8:** Apply the **matched-resistor condition** R₁ = R₂ and Rf = R₃.
Under this condition, the voltage divider factor becomes R₃ / (R₂ + R₃) = Rf / (R₁ + Rf).

Substituting R₂ = R₁ and R₃ = Rf:
$$V_{out} = \left(1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}\right) \cdot \dfrac{R_f}{R_1 + R_f} \cdot V_2 - \dfrac{R_f}{R_1} V_1$$

Note that $\left(1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}\right) = \dfrac{R_1 + R_f}{R_1}$, so the prefactor simplifies:
$$\left( \dfrac{R_1 + R_f}{R_1} \right) \cdot \left( \dfrac{R_f}{R_1 + R_f} \right) = \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}$$

**Step 9:** Final matched-resistor difference amplifier equation.
$$V_{out} = \dfrac{R_f}{R_1} (V_2 - V_1)$$

> [!NOTE]
> The elegance of the matched-resistor condition is that the gain is set by **a single ratio Rf / R₁**, and the formula is symmetric — increasing Rf increases the gain equally for both inputs, and Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR) is maximised when resistors are precisely matched (typically 0.1% tolerance metal-film resistors are used in instrumentation designs).

---

### 3.3 Derivation — Non-Inverting Summing Amplifier (2-Input Case)

This is the trickier configuration. Two inputs V₁ and V₂ feed the non-inverting (+) node through R₁ and R₂ respectively. A resistor R_g connects the non-inverting node to ground. The inverting node has the standard feedback pair Rf and R₁ (using a different R₁ for the feedback network — we will call it R_a to avoid conflict).

**Step 1:** Apply KCL at the non-inverting node V⁺.
The op-amp input draws no current, so the currents from V₁, V₂, and the ground resistor R_g must sum to zero at node V⁺:
$$\dfrac{V_1 - V^{+}}{R_1} + \dfrac{V_2 - V^{+}}{R_2} + \dfrac{0 - V^{+}}{R_g} = 0$$

**Step 2:** Collect the V⁺ terms.
$$\dfrac{V_1}{R_1} + \dfrac{V_2}{R_2} = V^{+} \left( \dfrac{1}{R_1} + \dfrac{1}{R_2} + \dfrac{1}{R_g} \right)$$

**Step 3:** Solve for V⁺.
$$V^{+} = \dfrac{\dfrac{V_1}{R_1} + \dfrac{V_2}{R_2}}{\dfrac{1}{R_1} + \dfrac{1}{R_2} + \dfrac{1}{R_g}}$$

**Step 4:** Apply virtual short: V⁻ = V⁺.
The inverting node voltage equals the non-inverting node voltage.

**Step 5:** Apply KCL at the inverting node V⁻.
The inverting node is connected to ground through R_a and to V_out through Rf:
$$\dfrac{0 - V^{-}}{R_a} + \dfrac{V_{out} - V^{-}}{R_f} = 0$$

**Step 6:** Solve for V_out in terms of V⁻.
$$V_{out} = V^{-} \left( 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_a} \right)$$

**Step 7:** Substitute V⁻ = V⁺ from Step 3.
$$V_{out} = \left( 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_a} \right) \cdot \dfrac{\dfrac{V_1}{R_1} + \dfrac{V_2}{R_2}}{\dfrac{1}{R_1} + \dfrac{1}{R_2} + \dfrac{1}{R_g}}$$

**Step 8 (Simplified balanced case):** When R₁ = R₂ = R_g = R, the denominator becomes 3/R and the numerator becomes (V₁ + V₂)/R:
$$V_{out} = \left( 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_a} \right) \cdot \dfrac{V_1 + V_2}{3}$$

To make the gain equal to 1, choose Rf = 2 R_a (e.g., R_a = 10 kΩ, Rf = 20 kΩ):
$$V_{out} = (1 + 2) \cdot \dfrac{V_1 + V_2}{3} = V_1 + V_2$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why the Non-Inverting Summer is Less Popular:** The output depends on the **resistor matching accuracy** of three components (R₁, R₂, R_g) plus the gain-setting pair (Rf, R_a). Any mismatch introduces a gain error and a Common-Mode voltage component. The inverting summer is preferred in practice because the virtual ground makes each input mathematically *independent* of the others.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Design Quick-Reference

| Desired Transfer Function | Configuration | Recommended Resistor Choice |
|---|---|---|
| $V_{out} = -(V_1 + V_2 + V_3)$ | 3-Input Inverting Summer | $R_1 = R_2 = R_3 = R_f = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$ |
| $V_{out} = -(2V_1 + 3V_2 + V_3)$ | 3-Input Weighted Inverting Summer | $R_f = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$; $R_1 = 5 \text{ k}\Omega$; $R_2 = 3.3 \text{ k}\Omega$; $R_3 = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$ |
| $V_{out} = 5(V_2 - V_1)$ | Difference Amplifier | $R_1 = R_2 = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$; $R_f = R_3 = 50 \text{ k}\Omega$ |
| $V_{out} = V_1 + V_2$ | Non-Inverting Summer | $R_1 = R_2 = R_g = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$; $R_a = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$; $R_f = 20 \text{ k}\Omega$ |
| $V_{out} = 10(V_2 - V_1)$ | Difference Amplifier (high gain) | $R_1 = R_2 = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$; $R_f = R_3 = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagrams represent the **block-level signal flow architecture** for each op-amp configuration. Physical circuit diagrams with op-amp pinouts, supply rails, and ground symbols are drawn in your lab record book; here, the focus is on the **functional data-path** for conceptual clarity.

### 4.1 Inverting Summing Amplifier — Signal Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    A1[V1 Input] -->|via R1| SUM1((Summing Node V-))
    A2[V2 Input] -->|via R2| SUM1
    A3[V3 Input] -->|via R3| SUM1
    SUM1 -->|V+ = V-| OPA1[Op-Amp uA741]
    OPA1 -->|Feedback via Rf| SUM1
    OPA1 --> OUT1[Vout = -Rf x V1/R1 + V2/R2 + V3/R3]
    GND1[Ground via Rb] --> SUM1
    GND2[Ground] --> OPA1
```

**Interpretation:**
- Three input voltages enter through **three input resistors** into the inverting node.
- The feedback path closes the negative feedback loop.
- The balance resistor **R_b** connects the non-inverting input to ground to nullify bias-current effects.

---

### 4.2 Difference Amplifier — Signal Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    B1[V1 Input] -->|via R1| INVNODE((Inverting Node V-))
    INVNODE -->|Feedback via Rf| OUT2[Vout]
    OUT2 --> OPA2[Op-Amp]
    B2[V2 Input] -->|via R2| NONINODE((Non-Inverting Node V+))
    NONINODE -->|Divider to ground via R3| GND3[Ground]
    OPA2 --> INVNODE
    OPA2 --> OUT2
```

**Interpretation:**
- V₁ enters the **inverting path**; V₂ enters the **non-inverting path**.
- The op-amp output is the **scaled difference** when resistor ratios match: $V_{out} = (R_f / R_1) (V_2 - V_1)$.

---

### 4.3 Non-Inverting Summing Amplifier — Signal Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    C1[V1 Input] -->|via R1| NNODE((Non-Inverting Node V+))
    C2[V2 Input] -->|via R2| NNODE
    NNODE -->|Reference to ground via Rg| GND4[Ground]
    NNODE --> OPA3[Op-Amp]
    OPA3 -->|Feedback via Rf| INNODE2((Inverting Node V-))
    INNODE2 -->|Ground reference via Ra| GND5[Ground]
    OPA3 --> OUT3[Vout]
```

**Interpretation:**
- Both V₁ and V₂ enter the **non-inverting (+) terminal** simultaneously.
- The op-amp output is the **non-inverted sum** when resistors are properly scaled.

---

### 4.4 Functional Architecture — Experiment 2 Lab Setup

```mermaid
graph TB
    DCPS[DC Power Supply plus minus 15V] --> PWR[Power Rails to Op-Amp IC 741]
    FGEN1[Function Generator Channel 1] -->|V1| INA[Input Resistor Network]
    FGEN2[Function Generator Channel 2] -->|V2| INA
    FGEN3[Function Generator Channel 3] -->|V3| INA
    INA --> SUMMER[Summing Amplifier Circuit on Breadboard]
    SUMMER -->|Vout summing| SCOPE1[Channel 1 of CRO or DSO]
    DCPS --> PWR
    DMM[Digital Multimeter] -.->|DC measurement| SUMMER
    DMM2[Digital Multimeter] -.->|DC measurement| DIFF[Difference Amplifier Circuit on Breadboard]
    FGEN1 -->|V1| INB[Input Resistor R1]
    FGEN2 -->|V2| INB2[Input Resistor R2]
    INB --> DIFF
    INB2 --> DIFF
    DIFF -->|Vout difference| SCOPE2[Channel 2 of CRO or DSO]
```

**Interpretation:**
- This block diagram shows the **complete lab bench setup** for Experiment 2.
- A dual-polarity power supply energises the 741 IC.
- Function generators provide DC inputs (V₁, V₂, V₃) for the summer.
- The difference amplifier uses two of the same sources for V₁ and V₂.
- Outputs are observed on a **CRO/DSO** and verified quantitatively using a **DMM**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*With a neat circuit diagram, derive the output voltage expression of a 3-input inverting summing amplifier using an ideal op-amp. State the function of the balance resistor.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Circuit:** A 3-input inverting summer has V₁, V₂, V₃ applied through R₁, R₂, R₃ to the inverting node; Rf is the feedback resistor; the non-inverting input is grounded through the balance resistor R_b = R₁ ∥ R₂ ∥ R₃ ∥ Rf.

**Derivation (2 Marks):**
At the virtual-ground node (V⁻ = 0), KCL gives:
$$I_1 + I_2 + I_3 + I_f = 0$$
$$\dfrac{V_1}{R_1} + \dfrac{V_2}{R_2} + \dfrac{V_3}{R_3} = -\dfrac{V_{out}}{R_f}$$
$$\boxed{V_{out} = -R_f \left( \dfrac{V_1}{R_1} + \dfrac{V_2}{R_2} + \dfrac{V_3}{R_3} \right)}$$

**Function of R_b (1 Mark):**
The balance resistor R_b connected between the non-inverting input and ground is chosen to be the **parallel combination of all input and feedback resistors**. It minimises the **output offset voltage** caused by the op-amp's input bias currents, ensuring that the DC operating point remains stable.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is a difference amplifier? State the condition for which the CMRR of a single-op-amp difference amplifier becomes infinite (ideal).*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
A difference amplifier is an op-amp configuration that amplifies the **difference between two input voltages** while ideally rejecting any signal common to both inputs. It is realised using a single op-amp with two input resistors (R₁, R₂) and a feedback network (Rf, R₃).

**Output Equation (1 Mark):**
$$V_{out} = \left( 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1} \right) \left( \dfrac{R_3}{R_2 + R_3} \right) V_2 - \dfrac{R_f}{R_1} V_1$$

**Condition for Ideal (Infinite) CMRR (1 Mark):**
CMRR becomes infinite when the **resistor ratios are precisely matched**, i.e.,
$$\dfrac{R_f}{R_1} = \dfrac{R_3}{R_2}$$
Under this condition, the common-mode gain A_c = 0 and the differential-mode gain A_d = Rf / R₁, making the CMRR → ∞. Practically, this requires using **0.1% tolerance metal-film resistors** with identical temperature coefficients.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice Pattern)

---

#### **QUESTION A — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Design an inverting summing amplifier using a 741 op-amp to realise the transfer function $V_{out} = -(2V_1 + 3V_2 + 4V_3)$. Assume $R_f = 30 \text{ k}\Omega$. Draw the circuit diagram and calculate the balance resistor. Also, compute V_out when V₁ = 1 V, V₂ = 2 V, V₃ = 0.5 V. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a circuit diagram, explain the operation of a difference amplifier. Derive the expression for the output voltage and state the condition for which it acts as an ideal subtractor. Mention one practical application. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 — Identify the target equation (1 Mark):**
The general inverting summer output is:
$$V_{out} = -R_f \left( \dfrac{V_1}{R_1} + \dfrac{V_2}{R_2} + \dfrac{V_3}{R_3} \right)$$

Matching with the desired equation $V_{out} = -(2V_1 + 3V_2 + 4V_3)$ and using $R_f = 30 \text{ k}\Omega$:

**Step 2 — Solve for each input resistor (2 Marks):**
$$R_1 = \dfrac{R_f}{2} = \dfrac{30 \text{ k}\Omega}{2} = 15 \text{ k}\Omega$$
$$R_2 = \dfrac{R_f}{3} = \dfrac{30 \text{ k}\Omega}{3} = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$$
$$R_3 = \dfrac{R_f}{4} = \dfrac{30 \text{ k}\Omega}{4} = 7.5 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the balance resistor R_b (1 Mark):**
$$R_b = R_1 \parallel R_2 \parallel R_3 \parallel R_f = 15\text{k} \parallel 10\text{k} \parallel 7.5\text{k} \parallel 30\text{k}$$
$$\dfrac{1}{R_b} = \dfrac{1}{15} + \dfrac{1}{10} + \dfrac{1}{7.5} + \dfrac{1}{30} = 0.0667 + 0.1 + 0.1333 + 0.0333 = 0.3333 \text{ (in } \mu S\text{)}$$
$$R_b = 3 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 4 — Calculate V_out for the given inputs (2 Marks):**
$$V_{out} = -30\text{k} \left( \dfrac{1}{15\text{k}} + \dfrac{2}{10\text{k}} + \dfrac{0.5}{7.5\text{k}} \right)$$
$$= -30\text{k} \left( 66.67 \mu\text{S} + 200 \mu\text{S} + 66.67 \mu\text{S} \right)$$
$$= -30\text{k} \times 333.33 \mu\text{S}$$
$$\boxed{V_{out} = -10 \text{ V}}$$

**Step 5 — Circuit diagram (1 Mark):**
