[Student should draw: 741 op-amp with pin 2 (inverting) connected to V₁, V₂, V₃ through R₁, R₂, R₃; pin 3 (non-inverting) grounded through R_b = 3 kΩ; pin 6 (output) connected back to pin 2 through Rf = 30 kΩ; pins 7 and 4 connected to +15 V and −15 V respectively; pin 1 and 5 (offset null) connected to a 10 kΩ potentiometer.]

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 — Circuit Diagram (2 Marks):**
[Student should draw the standard difference amplifier: V₁ through R₁ to pin 2; Rf from pin 2 to pin 6; V₂ through R₂ to pin 3; R₃ from pin 3 to ground; ±15 V supplies on pins 7 and 4.]

**Step 2 — Derivation (3 Marks):**
Voltage at non-inverting input (voltage divider, no current into op-amp):
$$V^{+} = V_2 \cdot \dfrac{R_3}{R_2 + R_3}$$

KCL at inverting node (V⁻ = V⁺ by virtual short):
$$\dfrac{V_1 - V^{-}}{R_1} = \dfrac{V^{-} - V_{out}}{R_f}$$

Substituting and solving (as detailed in Section 3.2):
$$V_{out} = \left( 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1} \right) \left( \dfrac{R_3}{R_2 + R_3} \right) V_2 - \dfrac{R_f}{R_1} V_1$$

**Step 3 — Condition for Ideal Subtractor (1 Mark):**
When R₁ = R₂ and Rf = R₃, the output simplifies to:
$$V_{out} = \dfrac{R_f}{R_1} (V_2 - V_1)$$
This is the **ideal subtractor equation** — pure difference with single-gain control.

**Step 4 — Practical Application (1 Mark):**
Used as the **front-end error amplifier in Switched-Mode Power Supplies (SMPS)**, where it compares the feedback voltage (from the output divider) with a precision reference voltage (V_ref, e.g., TL431).

---

#### **QUESTION B — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Alternate Set]**

**(a)** Design a difference amplifier with a gain of 10 to subtract V₁ from V₂. Choose appropriate resistor values, draw the circuit, and calculate the output voltage for V₁ = 1.5 V and V₂ = 2.5 V. Verify whether the op-amp remains in the linear region with ±12 V supplies. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a circuit diagram, explain the working of a non-inverting summing amplifier. Derive its output expression for a 2-input case and state the condition under which it becomes a pure unity-gain adder. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 — Choose resistor values for gain = 10 (2 Marks):**
For a difference amplifier with matched resistors, the gain is Rf / R₁. To obtain a gain of 10:
Choose R₁ = R₂ = 10 kΩ, and Rf = R₃ = 100 kΩ.

**Step 2 — Draw the circuit (1 Mark):**
[Student should draw: V₁ through 10 kΩ to pin 2; 100 kΩ feedback from pin 6 to pin 2; V₂ through 10 kΩ to pin 3; 100 kΩ from pin 3 to ground; pin 6 is the output.]

**Step 3 — Calculate V_out (2 Marks):**
$$V_{out} = \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}(V_2 - V_1) = 10 \times (2.5 - 1.5) = 10 \times 1.0 = 10 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4 — Linearity Check (2 Marks):**
The op-amp saturation voltage for a 741 with ±12 V supply is typically:
$$V_{sat} \approx \pm (V_{cc} - 2) = \pm 10 \text{ V}$$
Since the calculated V_out = +10 V **exactly equals** the positive saturation limit, the op-amp is on the **edge of saturation**. In practice, it will clip slightly below +10 V.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Mark-Loss Alert (KTU 2024 Valuation Pattern):**
> Students frequently lose **2 to 3 marks** on this question because they forget to verify the **linear-region condition** ($V_{out}$ within supply rails minus ~2 V headroom for a 741). The valuation key explicitly penalises missing this check. A safer design would use ±15 V supplies to guarantee V_out = 10 V remains comfortably in the linear range.

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 — Circuit Diagram (2 Marks):**
[Student should draw: V₁ through R₁ and V₂ through R₂ entering pin 3 (non-inverting); R_g from pin 3 to ground; pin 2 connected to ground through R_a and to output through Rf; ±15 V supplies.]

**Step 2 — Working Principle (1 Mark):**
Both inputs enter the **non-inverting (+) terminal**. The op-amp output is the **non-inverted weighted sum** of V₁ and V₂ — the output polarity matches the input polarity (no sign inversion).

**Step 3 — Derivation (3 Marks):**
KCL at the non-inverting node (no current into op-amp):
$$\dfrac{V_1 - V^{+}}{R_1} + \dfrac{V_2 - V^{+}}{R_2} = \dfrac{V^{+}}{R_g}$$

Solving for V⁺:
$$V^{+} = \dfrac{\dfrac{V_1}{R_1} + \dfrac{V_2}{R_2}}{\dfrac{1}{R_1} + \dfrac{1}{R_2} + \dfrac{1}{R_g}}$$

KCL at the inverting node (V⁻ = V⁺):
$$V_{out} = V^{-} \left(1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_a}\right) = V^{+} \left(1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_a}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Condition for Unity-Gain Pure Adder (1 Mark):**
When R₁ = R₂ = R_g = R, the numerator becomes (V₁ + V₂)/R and the denominator is 3/R:
$$V_{out} = \left(1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_a}\right) \cdot \dfrac{V_1 + V_2}{3}$$
Setting $1 + R_f/R_a = 3$ (i.e., $R_f = 2 R_a$), we get the **pure unity-gain adder**:
$$V_{out} = V_1 + V_2$$

---

### 5.3 Examiner's Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the balance resistor R_b** in the inverting summer — 1 mark lost per question.
> 2. **Missing the saturation / linearity check** in difference amplifier design problems — 2 marks lost.
> 3. **Sign convention errors** — confusing whether the output is V₂ − V₁ or V₁ − V₂. Always re-derive using the formula: V_out = (Rf/R₁)(V₂ − V₁) **only when matched**.
> 4. **Confusing the inverting and non-inverting summer output equations** — the non-inverting summer has a denominator term involving R_g, unlike the inverting summer's clean form.
> 5. **Writing the op-amp pin numbers incorrectly** in the circuit diagram — 741 pins 2 (inverting), 3 (non-inverting), 4 (V−), 6 (output), 7 (V+), 1 & 5 (offset null), 8 (NC). Marks are deducted for pin-number errors in lab records.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Inverting Summer Formula:** $V_{out} = -R_f \sum V_k / R_k$ — always produces a **negative** weighted sum.
- **Difference Amplifier Formula (matched):** $V_{out} = (R_f / R_1)(V_2 - V_1)$ — the **single most-tested formula** in KTU lab exams for this experiment.
- **Virtual Ground Assumption:** Valid **only** when the op-amp operates in the linear region with negative feedback.
- **Balance Resistor R_b:** R_b = R₁ ∥ R₂ ∥ R₃ ∥ Rf — minimises bias-current-induced output offset.
- **CMRR Maximisation:** Achieved when R₁ = R₂ and Rf = R₃; precision resistors (0.1% tolerance) are essential.
- **Saturation Check:** $\vert V_{out} \vert \le V_{sat} \approx \pm (V_{cc} - 2)$ — for ±15 V supplies, $V_{sat} \approx \pm 13.5$ V.
- **Standard Lab IC:** μA741 or LM358; supplies ±15 V; offset-null potentiometer 10 kΩ between pins 1 and 5.
- **Pin Assignment Reminder (741):** Pin 2 = inverting input, Pin 3 = non-inverting input, Pin 6 = output, Pin 4 = V−, Pin 7 = V+, Pin 8 = NC.
- **Practical Applications (Memorise 2-3):** Audio mixers, DACs, ECG front-ends, SMPS error amplifiers, bridge-signal conditioning.
- **Lab Viva Quick-Fire Questions:**
  - *Q: What happens if R_b is removed?* → Output offset increases due to input bias currents.
  - *Q: Can the difference amplifier amplify DC signals?* → Yes, since it is a direct-coupled configuration.
  - *Q: Why use 0.1% resistors in a difference amplifier?* → To maximise CMRR by minimising resistor mismatch.
  - *Q: What is the input impedance of an inverting summer?* → Equal to R₁ (the smallest input resistor dominates).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of Instrumentation Amplifier
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICL408 - ANALOG CIRCUITS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Design of Instrumentation Amplifier

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

An **Instrumentation Amplifier (IA / In-Amp)** is a precision, closed-loop, differential-input gain block whose output is a single-ended signal proportional to the **difference** between two input voltages, while actively rejecting any voltage common to both inputs. It is typically realized using the classic **three op-amp topology** (two input-buffer op-amps feeding a unity-gain difference amplifier) and is characterized by an extremely high **Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR)**, very high input impedance, low DC offset, low drift, and a gain that is settable by a single external resistor.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition to Memorize:**
> *"An Instrumentation Amplifier is a high-performance, differential-input, single-ended-output analog amplifier that combines high input impedance, high common-mode rejection ratio (CMRR), and a precisely settable gain via a single resistor — making it the industry-standard front-end for sensor signal conditioning (strain gauges, thermocouples, ECG/biomedical electrodes, bridge networks)."*

## 1.2 The "Why" — Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are trying to **listen to a single conversation in a crowded, noisy market**:
- The two voices talking to each other (the *signal* you want) is tiny — perhaps only a few microvolts.
- The ambient market noise (the *common-mode* noise) is huge — tens or hundreds of millivolts.
- A normal amplifier would simply make everything louder, drowning out the conversation in noise.
- An Instrumentation Amplifier acts like **highly directional noise-cancelling headphones**:
  1. It places two high-impedance "ears" right at the conversation — picking up the tiny signal without loading the source.
  2. It amplifies *only* the *difference* between the two voices.
  3. It throws away anything that both ears hear equally (the market noise).
  4. It converts the balanced differential signal to a single-ended output that a microcontroller/ADC can read.

This is exactly what is needed when reading:
- An **ECG electrode pair** on the chest (microvolt cardiac signal sitting on top of hundreds of millivolts of 50 Hz mains pickup).
- A **strain-gauge Wheatstone bridge** (millivolt differential signal on a common-mode voltage of VCC/2).
- A **thermocouple** in an industrial furnace (microvolt differential signal on top of a large common-mode voltage).

## 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (Memorize These Bold Terms)

- **Differential Gain ($A_d$)** — Gain applied to the difference of the two inputs.
- **Common-Mode Gain ($A_{cm}$)** — Gain applied to the average of the two inputs (ideally zero).
- **Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR)** = $20 \log_{10}(A_d / A_{cm})$ — measured in **dB**.
- **Input Impedance ($Z_{in}$)** — ideally **infinite** (typically > $10^9\ \Omega$ for FET/CMOS op-amps).
- **Power-Supply Rejection Ratio (PSRR)** — ability to ignore ripples on the supply rails.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why we don't use a simple 1-op-amp difference amplifier:**
> A single-op-amp difference amplifier has the fatal flaw of *low and mismatched input impedances*. The two inputs see different source impedances, which destroys CMRR in real circuits. The 3-op-amp IA solves this by using **two non-inverting buffers** at the front-end — giving both inputs an identical, near-infinite impedance.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ⚙️ SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Op-Amp Topology — Block Logic

The classical **3-op-amp Instrumentation Amplifier** is built in two cascaded stages:

### Stage 1 — The Input Buffer Stage (Op-Amps A1 and A2)
- Two op-amps ($A_1$ and $A_2$) are configured in the **non-inverting** topology.
- The two source signals $V_1$ and $V_2$ feed the **positive (non-inverting)** inputs.
- The negative inputs of $A_1$ and $A_2$ are tied together through a single **gain-setting resistor** $R_G$ (often called $R_{gain}$).
- Each op-amp has a feedback resistor $R_1$ between its output and its negative input.
- Because both positive inputs are high-impedance, the stage *does not load* the source.
- **This stage provides all the voltage gain AND the high input impedance.**

### Stage 2 — The Difference Amplifier (Op-Amp A3)
- A standard **single op-amp difference amplifier** built with four matched resistors $R_2, R_2, R_3, R_3$.
- It takes the outputs of Stage 1 and converts the **differential signal** to a **single-ended output** with respect to ground.
- It also performs **common-mode rejection** at high precision (because matched resistors are easy to trim).
- **Gain in this stage is usually fixed at 1** (when $R_2 = R_3$).

## 2.2 Operational Logic — Why Each Step Matters

| Step | What Happens | Why It Matters |
|------|--------------|----------------|
| 1 | Both inputs see the op-amp's non-inverting terminal | Gives **infinite input impedance** to both sources |
| 2 | $R_G$ sets the transconductance between the two inverting inputs | A *single resistor* controls the differential gain — easy to tune |
| 3 | Stage 2 difference amp uses 4 matched resistors | Rejects common-mode signal precisely |
| 4 | Output is single-ended referenced to ground | Compatible with ADCs and microcontrollers |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorize this exact formula — it appears in 90% of KTU IA design problems.**

$$
A_d \;=\; \underbrace{\left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_G}\right)}_{\text{Stage 1 Gain}} \;\times\; \underbrace{\left(\frac{R_3}{R_2}\right)}_{\text{Stage 2 Gain}}
$$

### Compact Formula Reference Table

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|----------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Stage 1 Differential Gain | $A_1 = 1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_G}$ | Dimensionless |
| 2 | Stage 2 Gain | $A_2 = \dfrac{R_3}{R_2}$ | Dimensionless |
| 3 | Total Differential Gain | $A_d = A_1 \cdot A_2$ | Dimensionless |
| 4 | CMRR | $\text{CMRR}_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{A_d}{A_{cm}}\right)$ | **decibels (dB)** |
| 5 | Output Voltage | $V_{out} = A_d \cdot (V_1 - V_2) + A_{cm}\cdot V_{cm}$ | Volts (V) |
| 6 | Common-Mode Voltage | $V_{cm} = \dfrac{V_1 + V_2}{2}$ | Volts (V) |
| 7 | DC Offset (ideal) | $V_{os,out} = A_d \cdot V_{os,input}$ | Volts (V) |
| 8 | Typical KTU Design Targets | $A_d = 10,\ 100,\ \text{or}\ 1000$ | Dimensionless |

### Design Trade-Off Constraints

- **Resistor Matching** (Stage 2): For $\text{CMRR} > 80\ \text{dB}$, the four resistors in Stage 2 must match within **0.1 %**. Any mismatch directly degrades CMRR.
- **Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBP)**: Closed-loop bandwidth $f_{3dB} \approx \dfrac{\text{GBP}}{A_d}$. A high gain of 1000 from an op-amp with GBP = 1 MHz gives only **1 kHz usable bandwidth**.
- **Input Bias Current**: With very high source impedances (e.g., > $1\ \text{M}\Omega$), input bias current flowing through $R_G$ creates an offset error.

### Real-World Engineering Utility

Instrumentation amplifiers are the **front-end of choice** in:
- **Medical electronics** — ECG, EEG, EMG, pulse-oximeter front-ends.
- **Industrial process control** — Load cells, pressure transducers, weigh scales (Wheatstone bridges).
- **Automotive** — Current-sense amplifiers, sensor signal conditioning.
- **Test & measurement** — Oscilloscope differential probes, data-acquisition systems (NI, Keysight, Texas Instruments DAQ platforms).
- **Aerospace** — Strain-gauge bridges on airframe wings and turbine blades.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Stage 1 Gain (The Two Non-Inverting Buffers)

Let the two inputs be $V_1$ and $V_2$. Both op-amps $A_1$ and $A_2$ are in the non-inverting configuration, so the voltages at their inverting inputs equal their non-inverting inputs due to the virtual-short principle.

**Step 1 — Apply virtual short at $A_1$:**
Voltage at the inverting input of $A_1$ = $V_1$ (since non-inverting input is $V_1$).

**Step 2 — Apply virtual short at $A_2$:**
Voltage at the inverting input of $A_2$ = $V_2$.

**Step 3 — Voltage across $R_G$:**
Since $R_G$ connects the two inverting inputs, the voltage drop across $R_G$ is:

$$
V_{R_G} \;=\; V_1 - V_2
$$

**Step 4 — Current through $R_G$ (Ohm's Law):**

$$
I_{R_G} \;=\; \frac{V_1 - V_2}{R_G}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply KCL at each inverting node:**

The same current $I_{R_G}$ must also flow through both $R_1$ resistors (because no current enters the op-amp's ideal input terminal).

$$
I_{R_1} \;=\; \frac{V_1 - V_2}{R_G}
$$

**Step 6 — Output voltages of $A_1$ and $A_2$:**

For op-amp $A_1$, KVL through $R_1$ from output back to its inverting input:

$$
V_{o1} - V_1 \;=\; I_{R_1} \cdot R_1
$$

For op-amp $A_2$, KVL through $R_1$ from output back to its inverting input:

$$
V_{o2} - V_2 \;=\; -I_{R_1} \cdot R_1
$$

**Step 7 — Subtract the two output voltages to get the differential output of Stage 1:**

$$
V_{o1} - V_{o2} \;=\; (V_1 - V_2) + 2 I_{R_1} R_1
$$

Substitute $I_{R_1}$ from Step 5:

$$
V_{o1} - V_{o2} \;=\; (V_1 - V_2) + 2 \cdot \frac{V_1 - V_2}{R_G} \cdot R_1
$$

**Step 8 — Factor out the differential input:**

$$
V_{o1} - V_{o2} \;=\; \left(1 + \frac{2 R_1}{R_G}\right) (V_1 - V_2)
$$

**Step 9 — Therefore, the Stage 1 differential gain is:**

$$
\boxed{\,A_1 \;=\; 1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_G}\,}
$$

This confirms the gain is set by a single resistor $R_G$, with the special feature that the coefficient is "2" (because $R_G$ appears in both halves of the symmetric input stage).

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Stage 2 Gain (Difference Amplifier)

The Stage 2 difference amplifier is a standard op-amp circuit. For the four-resistor version with $R_2, R_2, R_3, R_3$ and ideal op-amp assumptions, the textbook result is:

**Step 1 — Define the inputs to Stage 2:**
$V_{o1}$ feeds through $R_2$ into the inverting node.
$V_{o2}$ feeds directly into the non-inverting node (through $R_2$ to ground for bias-current compensation).

**Step 2 — Apply KCL at the inverting node:**

$$
\frac{V_{o1} - V_-}{R_2} \;=\; \frac{V_- - V_{out}}{R_3}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply virtual short:** $V_- = V_+ = V_{o2} \cdot \dfrac{R_3}{R_2 + R_3}$

**Step 4 — Solve for $V_{out}$:**

$$
V_{out} \;=\; \left(\frac{R_3}{R_2}\right) \cdot (V_{o2} - V_{o1})
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the Stage 1 output:**

$$
V_{out} \;=\; \left(\frac{R_3}{R_2}\right) \cdot \left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_G}\right) \cdot (V_2 - V_1)
$$

Therefore the overall differential gain is:

$$
\boxed{\,A_d \;=\; \left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_G}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{R_3}{R_2}\right)\,}
$$

---

## 3.3 Worked-Out Numerical Design Example (KTU Style)

### 🎯 Problem Statement
*Design an Instrumentation Amplifier using three op-amps (µA741 or equivalent) to achieve a total differential gain of **$A_d = 100$**, with Stage 2 set as a unity-gain difference amplifier. Choose $R_1 = 10\ \text{k}\Omega$. Find $R_G$. Also determine the output voltage if $V_1 = 30\ \text{mV}$ and $V_2 = 25\ \text{mV}$. Assume ideal op-amps.*

### Solution

**Step 1 — Choose Stage 2 gain:**
The problem states "Stage 2 as unity-gain difference amplifier," so:

$$
A_2 \;=\; \frac{R_3}{R_2} \;=\; 1 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad R_2 = R_3
$$

Choose a standard E12 value: $R_2 = R_3 = 10\ \text{k}\Omega$.

**Step 2 — Determine required Stage 1 gain:**

$$
A_1 \cdot A_2 \;=\; A_d \quad\Longrightarrow\quad A_1 \cdot 1 \;=\; 100 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad A_1 = 100
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Stage 1 gain formula to find $R_G$:**

$$
A_1 \;=\; 1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_G} \;\Longrightarrow\; 100 \;=\; 1 + \frac{2 \cdot 10\text{k}}{R_G}
$$

$$
99 \;=\; \frac{20\text{k}}{R_G} \;\Longrightarrow\; R_G \;=\; \frac{20{,}000}{99}\ \Omega
$$

$$
\boxed{\,R_G \;\approx\; 202.02\ \Omega\,}
$$

**Step 4 — Verify output voltage:**

$$
V_{out} \;=\; A_d \cdot (V_2 - V_1) \;=\; 100 \cdot (25\text{ mV} - 30\text{ mV})
$$

$$
V_{out} \;=\; 100 \cdot (-5\text{ mV}) \;=\; -0.5\ \text{V}
$$

The **negative sign** indicates $V_{out}$ swings *below* the common-mode reference (which is typically $V_{CC}/2$ if biased from a single supply). For dual-supply operation (±V), this is perfectly valid.

---

## 3.4 Alternative Worked Example — Different Target Gain

*Design an IA with $A_d = 10$, using $R_1 = 5\ \text{k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 10\ \text{k}\Omega$, $R_3 = 20\ \text{k}\Omega$. Find $R_G$.*

**Solution:**

$$
A_2 \;=\; \frac{R_3}{R_2} \;=\; \frac{20\text{k}}{10\text{k}} \;=\; 2
$$

$$
A_1 \;=\; \frac{A_d}{A_2} \;=\; \frac{10}{2} \;=\; 5
$$

$$
5 \;=\; 1 + \frac{2 \cdot 5\text{k}}{R_G} \;\Longrightarrow\; 4 \;=\; \frac{10\text{k}}{R_G} \;\Longrightarrow\; R_G = 2.5\ \text{k}\Omega
$$

$$
\boxed{\,R_G \;=\; 2.5\ \text{k}\Omega\,}
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🔌 SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Three-Op-Amp Instrumentation Amplifier — Functional Block Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the **signal flow architecture** of the standard 3-op-amp IA, including the gain-setting resistor $R_G$ and the matched-resistor difference amplifier at the output stage.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Vin1[/"V1 / IN+  / Source Signal A"/] --> stageA1[("Op-Amp A1\nNon-Inverting\nBuffer")]
    Vin2[/"V2 / IN-  / Source Signal B"/] --> stageA2[("Op-Amp A2\nNon-Inverting\nBuffer")]
    stageA1 --- Rgain{{"Gain Resistor R_G\n(Sets A1 = 1 + 2R1/R_G)"}}
    stageA2 --- Rgain
    stageA1 -- "R1 Feedback" --> fb1((Feedback Path 1))
    stageA2 -- "R1 Feedback" --> fb2((Feedback Path 2))
    stageA1 --> vo1[/"Stage 1 Output: Vo1"/]
    stageA2 --> vo2[/"Stage 1 Output: Vo2"/]
    vo1 --> Rtwo1{{"R2"}}
    vo2 --> Rtwo2{{"R2"}}
    Rtwo2 --> invNode(("Virtual Ground\nat V+ node"))
    Rtwo1 --> stageA3[("Op-Amp A3\nDifference Amplifier\nGain = R3 / R2")]
    invNode --> stageA3
    stageA3 -- "R3 Feedback" --> Rthree1{{"R3"}}
    Rthree1 --> invNode
    stageA3 --> vout[/"Vout  /  Single-Ended Output\nTo ADC or Microcontroller"/]

    classDef inputNode fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#88c0d0,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px;
    classDef opampNode fill:#5e81ac,stroke:#eceff4,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px;
    classDef resistorNode fill:#bf616a,stroke:#eceff4,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px;
    classDef outputNode fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#a3be8c,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px;
    class Vin1,Vin2 inputNode;
    class stageA1,stageA2,stageA3 opampNode;
    class Rgain,Rtwo1,Rtwo2,Rthree1 resistorNode;
    class vout outputNode;
```

## 4.2 Signal-Flow Architecture — Stage-by-Stage Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SRC[Signal Sources]
        src1[/"V1\nDifferential Sensor Output"/]:::srcStyle
        src2[/"V2\nDifferential Sensor Output"/]:::srcStyle
    end

    subgraph STAGE1[Stage 1 - Input Buffer]
        a1["Op-Amp A1\nNon-Inverting Amplifier"]:::opStyle
        a2["Op-Amp A2\nNon-Inverting Amplifier"]:::opStyle
        rg{{"External Gain Resistor\nR_G - Single Resistor\nTuning Element"}}:::rgStyle
    end

    subgraph STAGE2[Stage 2 - Difference Amplifier]
        a3["Op-Amp A3\nMatched 4-Resistor\nDifference Amplifier"]:::opStyle
    end

    subgraph OUT[Output]
        vout[/"Vout = A_d x V1 - V2"/]:::outStyle
        adc[/"To ADC or Next Stage"/]:::outStyle
    end

    src1 --> a1
    src2 --> a2
    a1 --- rg
    a2 --- rg
    a1 --> a3
    a2 --> a3
    a3 --> vout --> adc

    classDef srcStyle fill:#37474f,stroke:#eceff4,color:#ffffff;
    classDef opStyle fill:#1565c0,stroke:#eceff4,color:#ffffff;
    classDef rgStyle fill:#d84315,stroke:#eceff4,color:#ffffff;
    classDef outStyle fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#a3be8c,color:#ffffff;
```

## 4.3 Pin-Level Operational View (Signal Path Summary)

| Node | Physical Meaning | Typical Signal Level |
|------|------------------|----------------------|
| $V_1, V_2$ | Differential sensor inputs (e.g., strain gauge bridge) | **µV to mV** |
| $V_{o1}, V_{o2}$ | Outputs of Stage 1 (already amplified by $A_1$) | mV to V |
| $V_{out}$ | Final single-ended output | V (e.g., 0 – 5 V) |
| $V_{cm}$ | Common-mode voltage = $(V_1 + V_2)/2$ | Ideally rejected |
| $R_G$ | The single resistor that sets the gain | External, tunable |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** Always draw the 3-op-amp block diagram with $R_G$ explicitly labelled between the two inverting inputs of the input op-amps. Examiners award marks for this diagram even if the calculation has minor errors.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define an Instrumentation Amplifier. List its three key characteristics that distinguish it from a simple op-amp difference amplifier.**

**Model Answer (For 3-Mark Valuation):**

An Instrumentation Amplifier (IA) is a precision closed-loop differential amplifier that uses the three-op-amp topology to provide:
1. **Very high input impedance** at both inputs (because both inputs feed the non-inverting terminals of the input op-amps).
2. **Very high Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR)**, typically **> 80 dB**, owing to matched resistors in the output difference-amplifier stage.
3. **Single-resistor adjustable gain**, set by the external gain resistor $R_G$, giving the gain equation $A_d = (1 + 2R_1/R_G)(R_3/R_2)$.

Unlike a simple difference amplifier (which has unequal input impedances at its two inputs and lower CMRR), the IA's input stage buffers both inputs symmetrically, hence its performance is preserved even with high-impedance or mismatched sources.

*(Examiner's note: Award full 3 marks only if all three characteristics and the difference from a simple diff-amp are clearly stated.)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Derive the expression for the differential gain of the input stage of a 3-op-amp Instrumentation Amplifier.**

**Model Answer:**

Applying virtual short at both input op-amps $A_1$ and $A_2$, the voltage across the gain resistor $R_G$ is $V_1 - V_2$. By Ohm's law, the current through $R_G$ is $I = (V_1 - V_2)/R_G$. The same current flows through both feedback resistors $R_1$. Applying KVL and subtracting the two stage outputs:

$$
V_{o1} - V_{o2} \;=\; \left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_G}\right)(V_1 - V_2)
$$

Hence the **differential gain of the input stage** is:

$$
\boxed{\,A_1 \;=\; 1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_G}\,}
$$

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (KTU ESE Module Internal Choice Format)

> [!WARNING]
> **🛑 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of "2"** in $A_1 = 1 + 2R_1/R_G$ is the single most common mistake. Students write $1 + R_1/R_G$ — this yields **wrong gain by a factor of ~2**.
> 2. **Confusing Stage 1 and Stage 2 gain formulas** — Stage 1 contains the "2" factor, Stage 2 does not.
> 3. **Not specifying units** (Ω, kΩ, mV, V) in the final answer — leads to 0.5–1 mark deduction.
> 4. **Forgetting to draw the circuit/block diagram** — KTU awards 2–3 marks just for the diagram.
> 5. **Sign errors** — remember $V_{out} = A_d \cdot (V_2 - V_1)$ in the most common convention.

---

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) — Design of IA with Gain = 100

**[A] `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme]`**

(a) **[7 Marks]** Draw the circuit diagram of a three-op-amp Instrumentation Amplifier. Explain the function of each op-amp stage and derive the expression for the total differential gain.

(b) **[7 Marks]** Design a 3-op-amp Instrumentation Amplifier to provide a differential gain of **$A_d = 100$**. Use the following specifications:
   - Op-amp: µA741
   - Stage 2 gain = 1 (unity-gain difference amplifier)
   - Choose $R_1 = 10\ \text{k}\Omega$
   - Calculate $R_G$ and select a standard E12 resistor value.
   - If $V_1 = 40\ \text{mV}$ and $V_2 = 35\ \text{mV}$, compute $V_{out}$.
   - Mention one practical application of the designed amplifier.

---

### ✅ Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — [7 Marks]

**Circuit Diagram (2 marks):**
The IA consists of two non-inverting input op-amps ($A_1, A_2$) whose outputs feed a difference amplifier (op-amp $A_3$ with four matched resistors $R_2, R_2, R_3, R_3$). A single resistor $R_G$ connects the inverting inputs of $A_1$ and $A_2$. (Refer to Section 4.1 diagram above.)

**Function of Each Stage (2 marks):**
- **Op-Amps A1, A2 (Input Buffer Stage):** Provide high input impedance and amplify the differential signal by $A_1 = 1 + 2R_1/R_G$.
- **Op-Amp A3 (Difference Amplifier):** Converts differential output of Stage 1 to single-ended output and rejects common-mode voltage. Gain = $R_3/R_2$.

**Derivation (3 marks):**
(Refer to Section 3.1 derivation. Summary:)

$$
A_d \;=\; \underbrace{\left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_G}\right)}_{A_1} \cdot \underbrace{\left(\frac{R_3}{R_2}\right)}_{A_2}
$$

The factor "2" arises because the same current $I = (V_1-V_2)/R_G$ flows through both $R_1$ resistors.

---

#### Part (b) — [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Stage 2 Gain = 1:**
Choose $R_2 = R_3 = 10\ \text{k}\Omega$ (matched, E12 standard value). (1 mark)

**Step 2 — Required Stage 1 Gain:**
$A_1 = A_d / A_2 = 100 / 1 = 100$. (1 mark)

**Step 3 — Compute $R_G$:**

$$
R_G \;=\; \frac{2 R_1}{A_1 - 1} \;=\; \frac{2 \cdot 10\text{k}\Omega}{100 - 1} \;=\; \frac{20{,}000}{99}\;\Omega
$$

$$
R_G \;\approx\; 202.02\ \Omega
$$

**Step 4 — Select E12 Standard Value:**
Nearest E12 value is **$R_G = 220\ \Omega$**. (Recompute gain for the chosen value, if asked: $A_1 \approx 1 + 20000/220 \approx 91.9$, so $A_d \approx 91.9$.) (1 mark)

**Step 5 — Compute $V_{out}$ (using exact $R_G = 220\ \Omega$):**

$$
V_{out} \;=\; A_d \cdot (V_2 - V_1) \;=\; 91.9 \cdot (35\text{ mV} - 40\text{ mV}) \;=\; 91.9 \cdot (-5\text{ mV})
$$

$$
V_{out} \;\approx\; -0.46\ \text{V} \;\;\text{(approximately -0.5 V)}
$$

If using the ideal $R_G = 202.02\ \Omega$:

$$
V_{out} \;=\; 100 \cdot (35 - 40)\text{ mV} \;=\; -0.5\ \text{V}
$$

(2 marks)

**Step 6 — Application (1 mark):**
Suitable for **ECG signal conditioning** or **strain-gauge bridge amplifier** in weigh scales / load cells.

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Design Problem with CMRR Focus

**[B] `[KTU University Exam - Sample Question Bank 2024]`**

(a) **[7 Marks]** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the operation of the 3-op-amp Instrumentation Amplifier. Derive the expression for its differential-mode gain and show that the common-mode gain is ideally zero.

(b) **[7 Marks]** For an IA with $R_1 = 5.1\ \text{k}\Omega$, $R_G = 1\ \text{k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 10\ \text{k}\Omega$, $R_3 = 10\ \text{k}\Omega$:
   - Compute the differential gain $A_d$.
   - If the measured common-mode gain is $A_{cm} = 0.001$, calculate the CMRR in dB.
   - Comment on whether the CMRR is satisfactory for biomedical instrumentation (which typically requires CMRR > 80 dB).

---

### ✅ Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — [7 Marks]

**Block Diagram (2 marks):** (Refer to Section 4.2 above.) The IA has two cascaded stages — input buffer stage and difference amplifier — with a single gain-setting resistor $R_G$.

**Operation (1 mark):**
The input buffer stage provides high input impedance and amplifies only the *difference* $(V_1 - V_2)$. The difference amplifier then rejects the common-mode component and converts the differential output to a single-ended output.

**Differential-Mode Gain Derivation (2 marks):**

$$
V_{out} \;=\; \left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_G}\right)\left(\frac{R_3}{R_2}\right)(V_2 - V_1)
$$

Hence $A_d = \left(1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_G}\right)\left(\dfrac{R_3}{R_2}\right)$.

**Common-Mode Gain Proof (2 marks):**
When $V_1 = V_2 = V_{cm}$, both input op-amps see the *same* voltage on their non-inverting inputs. The voltage across $R_G$ becomes $V_1 - V_2 = 0$, hence no current flows through $R_G$, and the voltage drop across each $R_1$ is zero. Therefore $V_{o1} = V_{o2} = V_{cm}$, and the difference amplifier outputs $V_{out} = 0$. Thus **$A_{cm} = 0$** ideally, giving theoretically infinite CMRR.

---

#### Part (b) — [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Compute Differential Gain (3 marks):**

$$
A_1 \;=\; 1 + \frac{2 \cdot 5.1\text{k}\Omega}{1\text{k}\Omega} \;=\; 1 + 10.2 \;=\; 11.2
$$

$$
A_2 \;=\; \frac{R_3}{R_2} \;=\; \frac{10\text{k}}{10\text{k}} \;=\; 1
$$

$$
A_d \;=\; A_1 \cdot A_2 \;=\; 11.2 \cdot 1 \;=\; \boxed{11.2}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute CMRR (2 marks):**

$$
\text{CMRR}_{\text{dB}} \;=\; 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{A_d}{A_{cm}}\right) \;=\; 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{11.2}{0.001}\right)
$$

$$
\text{CMRR}_{\text{dB}} \;=\; 20 \log_{10}(11{,}200) \;=\; 20 \cdot (4.049)
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\text{CMRR} \;\approx\; 80.99\ \text{dB}\,}
$$

**Step 3 — Comment (2 marks):**
A CMRR of **80.99 dB** is **marginally satisfactory** for biomedical instrumentation, as the typical threshold is 80 dB. However, for high-fidelity ECG/EEG recording (where 50 Hz mains interference must be rejected at > 100 dB), this design is insufficient. Recommendations:
- Use precision op-amps (e.g., **INA128** or **AD620** monolithic IA) with matched laser-trimmed resistors.
- Use **guarded cables** and proper grounding.
- Increase the differential gain $A_d$ (which improves effective CMRR by reducing the ratio $A_{cm}/A_d$).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🚀

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This Before the Exam!**

- ✅ **Instrumentation Amplifier** = 3-op-amp topology with single-resistor gain control.
- ✅ **Key formula:** $A_d = \left(1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_G}\right)\left(\dfrac{R_3}{R_2}\right)$ — **memorize the factor of 2!**
- ✅ **Gain is set by ONE resistor** ($R_G$) — the unique selling point of the IA.
- ✅ **Inputs go to non-inverting terminals** → high input impedance (typically > $10^9\ \Omega$).
- ✅ **CMRR** = $20\log_{10}(A_d/A_{cm})$ in dB; ideal = ∞, practical > 80 dB.
- ✅ **Resistor matching** in Stage 2 (four matched resistors) is critical for high CMRR.
- ✅ **Output voltage sign** convention: $V_{out} = A_d \cdot (V_2 - V_1)$ when $V_1$ is connected to inverting side of difference amp.
- ✅ **Bandwidth shrinks with gain** — $f_{3dB} \approx \text{GBP}/A_d$ (Gain-Bandwidth trade-off).
- ✅ **Standard design procedure:** Fix $A_2$ → compute $A_1 = A_d/A_2$ → solve for $R_G = 2R_1/(A_1 - 1)$.
- ✅ **Common use cases:** ECG/EEG front-ends, strain-gauge bridges, load cells, thermocouples, industrial sensors.
- ✅ **Monolithic IA ICs** (e.g., INA128, AD620) outperform discrete designs due to on-chip laser trimming.
- ✅ **Always draw the circuit diagram with $R_G$ labelled** — KTU awards 2–3 marks just for this.
- ✅ **Watch out:** Single op-amp difference amplifier has mismatched input impedances → IA solves this.

> 🏁 **End of Notes — All the best for your KTU Analog Circuits Lab Examination!**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of Active Integrator and Differentiator Circuits
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICL408 - ANALOG CIRCUITS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Design of Active Integrator and Differentiator Circuits

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Active Integrator** is an operational amplifier based circuit that performs the mathematical operation of integration on the input time-varying signal, producing an output voltage proportional to the time integral of the input voltage. An **Active Differentiator** is its dual, producing an output voltage proportional to the time derivative of the input voltage. Both circuits employ an op-amp (the "active" element) with reactive components (capacitor and resistor) in a negative feedback configuration, overcoming the limitations of passive RC/CR networks such as loading effects, gain roll-off, and non-ideal amplitude response.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCICL408 - Module 2):** Students must be able to design, build, and experimentally verify the frequency response of first-order active integrators and differentiators. The design specification typically includes selecting $R$ and $C$ such that the input signal frequency $f_{in}$ lies in the proper integration/differentiation region with respect to the cut-off frequency $f_c$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**The Water Tank Analogy (Integrator):** Imagine pouring water into a cylindrical tank at a rate that varies over time. The *height* of the water level in the tank at any moment equals the *total accumulated volume* of water poured in up to that point. Mathematically:

$$h(t) = \frac{1}{A}\int_{0}^{t} q(\tau)\,d\tau$$

where $q(t)$ is the inflow rate (analogous to input voltage) and $A$ is the tank's cross-sectional area (analogous to the $RC$ time constant). The integrator circuit behaves identically: it accumulates the input signal over time and outputs the running integral.

**The Speedometer Analogy (Differentiator):** A car's speedometer displays not the *distance* traveled, but the *rate of change* of distance with respect to time. It differentiates position to yield velocity. The differentiator circuit does the same to an electrical signal — it outputs how *fast* the input is changing at each instant.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** The integrator is a "memory" device (cumulative history), while the differentiator is an "instability detector" (rate detector). This is why integrators are used in ADC (dual-slope), filter design, and analog computers, while differentiators are used in edge detectors, FM demodulators, and PID controllers (D-term).

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

The following constants and metrics are standard to the design of these circuits:

- **Cut-off Frequency** $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ in **Hz**
- **Time Constant** $\tau = RC$ in **seconds**
- **Angular Frequency** $\omega_c = \dfrac{1}{RC}$ in **rad/s**
- **DC Gain of Practical Integrator** $A_0 = \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}$ (dimensionless, typically 10)
- **High-Frequency Gain of Practical Differentiator** $A_{HF} = \dfrac{C_1}{C_f}$ (dimensionless, typically 0.01)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis and KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Theory — The Ideal Integrator

The ideal active integrator consists of an op-amp with a resistor $R$ at the inverting input and a capacitor $C$ in the feedback path. The non-inverting input is grounded.

### Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

- **Virtual Ground Concept:** The op-amp's high open-loop gain $A_{OL} \to \infty$ forces the inverting node voltage to match the non-inverting node. Since the non-inverting input is grounded, $V^- = 0$ V. This is the **virtual ground**.
- **No Current into Op-amp Terminals:** The op-amp's high input impedance ensures the current entering the inverting terminal is approximately zero ($I^- \approx 0$).
- **KCL at Inverting Node:** All current flowing through $R$ from the input must flow through $C$ into the feedback path.
- **Current through Resistor:** $I_R = \dfrac{V_{in} - 0}{R} = \dfrac{V_{in}}{R}$
- **Current through Capacitor:** $I_C = C \dfrac{d(0 - V_{out})}{dt} = -C\dfrac{dV_{out}}{dt}$
- **Equating Currents:** Since KCL gives $I_R = -I_C$, we get the governing differential equation.

## 2.2 Operational Theory — The Ideal Differentiator

The ideal differentiator swaps the position of $R$ and $C$: the capacitor $C$ is at the input, and resistor $R$ is in the feedback path.

### Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

- **Input Current through C:** $I_C = C \dfrac{dV_{in}}{dt}$ (since the inverting node is at virtual ground)
- **Feedback Current through R:** $I_R = -\dfrac{V_{out}}{R}$
- **KCL at Inverting Node:** $I_C + I_R = 0$, leading to the governing equation.

## 2.3 Frequency Response Behavior

| Parameter | Active Integrator | Active Differentiator |
|---|---|---|
| **Input Impedance** | $R$ (finite) | $\dfrac{1}{j\omega C}$ (decreases with frequency) |
| **Output Impedance** | Near zero (due to op-amp) | Near zero (due to op-amp) |
| **Type of Filter** | Low-Pass Filter (LPF) | High-Pass Filter (HPF) |
| **Gain Behavior** | $\propto \dfrac{1}{\omega}$ (rolls off at $-20$ dB/decade) | $\propto \omega$ (rises at $+20$ dB/decade) |
| **Phase Shift** | $-90^\circ$ (constant) | $+90^\circ$ (constant) |
| **Operating Region** | $f_{in} \gg f_c$ | $f_{in} \ll f_c$ |
| **Stability** | Unstable in ideal form (needs $R_f$ for compensation) | Unstable in ideal form (needs $C_f$ for compensation) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** A common 2-mark question asks: *"For an ideal integrator, what happens if the input is a DC signal?"* The answer is that the output will ramp linearly until saturation, because integrating a constant gives a ramp. This saturation is precisely why the practical integrator is preferred in real designs.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula | Expression | Units | Applicable To |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Ideal Integrator Output** | $V_{out}(t) = -\dfrac{1}{RC}\int_{0}^{t} V_{in}(\tau)\,d\tau + V_{out}(0)$ | Volts | Active Integrator |
| **Ideal Differentiator Output** | $V_{out}(t) = -RC\dfrac{dV_{in}(t)}{dt}$ | Volts | Active Differentiator |
| **Cut-off Frequency** | $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | Hz | Both |
| **Time Constant** | $\tau = RC$ | s | Both |
| **Integrator Transfer Function** | $\dfrac{V_{out}(s)}{V_{in}(s)} = -\dfrac{1}{sRC}$ | Dimensionless | Integrator in s-domain |
| **Differentiator Transfer Function** | $\dfrac{V_{out}(s)}{V_{in}(s)} = -sRC$ | Dimensionless | Differentiator in s-domain |
| **Integrator Magnitude Response** | $\vert H(j\omega) \vert = \dfrac{1}{\omega RC}$ | V/V | Integrator |
| **Differentiator Magnitude Response** | $\vert H(j\omega) \vert = \omega RC$ | V/V | Differentiator |
| **Practical Integrator DC Gain** | $A_0 = \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}$ | Dimensionless | Practical Integrator |
| **Practical Differentiator HF Gain** | $A_{HF} = \dfrac{C_1}{C_f}$ | Dimensionless | Practical Differentiator |
| **Practical Integrator Cut-off** | $f_a = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_f C}$ | Hz | Practical Integrator |
| **Practical Differentiator Cut-off** | $f_b = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R C_f}$ | Hz | Practical Differentiator |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Analog Computers (Historical):** Integrators and differentiators were the building blocks of analog computers used to solve differential equations in real-time (e.g., flight simulators in the 1960s).
- **Active Filters:** Cascaded integrators form second-order low-pass filters (Sallen-Key, Tow-Thomas biquads) used in audio equalizers, biomedical signal processing, and control loops.
- **Control Systems (PID):** The **D** (derivative) and **I** (integral) terms in a Proportional-Integral-Derivative controller are implemented using exactly these active circuits to reduce steady-state error and improve transient response.
- **Signal Processing:** Integrators are used in dual-slope ADCs, sample-and-hold integrators, and energy meters. Differentiators are used in FM demodulators, edge detectors in image processing, and high-frequency noise amplifiers (with care).
- **Communication Systems:** Phase-locked loops (PLL), waveform generators (function generators), and zero-crossing detectors all employ integrator/differentiator stages.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations and Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Ideal Integrator Output

We start with the ideal integrator circuit. Let the input voltage be $V_{in}(t)$ and the output be $V_{out}(t)$.

**Step 1: Apply the Virtual Short Concept**

The op-amp is in negative feedback with high open-loop gain. By the virtual short principle:

$$V^- = V^+ = 0 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2: Write the Current through the Input Resistor**

Since the inverting terminal is at virtual ground, the full input voltage appears across $R$:

$$I_R(t) = \frac{V_{in}(t) - V^-}{R} = \frac{V_{in}(t) - 0}{R} = \frac{V_{in}(t)}{R}$$

**Step 3: Write the Current through the Feedback Capacitor**

The voltage across the feedback capacitor is $(V^- - V_{out}) = (0 - V_{out}) = -V_{out}(t)$. The fundamental current-voltage relationship of a capacitor is $i = C \frac{dv}{dt}$. Therefore:

$$I_C(t) = C \frac{d(V^- - V_{out})}{dt} = C \frac{d(0 - V_{out})}{dt} = -C \frac{dV_{out}(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 4: Apply Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL) at the Inverting Node**

Since no current enters the op-amp's inverting input ($I^- \approx 0$):

$$I_R + I_C = 0$$

**Step 5: Substitute and Simplify**

$$\frac{V_{in}(t)}{R} - C \frac{dV_{out}(t)}{dt} = 0$$

**Step 6: Rearrange to Isolate the Output Derivative**

$$C \frac{dV_{out}(t)}{dt} = \frac{V_{in}(t)}{R}$$

**Step 7: Multiply Both Sides by $\frac{1}{C}$**

$$\frac{dV_{out}(t)}{dt} = \frac{1}{RC} V_{in}(t)$$

**Step 8: Integrate Both Sides with Respect to Time**

$$\int_{0}^{t} \frac{dV_{out}(\tau)}{d\tau} d\tau = \frac{1}{RC} \int_{0}^{t} V_{in}(\tau)\, d\tau$$

**Step 9: Evaluate the Left-Hand Integral (Fundamental Theorem of Calculus)**

$$V_{out}(t) - V_{out}(0) = \frac{1}{RC} \int_{0}^{t} V_{in}(\tau)\, d\tau$$

**Step 10: Final Result — The Governing Integrator Equation**

$$\boxed{V_{out}(t) = -\left[ \frac{1}{RC}\int_{0}^{t} V_{in}(\tau)\,d\tau \right] + V_{out}(0)}$$

The negative sign indicates a $180^\circ$ phase inversion, characteristic of inverting op-amp configurations.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — Ideal Differentiator Output

The differentiator circuit is the dual of the integrator. The capacitor is now at the input and the resistor is in the feedback path.

**Step 1: Apply Virtual Short**

$$V^- = V^+ = 0 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2: Write the Current through the Input Capacitor**

The voltage across the input capacitor is $(V_{in} - V^-) = V_{in}(t)$. Therefore:

$$I_C(t) = C \frac{d(V_{in} - V^-)}{dt} = C \frac{dV_{in}(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 3: Write the Current through the Feedback Resistor**

The voltage across the feedback resistor is $(V^- - V_{out}) = -V_{out}(t)$. Therefore:

$$I_R(t) = \frac{V^- - V_{out}}{R} = \frac{0 - V_{out}(t)}{R} = -\frac{V_{out}(t)}{R}$$

**Step 4: Apply KCL at the Inverting Node**

$$I_C + I_R = 0$$

**Step 5: Substitute the Expressions**

$$C \frac{dV_{in}(t)}{dt} - \frac{V_{out}(t)}{R} = 0$$

**Step 6: Solve for $V_{out}(t)$**

$$\frac{V_{out}(t)}{R} = C \frac{dV_{in}(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 7: Multiply Both Sides by $R$**

$$V_{out}(t) = RC \frac{dV_{in}(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 8: Apply the Sign Convention**

Including the negative sign for the inverting configuration:

$$\boxed{V_{out}(t) = -RC \frac{dV_{in}(t)}{dt}}$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Input Waveform vs. Output Response

| Input Waveform $V_{in}(t)$ | Integrator Output $V_{out}(t)$ | Differentiator Output $V_{out}(t)$ |
|---|---|---|
| **DC** $V_0$ (constant) | **Ramp** $-\dfrac{V_0}{RC} t$ (linear ramp to saturation) | **Zero** (derivative of constant is 0) |
| **Sinusoidal** $V_p \sin(\omega t)$ | $-\dfrac{V_p}{\omega RC}\cos(\omega t)$ (cosine, $-90^\circ$ phase shift) | $-V_p \omega RC \cos(\omega t)$ (cosine, $+90^\circ$ phase shift) |
| **Square Wave** (amplitude $\pm V_p$) | **Triangular Wave** (peaks at $V_p T/4RC$) | **Spikes** (impulses at transitions) |
| **Triangular Wave** | **Parabolic Wave** (squared segments) | **Square Wave** (constant $\pm V_p \omega RC$) |
| **Ramp** $mt$ | **Parabola** $-\dfrac{m t^2}{2RC}$ | **Constant** $-mRC$ |
| **Pulse** (impulse) | **Step Response** (capacitor charges instantly) | **Impulse Response** (Dirac delta) |

---

## 3.4 Complete Design Procedure — Active Integrator

The following is a KTU-standard design workflow, applicable to a 14-mark ESE question.

**Design Specification:** Design an ideal integrator using op-amp IC 741 to integrate a sinusoidal input signal of frequency $f_{in} = 1 \text{ kHz}$ and amplitude $V_p = 2 \text{ V}$. The integration should be faithful (at least 10x below cut-off).

**Step 1: Choose the Operating Frequency Range**

For faithful integration, the input frequency $f_{in}$ must be much greater than the cut-off frequency $f_c$. A common design choice is:

$$f_c = \frac{f_{in}}{10} = \frac{1000}{10} = 100 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 2: Apply the Cut-off Frequency Formula**

$$f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi R C} \implies RC = \frac{1}{2\pi f_c}$$

$$RC = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 100} = 1.5915 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 1.5915 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 3: Choose a Standard Capacitor Value**

Select $C = 0.01 \text{ }\mu\text{F} = 10^{-8} \text{ F}$ (a standard E12 value).

**Step 4: Calculate the Required Resistor**

$$R = \frac{1.5915 \times 10^{-3}}{10^{-8}} = 1.5915 \times 10^{5} \text{ }\Omega \approx 159 \text{ k}\Omega$$

Use the nearest standard value: $R = 150 \text{ k}\Omega$ or $R = 160 \text{ k}\Omega$.

**Step 5: Verify the Output Amplitude**

For a sinusoidal input $V_{in}(t) = 2\sin(2\pi \times 1000\, t)$:

$$V_{out}(t) = -\frac{1}{RC} \int V_{in}(t)\, dt = -\frac{V_p}{\omega RC}\cos(\omega t)$$

$$\vert V_{out,p} \vert = \frac{2}{2\pi \times 1000 \times 1.5915 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.2 \text{ V}$$

This is well within the op-amp's linear range ($\pm 13$ V typical for $\pm 15$ V supply), so no saturation will occur.

**Step 6: List the Components and Connections**

- IC 741 op-amp
- $R = 150 \text{ k}\Omega$ at the inverting input
- $C = 0.01 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$ in the feedback path
- $\pm 15$ V dual power supply
- Function generator for sinusoidal input
- CRO for output observation

---

## 3.5 Complete Design Procedure — Active Differentiator

**Design Specification:** Design an active differentiator using IC 741 to differentiate a triangular wave of frequency $f_{in} = 500 \text{ Hz}$ and amplitude $V_p = 1 \text{ V}$.

**Step 1: Choose the Cut-off Frequency**

For faithful differentiation, $f_{in} \ll f_c$. Choose:

$$f_c = 10 \times f_{in} = 10 \times 500 = 5000 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 2: Apply the Cut-off Formula**

$$RC = \frac{1}{2\pi f_c} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 5000} = 3.183 \times 10^{-5} \text{ s} = 31.83 \text{ }\mu\text{s}$$

**Step 3: Choose a Standard Capacitor**

Select $C = 0.01 \text{ }\mu\text{F} = 10^{-8} \text{ F}$.

**Step 4: Calculate the Required Resistor**

$$R = \frac{3.183 \times 10^{-5}}{10^{-8}} = 3183 \text{ }\Omega \approx 3.3 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 5: Verify the Output**

For a triangular input with slope $m = \dfrac{2V_p}{T/2} = 4V_p f_{in} = 4 \times 1 \times 500 = 2000 \text{ V/s}$:

$$V_{out} = -RC \cdot m = -3.183 \times 10^{-5} \times 2000 = -0.0637 \text{ V}$$

This is a clean square wave of amplitude $63.7 \text{ mV}$, confirming correct differentiation.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank and Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define an active integrator. State its ideal output equation. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Model Answer:**

An active integrator is an op-amp based circuit that produces an output voltage proportional to the time integral of the input voltage. It uses an op-amp with a resistor at the inverting input and a capacitor in the feedback path. The ideal output equation is:

$$V_{out}(t) = -\frac{1}{RC}\int_{0}^{t} V_{in}(\tau)\, d\tau + V_{out}(0)$$

where $R$ is the input resistor, $C$ is the feedback capacitor, and $V_{out}(0)$ is the initial capacitor voltage. It acts as a low-pass filter with cut-off frequency $f_c = 1/(2\pi RC)$.

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between an ideal integrator and a practical integrator. [KTU University Exam - July 2023]

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Ideal Integrator | Practical Integrator |
|---|---|---|
| **Feedback Component** | Only capacitor $C$ | Resistor $R_f$ in parallel with $C$ |
| **DC Gain** | Infinite (causes saturation) | Finite $A_0 = R_f/R_1$ (typically 10) |
| **Low-Frequency Behavior** | Output ramps indefinitely | Output saturates at $A_0 V_{in}$ |
| **Stability** | Marginally stable, drifts | Stable due to bounded DC gain |
| **Application** | Theoretical analysis | Real-world signal processing |

The practical integrator (also called the *lossy integrator*) prevents DC saturation by limiting the low-frequency gain to a finite, controllable value.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]

**(a)** Derive the output equation of an ideal op-amp based active integrator. Explain the role of the virtual ground concept. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Design an ideal integrator using IC 741 to integrate a $1 \text{ kHz}$ sine wave of $2 \text{ V}$ peak. Verify that the output is faithful and lies within the linear range of the op-amp. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

The derivation proceeds in 10 steps (refer to Section 3.1 for the full step-by-step breakdown):

**Step 1:** Apply virtual short: $V^- = V^+ = 0$ V
**Step 2:** Current through input resistor: $I_R = V_{in}/R$
**Step 3:** Current through feedback capacitor: $I_C = -C \frac{dV_{out}}{dt}$
**Step 4:** KCL at inverting node: $I_R + I_C = 0$
**Step 5 to Step 10:** Algebraic manipulation yields the final governing equation:

$$V_{out}(t) = -\frac{1}{RC}\int_{0}^{t} V_{in}(\tau)\, d\tau + V_{out}(0)$$

**Role of Virtual Ground:** The virtual ground concept is essential because:
- It simplifies the analysis by fixing $V^-$ at 0 V, regardless of $V_{in}$.
- It decouples the input and output, ensuring the output is determined solely by the feedback network.
- It allows the capacitor to act as a true integrating element without loading effects from the source.

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Step 1: Choose the cut-off frequency.** For faithful integration, $f_c \leq f_{in}/10$:

$$f_c = \frac{1000}{10} = 100 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 2: Calculate the time constant:**

$$RC = \frac{1}{2\pi f_c} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 100} = 1.5915 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 3: Choose a standard capacitor.** Let $C = 0.01 \text{ }\mu\text{F} = 10 \text{ nF}$ (E12 value).

**Step 4: Calculate the resistor:**

$$R = \frac{1.5915 \times 10^{-3}}{10^{-8}} = 159.15 \text{ k}\Omega \approx 160 \text{ k}\Omega \text{ (standard E12)}$$

**Step 5: Verify output amplitude.** For $V_{in}(t) = 2\sin(2\pi \times 1000\,t)$:

$$V_{out}(t) = -\frac{1}{RC}\int V_{in}(t)\,dt = -\frac{2}{2\pi \times 1000 \times 1.5915 \times 10^{-3}}\cos(2\pi \times 1000\,t)$$

$$\vert V_{out,p} \vert = \frac{2}{10} = 0.2 \text{ V}$$

Since $0.2 \text{ V} \ll V_{sat} \approx 13 \text{ V}$, the op-amp operates in the linear region. The output is a cosine wave (90-degree phase lag) of amplitude $0.2 \text{ V}$, confirming faithful integration.

**Component List:**

| Component | Value | Rating |
|---|---|---|
| Op-Amp | IC 741 | DIP-8 package |
| $R$ | $160 \text{ k}\Omega$ | $\pm 5\%$, $1/4 \text{ W}$ |
| $C$ | $10 \text{ nF}$ | Ceramic, $50 \text{ V}$ |
| Supply | $\pm 15 \text{ V}$ DC | Regulated |

---

### Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - July 2024]

**(a)** Derive the output equation of an ideal op-amp based active differentiator. Mention the input waveform required to obtain a square wave at the output. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Compare the active integrator and differentiator in terms of circuit topology, transfer function, frequency response, and applications. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

The derivation follows 8 steps (refer to Section 3.2 for the exhaustive breakdown):

**Step 1:** Virtual short gives $V^- = 0$ V.
**Step 2:** Input current through $C$: $I_C = C \frac{dV_{in}}{dt}$
**Step 3:** Feedback current through $R$: $I_R = -V_{out}/R$
**Step 4 to Step 8:** KCL and algebraic manipulation yields:

$$\boxed{V_{out}(t) = -RC \frac{dV_{in}(t)}{dt}}$$

**Input Waveform for Square-Wave Output:** A **triangular wave** input produces a **square wave** output. The constant slope of the triangular wave (rising and falling linearly) yields a constant positive or negative derivative, which the differentiator outputs as $\pm V_{out}$ (a square wave).

**Verification:** For $V_{in}(t) = mt$ (rising ramp with slope $m$):

$$V_{out}(t) = -RC \cdot m = \text{constant}$$

This produces a flat-topped square pulse during the rising edge and an inverted flat pulse during the falling edge — a square wave.

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

| Aspect | Active Integrator | Active Differentiator |
|---|---|---|
| **Circuit Topology** | $R$ at input, $C$ in feedback | $C$ at input, $R$ in feedback |
| **Time-Domain Output** | $V_{out} = -\dfrac{1}{RC}\int V_{in}\, dt$ | $V_{out} = -RC \dfrac{dV_{in}}{dt}$ |
| **Transfer Function** | $H(s) = -\dfrac{1}{sRC}$ | $H(s) = -sRC$ |
| **Frequency Response** | $\vert H(j\omega) \vert = \dfrac{1}{\omega RC}$ (decreasing) | $\vert H(j\omega) \vert = \omega RC$ (increasing) |
| **Filter Type** | Low-Pass Filter | High-Pass Filter |
| **Phase Response** | $-90^\circ$ (constant lag) | $+90^\circ$ (constant lead) |
| **Stability** | Unstable in ideal form | Unstable in ideal form |
| **Bode Plot Slope** | $-20$ dB/decade | $+20$ dB/decade |
| **Input Frequency for Operation** | $f_{in} \gg f_c$ | $f_{in} \ll f_c$ |
| **DC Input Response** | Output ramps to saturation | Output is zero |
| **Practical Applications** | ADC integrators, analog computers, filters, PID controllers | FM demodulators, edge detectors, PID-D term, waveform generators |
| **Real-World Waveform Pair** | Square $\to$ Triangle | Triangle $\to$ Square |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks**
> 1. **Sign Convention Error ($-2$ marks):** Forgetting the negative sign in $V_{out} = -(1/RC)\int V_{in}\, dt$. The inverting configuration always introduces a $180^\circ$ phase inversion.
> 2. **Wrong Operating Region ($-2$ marks):** Confusing the condition for integrator and differentiator. Remember: **Integrator works when $f_{in} \gg f_c$**, and **Differentiator works when $f_{in} \ll f_c$**.
> 3. **Saturation Ignored ($-1$ mark):** Not verifying that the designed output amplitude lies within the op-amp's linear range ($\pm V_{sat} - 2$ V typically).
> 4. **Standard Values ($-1$ mark):** Failing to choose $R$ and $C$ from the E12/E24 standard series. KTU expects practical, buildable designs.
> 5. **Initial Condition Omission ($-1$ mark):** In integrator derivations, ignoring $V_{out}(0)$ — the initial capacitor voltage — leads to an incomplete answer.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap and Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist**

### Core Concepts

- The **active integrator** performs mathematical integration; the **active differentiator** performs mathematical differentiation.
- Both circuits use an op-amp in inverting configuration, but with $R$ and $C$ **swapped** between input and feedback paths.
- The governing equations are:
  - **Integrator:** $V_{out}(t) = -\dfrac{1}{RC}\int_{0}^{t} V_{in}(\tau)\, d\tau + V_{out}(0)$
  - **Differentiator:** $V_{out}(t) = -RC \dfrac{dV_{in}(t)}{dt}$
- The cut-off frequency is $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ for both circuits.

### Operating Region Rules

- For **faithful integration**: $f_{in} \geq 10 \times f_c$ (input frequency much greater than cut-off)
- For **faithful differentiation**: $f_{in} \leq f_c / 10$ (input frequency much less than cut-off)

### Design Workflow (4 Steps)

1. **Specify** input frequency $f_{in}$ and required operating region.
2. **Choose** $f_c$ to satisfy the region rule.
3. **Select** a standard $C$ and compute $R = 1/(2\pi f_c C)$.
4. **Verify** output amplitude is within op-amp linear range and within the design specification.

### Waveform Transformation Pairs

- **Integrator:** Square $\to$ Triangle, Step $\to$ Ramp, Sine $\to$ Cosine (with $-90^\circ$ shift)
- **Differentiator:** Triangle $\to$ Square, Ramp $\to$ Step, Sine $\to$ Cosine (with $+90^\circ$ shift)

### Stability Caveats

- **Ideal Integrator**: Suffers from DC drift and saturation; impractical for DC inputs.
- **Ideal Differentiator**: Amplifies high-frequency noise aggressively; unstable at high frequencies.
- **Practical Integrator**: Adds $R_f$ in feedback to limit DC gain to $A_0 = R_f/R_1$.
- **Practical Differentiator**: Adds $C_f$ in feedback to limit high-frequency gain to $A_{HF} = C_1/C_f$.

### Component Selection Hints for Lab

- Standard capacitors: $0.01 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$, $0.1 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$, $1 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$ (E12 values)
- Standard resistors: $1 \text{ k}\Omega$, $10 \text{ k}\Omega$, $100 \text{ k}\Omega$ (E12 values)
- Op-amp: IC 741 (or any general-purpose op-amp like TL081, LM358)
- Power supply: $\pm 15$ V DC regulated

### Key Differences at a Glance

| Feature | Integrator | Differentiator |
|---|---|---|
| Math Operation | Integral of input | Derivative of input |
| Filter Type | LPF | HPF |
| Frequency Behavior | Gain $\propto 1/f$ | Gain $\propto f$ |
| Phase | $-90^\circ$ | $+90^\circ$ |
| Noise | Smooths noise | Amplifies noise |

### Common Viva Questions

1. *Why is the practical integrator preferred over the ideal integrator?* — *Answer:* It prevents DC saturation by limiting the low-frequency gain through a feedback resistor $R_f$.
2. *What happens if the input frequency equals the cut-off frequency?* — *Answer:* Neither faithful integration nor differentiation occurs; the gain is exactly 1 (0 dB) and the phase shift is $\pm 45^\circ$ from the unity-gain crossover.
3. *Can a differentiator be used to detect edges in a digital signal?* — *Answer:* Yes, the sharp transition produces a high-amplitude spike at the output, signaling an edge.
4. *Why is the differentiator called a "high-pass filter"?* — *Answer:* Because high-frequency components are passed with high gain while low-frequency components are attenuated.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Comparator Circuits Using Op-Amp
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICL408 - ANALOG CIRCUITS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Comparator Circuits Using Op-Amp

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Comparator Definition (KTU 2024 Standard):**
> An **Op-Amp Comparator** is a high-gain, open-loop configuration of an operational amplifier that compares two analog input voltages — one applied to the inverting terminal ($V_{in}^-$) and the other to the non-inverting terminal ($V_{in}^+$) — and produces a **binary (two-level) digital output** indicating which input is larger. Because the open-loop differential gain $A_{OL}$ of a typical op-amp exceeds **$10^5$**, even a micro-volt difference between inputs drives the output into saturation.

The two canonical output saturation levels are:

$$V_{out} = +V_{sat} \approx +V_{CC} - 1.5\,\text{V} \quad \text{when } V_{in}^+ > V_{in}^-$$

$$V_{out} = -V_{sat} \approx -V_{EE} + 1.5\,\text{V} \quad \text{when } V_{in}^+ < V_{in}^-$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In the KTU 2024 Analog Circuits Lab (PCICL408), the comparator experiment requires the student to (i) build and test a **basic comparator**, (ii) a **Schmitt trigger** (regenerative comparator with hysteresis), and (iii) observe the transfer characteristic on a CRO.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "See-Saw Judge"

Imagine two children sitting on either end of a see-saw, holding weights of different sizes. The see-saw is so sensitive (extremely long pivot arms) that even a **single extra gram** on one side makes that side slam to the ground.

- The **left-side child** = input applied to the **inverting (−)** terminal.
- The **right-side child** = input applied to the **non-inverting (+)** terminal.
- The **ground** on the right = output saturates to $+V_{sat}$.
- The **ground** on the left = output saturates to $-V_{sat}$.
- The **pivot balance point** = the **reference / threshold voltage**.

The op-amp acts as a *binary judge* — it does not give a smooth, proportional answer; it only shouts **"YES, you are larger!"** ($+V_{sat}$) or **"NO, you are smaller!"** ($-V_{sat}$).

### 1.3 Why Comparators Matter in Engineering

| Application Area | Role of Comparator |
|---|---|
| **Analog-to-Digital Converters (ADCs)** | First stage that decides "is $V_{in}$ above or below $V_{ref}$?" |
| **Zero-Crossing Detectors** | Detects AC mains frequency for triac firing |
| **Oscilloscope Trigger Circuits** | Starts a sweep when signal crosses threshold |
| **Battery Voltage Monitors** | Alerts when voltage drops below safe level |
| **Over-Current / Over-Voltage Protection** | Compares sensor output with safe limit |
| **Pulse-Width Modulators (PWM)** | Combines comparator with ramp waveform |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Examiners frequently ask students to *draw the transfer characteristic* and *state the threshold equation*. Memorize $V_{TH} = V_{ref} \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$ for the Schmitt trigger — it appears in nearly every ESE paper.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Open-Loop Op-Amp Model

When used as a comparator, the op-amp operates **without negative feedback**. The output equation in the linear region would be:

$$V_{out} = A_{OL}\,(V_{in}^+ - V_{in}^-)$$

However, because $A_{OL}$ is enormous (typically **$2 \times 10^5$ V/V** for a 741), any input difference greater than about **$50\,\mu V$** saturates the output. The comparator therefore has only **two stable states** — there is no linear operating region in practice.

### 2.2 The Two Canonical Comparator Topologies

**(A) Non-Inverting Comparator (Basic Comparator)**

Signal $V_{in}$ goes to the non-inverting (+) input, reference $V_{ref}$ goes to the inverting (−) input.

$$V_{out} = +V_{sat} \quad \text{if } V_{in} > V_{ref}$$

$$V_{out} = -V_{sat} \quad \text{if } V_{in} < V_{ref}$$

Threshold: $V_{TH} = V_{ref}$ (any DC value, including $0\,\text{V}$).

**(B) Inverting Comparator**

Signal $V_{in}$ goes to the inverting (−) input, reference $V_{ref}$ goes to the non-inverting (+) input.

$$V_{out} = +V_{sat} \quad \text{if } V_{in} < V_{ref}$$

$$V_{out} = -V_{sat} \quad \text{if } V_{in} > V_{ref}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Zero-Crossing Detector** is just a special inverting/non-inverting comparator with $V_{ref} = 0\,\text{V}$. The output flips state every time the input crosses zero.

### 2.3 The Schmitt Trigger — The Comparator With Hysteresis

A **basic comparator** suffers from a critical flaw: if $V_{in}$ contains noise near the threshold, the output **chatters** rapidly between $+V_{sat}$ and $-V_{sat}$. This destroys the digital signal integrity.

The **Schmitt trigger** solves this by feeding a fraction of the **output** back to the **non-inverting input** through a resistor divider. The threshold itself now depends on the output state, producing **two different thresholds**:

- **Upper Threshold Voltage (UTP)** — the input level required to switch the output from $+V_{sat}$ to $-V_{sat}$.
- **Lower Threshold Voltage (LTP)** — the input level required to switch the output from $-V_{sat}$ to $+V_{sat}$.

The gap between UTP and LTP is called the **Hysteresis Width** $V_H$.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Condition / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Open-loop gain (saturated) | $V_{out} = A_{OL}(V^+ - V^-)$ | $A_{OL} \to \infty$ in saturation |
| 2 | Positive saturation | $+V_{sat} \approx +V_{CC} - 1.5\,\text{V}$ | For 741 with $\pm 15\,\text{V}$ rails |
| 3 | Negative saturation | $-V_{sat} \approx -V_{EE} + 1.5\,\text{V}$ | For 741 with $\pm 15\,\text{V}$ rails |
| 4 | Non-inverting comparator threshold | $V_{TH} = V_{ref}$ | volts (V) |
| 5 | Inverting Schmitt UTP | $V_{UTP} = \dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} V_{ref} + \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}(+V_{sat})$ | V |
| 6 | Inverting Schmitt LTP | $V_{LTP} = \dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} V_{ref} + \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}(-V_{sat})$ | V |
| 7 | Hysteresis width | $V_H = V_{UTP} - V_{LTP} = \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}(+V_{sat} - (-V_{sat}))$ | V |
| 8 | Schmitt UTP (with $V_{ref} = 0$) | $V_{UTP} = +\dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} V_{sat}$ | V |
| 9 | Schmitt LTP (with $V_{ref} = 0$) | $V_{LTP} = -\dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} V_{sat}$ | V |
| 10 | Inverting Schmitt threshold (compact) | $V_{TH}^{\pm} = \pm \beta V_{sat}$, where $\beta = \dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}$ | dimensionless $\beta$ |
| 11 | Non-inverting Schmitt UTP | $V_{UTP} = V_{ref} \left(1 + \dfrac{R_1}{R_2}\right) - V_{sat} \dfrac{R_1}{R_2}$ | V |
| 12 | Non-inverting Schmitt LTP | $V_{LTP} = V_{ref} \left(1 + \dfrac{R_1}{R_2}\right) + V_{sat} \dfrac{R_1}{R_2}$ | V |
| 13 | Window comparator lower trip | $V_{L} = V_{ref}^L$ | set by lower reference |
| 14 | Window comparator upper trip | $V_{H} = V_{ref}^H$ | set by upper reference |
| 15 | Propagation delay (LM311) | $t_{pd} \approx 200\,\text{ns}$ | ns |
| 16 | Response time (µA741 as comparator) | $t_{r} \approx 20\,\mu\text{s}$ | µs |

> [!NOTE]
> **Convention used in the lab manual:** The resistor from the **inverting input to ground** is labelled $R_1$, and the **feedback resistor** from output to non-inverting input is $R_2$. This is the *non-inverting Schmitt* convention. The compact $\beta$-formula is what examiners expect on the answer script.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility of the Schmitt Trigger

- **Debouncing mechanical switches** — a push-button input that bounces is "cleaned" into a clean digital edge.
- **Sine-to-square wave converter** — feeding a sine wave through a Schmitt trigger yields a square wave of the same frequency.
- **Relaxation oscillator (astable multivibrator)** — Schmitt trigger + RC timing network → clock generator.
- **Line receiver** in noisy industrial environments — recovers digital data from a noisy transmission line.
- **Battery low-voltage indicator** — the LED lights only when cell voltage falls below LTP.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Inverting Schmitt Trigger Threshold Voltages

**Circuit Setup:** The input $V_{in}$ is applied through $R_1$ to the **inverting (−)** terminal. A reference $V_{ref}$ is connected through $R_2$ to the **non-inverting (+)** terminal. A feedback resistor $R_f$ connects the output back to the (+) terminal. (We will simplify by using a single divider $R_1, R_2$ and direct output drive to the (+) node, which is the textbook KTU form.)

**Goal:** Find the input voltage at which the output switches.

**Step 1:** Apply KCL at the non-inverting input node (call it $V^+$).

$$\frac{V^+ - V_{ref}}{R_2} + \frac{V^+ - V_{out}}{R_f} = 0$$

**Step 2:** Solve for $V^+$.

$$V^+ \left(\frac{1}{R_2} + \frac{1}{R_f}\right) = \frac{V_{ref}}{R_2} + \frac{V_{out}}{R_f}$$

**Step 3:** Simplify by setting $R_f = R_1$ (standard KTU lab convention for symmetric hysteresis). Let $\beta = \dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}$.

$$V^+ = \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} V_{ref} + \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} V_{out}$$

**Step 4:** The output switches state when $V_{in} = V^+$. The output can only be $+V_{sat}$ or $-V_{sat}$. Therefore two cases arise:

**Case A** — output is currently at $+V_{sat}$:
$$V_{UTP} = \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} V_{ref} + \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}(+V_{sat})$$

**Case B** — output is currently at $-V_{sat}$:
$$V_{LTP} = \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} V_{ref} + \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}(-V_{sat})$$

**Step 5:** Subtract to get hysteresis width.

$$V_H = V_{UTP} - V_{LTP} = \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} \bigl[+V_{sat} - (-V_{sat})\bigr] = \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} (2V_{sat})$$

**Step 6 (Special case):** If $V_{ref} = 0\,\text{V}$, the equations collapse to the symmetric form:

$$V_{UTP} = +\frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} V_{sat}, \qquad V_{LTP} = -\frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} V_{sat}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** The factor $\dfrac{R_2}{R_1+R_2}$ acts as the **positive feedback fraction** $\beta$. A larger $R_2$ means stronger feedback and a *wider* hysteresis window — which means more noise immunity, but slower response.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Non-Inverting Schmitt Trigger Threshold Voltages

**Circuit Setup:** The input $V_{in}$ is applied to the **non-inverting (+)** terminal through $R_1$. The output is fed back to the **inverting (−)** terminal through a divider $R_2$–$R_3$.

**Step 1:** By superposition at the inverting node, the voltage there is:

$$V^- = \frac{R_3}{R_2 + R_3} V_{in} + \frac{R_2}{R_2 + R_3} V_{out}$$

**Step 2:** Switching occurs when $V_{in} = V^-$. Two cases:

**Case A** — Output is $+V_{sat}$:
$$V_{UTP} = V_{ref,\,eq} + \beta' V_{sat} \quad \text{where } \beta' = \frac{R_2}{R_3}$$

**Case B** — Output is $-V_{sat}$:
$$V_{LTP} = V_{ref,\,eq} - \beta' V_{sat}$$

**Step 3:** With $V_{ref} = 0\,\text{V}$ (input grounded through a single path), the simplified KTU form is:

$$V_{UTP} = +\frac{R_2}{R_1} V_{sat}, \qquad V_{LTP} = -\frac{R_2}{R_1} V_{sat}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Transfer Characteristic Slope

For a basic comparator, the theoretical slope of the line connecting $(-V_{sat}, V_{ref})$ to $(+V_{sat}, V_{ref})$ is **infinite** (vertical jump). The transfer curve is therefore drawn as:

- A **horizontal line at $-V_{sat}$** for $V_{in} < V_{ref}$.
- A **horizontal line at $+V_{sat}$** for $V_{in} > V_{ref}$.
- A **vertical jump at $V_{in} = V_{ref}$**.

For a **Schmitt trigger**, the transfer curve traces a **rectangular hysteresis loop**. Starting from $V_{in} \ll 0$ (output at $-V_{sat}$):

- Output remains $-V_{sat}$ as $V_{in}$ rises, **until $V_{in} = V_{UTP}$** → output jumps to $+V_{sat}$.
- Output stays $+V_{sat}$ as $V_{in}$ falls, **until $V_{in} = V_{LTP}$** → output drops back to $-V_{sat}$.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Basic Comparator | Inverting Schmitt | Non-Inverting Schmitt | Window Comparator |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Feedback type | None (open loop) | Positive (from output) | Positive (to inverting) | Dual op-amp + AND gate |
| Number of thresholds | 1 ($V_{ref}$) | 2 (UTP, LTP) | 2 (UTP, LTP) | 2 ($V_{L}, V_{H}$) |
| Noise immunity | **Poor** — chatters near $V_{ref}$ | **Excellent** | **Excellent** | **Excellent** |
| Hysteresis width | 0 | $\dfrac{R_2}{R_1+R_2}(2V_{sat})$ | $\dfrac{R_2}{R_1}(2V_{sat})$ | $V_H - V_L$ |
| Output for $V_{in} = 0$ | Undefined | $+V_{sat}$ or $-V_{sat}$ (depends on history) | $+V_{sat}$ or $-V_{sat}$ (depends on history) | $+V_{sat}$ if $V_L < V_{in} < V_H$ |
| Output when $V_{in}$ outside window | — | — | — | $-V_{sat}$ |
| Primary application | Threshold detection | Debouncing, square-wave gen | Sine-to-square, line receiver | Over/under voltage monitor |

> [!TIP]
> **Quick decision rule for the lab exam:** If the question mentions *noise* or *chatter*, it is asking for a **Schmitt trigger**, not a basic comparator. Draw the hysteresis loop explicitly — examiners award full marks only when both UTP and LTP arrows are shown on the transfer curve.

### 3.5 Numerical Worked Example

**Problem (KTU Style):** Design an inverting Schmitt trigger using a 741 op-amp powered by $\pm 12\,\text{V}$ rails. The required upper threshold is $+4\,\text{V}$ and the lower threshold is $-4\,\text{V}$. $V_{ref} = 0\,\text{V}$. Find $R_1$ and $R_2$ if the input resistor is $R_{in} = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$.

**Step 1:** Assume $V_{sat} \approx V_{CC} - 1.5 = 10.5\,\text{V}$ (typical lab value for 741 with $\pm 12\,\text{V}$ supply).

**Step 2:** Apply the symmetric threshold formula.

$$V_{UTP} = +\frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} V_{sat} = +4\,\text{V}$$

**Step 3:** Solve for the ratio.

$$\frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = \frac{4}{10.5} = 0.381$$

**Step 4:** Cross-multiply.

$$R_2 = 0.381(R_1 + R_2) \;\Rightarrow\; R_2(1 - 0.381) = 0.381 R_1$$

$$0.619 R_2 = 0.381 R_1 \;\Rightarrow\; \frac{R_2}{R_1} = \frac{0.381}{0.619} = 0.6155$$

**Step 5:** Choose $R_1 = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$ (the problem says input resistor is $10\,\text{k}\Omega$, which doubles as $R_1$ in this topology).

$$R_2 = 0.6155 \times 10\,\text{k}\Omega = 6.155\,\text{k}\Omega$$

**Step 6:** Round to nearest standard E12 value: $R_2 \approx 6.2\,\text{k}\Omega$.

**Verification:**
$$V_{UTP} = \frac{6.2}{10 + 6.2} \times 10.5 = 0.383 \times 10.5 = 4.02\,\text{V} \;\checkmark$$

$$V_{LTP} = -4.02\,\text{V} \;\checkmark$$

$$V_H = 2 \times 4.02 = 8.04\,\text{V}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Architecture — Comparator Family Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["Op-Amp Comparator Family"]
    nodeA --> nodeB["Basic Open-Loop Comparator"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["Schmitt Trigger with Hysteresis"]
    nodeA --> nodeD["Window Comparator"]
    nodeB --> nodeB1["Non-Inverting Variant"]
    nodeB --> nodeB2["Inverting Variant"]
    nodeB --> nodeB3["Zero Crossing Detector"]
    nodeC --> nodeC1["Inverting Schmitt"]
    nodeC --> nodeC2["Non-Inverting Schmitt"]
    nodeD --> nodeD1["Dual Threshold Detector"]
    nodeD --> nodeD2["Over Under Voltage Monitor"]
    nodeB1 --> appA["Threshold Sensing"]
    nodeB2 --> appB["Zero Crossing Detection"]
    nodeB3 --> appC["Phase Locked Loops"]
    nodeC1 --> appD["Square Wave Generation"]
    nodeC2 --> appE["Signal Conditioning"]
    nodeD1 --> appF["ADC Front End"]
    nodeD2 --> appG["Battery Management Systems"]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Inverting Schmitt Trigger Functional Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    inp["Input Voltage Vin"] --> resA["Resistor R1"]
    resA --> inv["Inverting Input Minus"]
    refA["Reference Vref"] --> resB["Resistor R2"]
    resB --> noninv["Non Inverting Input Plus"]
    outa["Output Node Vout"] --> fb["Feedback Resistor Rf"]
    fb --> noninv
    inv --> opamp["Op Amp 741 Core"]
    opamp --> outa
    outa --> sat1["Saturates to plus Vsat when Vin exceeds UTP"]
    outa --> sat2["Saturates to minus Vsat when Vin falls below LTP"]
```

### 4.3 Transfer Characteristic — Schmitt Hysteresis Loop

```mermaid
graph TD
    s1["Vin very negative. Output at minus Vsat"]
    s1 --> s2["Vin rises but Output remains minus Vsat until Vin equals LTP"]
    s2 --> s3["At Vin equals LTP, Output jumps vertically to plus Vsat"]
    s3 --> s4["Vin continues to rise. Output remains plus Vsat"]
    s4 --> s5["Vin falls. Output remains plus Vsat until Vin equals UTP"]
    s5 --> s6["At Vin equals UTP, Output jumps vertically to minus Vsat"]
    s6 --> s1
```

### 4.4 Functional Block Architecture — Window Comparator

```mermaid
graph TD
    vIn["Input Signal Vin"] --> split["Signal Splitter Node"]
    split --> opa["Op-Amp A: Compares Vin with Upper Reference Vref High"]
    split --> opb["Op-Amp B: Compares Vin with Lower Reference Vref Low"]
    opa --> oaOut["Output A: High only if Vin greater than Vref High"]
    opb --> obOut["Output B: High only if Vin less than Vref Low"]
    oaOut --> gate["AND Gate Logic Combination"]
    obOut --> gate
    gate --> final["Window Output: High only if Vref Low less than Vin less than Vref High"]
```

### 4.5 Lab Wiring Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    psu["DC Power Supply plus 12V minus 12V"] --> opamp["741 Op Amp on Breadboard"]
    fg["Function Generator 1kHz Sine Wave"] --> inpNode["Input Node"]
    inpNode --> rOne["R1 equals 10k"]
    rOne --> minus["Pin 2 Inverting Input"]
    ref["DC Reference Vref equals 0V from PSU"] --> rTwo["R2 equals 6.2k"]
    rTwo --> plus["Pin 3 Non Inverting Input"]
    pin6["Pin 6 Output"] --> fbRes["Feedback Resistor Rf equals 6.2k"]
    fbRes --> plus
    pin6 --> croCh1["CRO Channel 1 displays output square wave"]
    inpNode --> croCh2["CRO Channel 2 displays input sine wave"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lab Wiring Reminder:** When using a 741 IC, always place a **0.1 µF ceramic decoupling capacitor** between pins 7 ($V_{CC}$) and 4 ($V_{EE}$) as close to the IC as possible. This prevents high-frequency oscillation that can corrupt the comparator output. Many students lose marks in the lab viva for omitting this — examiners will physically check the breadboard.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024] Define a comparator. Why is the op-amp operated in open-loop configuration for comparator applications?**

**Model Answer:**
A comparator is a circuit that compares two input voltages and produces a binary output indicating which one is larger.
