The op-amp is used in open-loop configuration because the open-loop gain $A_{OL}$ is extremely high (typically $\geq 10^5$), so any minute difference between the two inputs drives the output to one of the two saturation levels ($+V_{sat}$ or $-V_{sat}$). The closed-loop linear region is therefore bypassed, yielding a hard-switching two-level output suitable for digital interfacing.

**(3 marks: 1 mark definition + 1 mark open-loop gain concept + 1 mark saturation reasoning)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] What is hysteresis in the context of a Schmitt trigger? Why is it necessary?**

**Model Answer:**
Hysteresis is the deliberate introduction of **two different switching thresholds** (UTP and LTP) for the rising and falling transitions of the input signal. It is necessary because a basic comparator with a single threshold produces multiple unwanted output transitions (*chattering*) when the input signal contains noise near the threshold. The hysteresis gap $V_H = V_{UTP} - V_{LTP}$ must be chosen wider than the peak noise amplitude to ensure clean, single-edge digital output transitions.

**(3 marks: 1 mark definition of UTP/LTP + 1 mark noise immunity + 1 mark chatter explanation)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** When asked to draw the transfer characteristic, students often draw it as a *single line* (incorrect for Schmitt) or forget to mark the **direction of traversal** with arrows. Always annotate with: (i) UTP and LTP on the X-axis, (ii) $+V_{sat}$ and $-V_{sat}$ on the Y-axis, (iii) clockwise arrows showing the hysteresis loop direction. Marks are deducted for missing any one of these three elements.

---

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Inverting Schmitt Trigger Design

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 2, Part B]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, derive the expressions for the **Upper Threshold Voltage (UTP)** and **Lower Threshold Voltage (LTP)** of an inverting Schmitt trigger using an op-amp. Assume the reference voltage $V_{ref}$ is applied to the non-inverting input through $R_2$, and a feedback resistor $R_f$ is connected from the output to the non-inverting input. The input signal $V_{in}$ is applied to the inverting terminal. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design an inverting Schmitt trigger with $V_{UTP} = +3\,\text{V}$ and $V_{LTP} = -3\,\text{V}$ using a 741 op-amp powered by $\pm 15\,\text{V}$. Take $V_{ref} = 0\,\text{V}$ and $R_1 = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$. Calculate the hysteresis width and the feedback resistor. Draw the transfer characteristic. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q.A(a) — Derivation of UTP and LTP (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Circuit Description (1 mark):**
Draw the inverting Schmitt trigger: input $V_{in}$ through $R_1$ to the inverting (−) terminal; reference $V_{ref}$ through $R_2$ to the non-inverting (+) terminal; feedback resistor $R_f$ from output to (+) terminal. Identify $V_{out}$ as either $+V_{sat}$ or $-V_{sat}$.

**Step 2 — KCL at the Non-Inverting Node (2 marks):**

$$\frac{V^+ - V_{ref}}{R_2} + \frac{V^+ - V_{out}}{R_f} = 0$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $V^+$ (2 marks):**

$$V^+ = \frac{R_f V_{ref} + R_2 V_{out}}{R_2 + R_f}$$

Setting $R_f = R_1$ (symmetric design), the formula becomes:

$$V^+ = \frac{R_1 V_{ref} + R_2 V_{out}}{R_1 + R_2}$$

**Step 4 — Switching Condition (1 mark):**
The output flips state when $V_{in} = V^+$. Therefore two cases give the two thresholds.

**Step 5 — Final Expressions (1 mark):**

$$V_{UTP} = \frac{R_1 V_{ref} + R_2 (+V_{sat})}{R_1 + R_2}, \qquad V_{LTP} = \frac{R_1 V_{ref} + R_2 (-V_{sat})}{R_1 + R_2}$$

---

**Model Solution to Q.A(b) — Numerical Design (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Identify $V_{sat}$ (1 mark):**
For a 741 with $\pm 15\,\text{V}$ rails, $V_{sat} \approx V_{CC} - 1.5 = 13.5\,\text{V}$.

**Step 2 — Apply the Symmetric Threshold Formula with $V_{ref} = 0$ (2 marks):**

$$V_{UTP} = +\frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} V_{sat} = 3\,\text{V}$$

$$\frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = \frac{3}{13.5} = 0.2222$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $R_2$ (2 marks):**

$$R_2 = 0.2222(R_1 + R_2) \;\Rightarrow\; 0.7778 R_2 = 0.2222 R_1$$

$$R_2 = \frac{0.2222}{0.7778} R_1 = 0.2857 \times 10\,\text{k}\Omega = 2.857\,\text{k}\Omega$$

Nearest E12 standard value: $R_2 \approx 2.7\,\text{k}\Omega$ (or use $2.8\,\text{k}\Omega$ from E24 series).

**Step 4 — Hysteresis Width and Transfer Characteristic (2 marks):**

$$V_H = V_{UTP} - V_{LTP} = 3 - (-3) = 6\,\text{V}$$

The transfer characteristic is a rectangular hysteresis loop with corners at $(V_{LTP}, +V_{sat}) = (-3, +13.5)$, $(V_{UTP}, +V_{sat}) = (+3, +13.5)$, $(V_{UTP}, -V_{sat}) = (+3, -13.5)$, and $(V_{LTP}, -V_{sat}) = (-3, -13.5)$, traversed **clockwise** as $V_{in}$ increases then decreases.

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Non-Inverting Schmitt Trigger

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2, Part B, Alternative]**

**(a)** Explain with a circuit diagram the operation of a **non-inverting Schmitt trigger**. Derive the expressions for UTP and LTP. Compare its transfer characteristic with that of the inverting Schmitt trigger. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A non-inverting Schmitt trigger is built using a 741 op-amp ($\pm 12\,\text{V}$ supply) with $R_1 = 5\,\text{k}\Omega$ and $R_2 = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$ and $V_{ref} = 0\,\text{V}$. Calculate the UTP, LTP, and hysteresis width. If a 1 kHz sine wave of peak amplitude $5\,\text{V}$ is applied, sketch the input and output waveforms showing the switching instants. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q.B(a) — Non-Inverting Schmitt Trigger (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Circuit Diagram (1 mark):**
Draw the circuit: input $V_{in}$ → non-inverting (+) terminal through $R_1$; output fed back to inverting (−) terminal through a divider $R_2$–$R_3$.

**Step 2 — Voltage at the Inverting Node (2 marks):**
By superposition:

$$V^- = \frac{R_3 V_{in} + R_2 V_{out}}{R_2 + R_3}$$

**Step 3 — Switching Condition $V_{in} = V^-$ (2 marks):**
Setting $V_{in} = V^-$ and solving for the two cases $V_{out} = \pm V_{sat}$:

$$V_{UTP} = V_{ref,\,eq} + \frac{R_2}{R_3} V_{sat}, \qquad V_{LTP} = V_{ref,\,eq} - \frac{R_2}{R_3} V_{sat}$$

With the simplified KTU form ($V_{ref} = 0$, $R_1$ as the input series resistor):

$$V_{UTP} = +\frac{R_2}{R_1} V_{sat}, \qquad V_{LTP} = -\frac{R_2}{R_1} V_{sat}$$

**Step 4 — Comparison with Inverting Topology (2 marks):**

| Feature | Inverting Schmitt | Non-Inverting Schmitt |
|---|---|---|
| Input applied to | Inverting (−) terminal | Non-inverting (+) terminal |
| Phase relationship | Output inverts during transition | Output inverts during transition |
| UTP/LTP formula | $\pm \dfrac{R_2}{R_1+R_2} V_{sat}$ | $\pm \dfrac{R_2}{R_1} V_{sat}$ |
| Loop direction | Anti-clockwise on $V_{in}$–$V_{out}$ plane | Clockwise on $V_{in}$–$V_{out}$ plane |

---

**Model Solution to Q.B(b) — Numerical Evaluation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Identify $V_{sat}$ (1 mark):**
For 741 with $\pm 12\,\text{V}$ supply, $V_{sat} \approx 10.5\,\text{V}$.

**Step 2 — Compute UTP and LTP (2 marks):**

$$V_{UTP} = +\frac{R_2}{R_1} V_{sat} = \frac{10\,\text{k}\Omega}{5\,\text{k}\Omega} \times 10.5 = 2 \times 10.5 = +21\,\text{V}$$

This exceeds the supply rail, so the op-amp saturates *immediately* on the first cycle. **Practical fix:** choose $R_2 < R_1$. Assume the design is re-evaluated with $R_1 = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$ and $R_2 = 2\,\text{k}\Omega$:

$$V_{UTP} = +\frac{2}{10} \times 10.5 = +2.1\,\text{V}, \qquad V_{LTP} = -2.1\,\text{V}$$

**Step 3 — Hysteresis Width (1 mark):**

$$V_H = V_{UTP} - V_{LTP} = 2.1 - (-2.1) = 4.2\,\text{V}$$

**Step 4 — Switching Instants for 5 V Peak Sine Wave (3 marks):**
Let $V_{in}(t) = 5 \sin(2\pi \cdot 1000 \cdot t)\,\text{V}$. Switching occurs when $V_{in} = \pm 2.1\,\text{V}$.

$$t_{\text{rise}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \cdot 1000} \arcsin\!\left(\frac{2.1}{5}\right) = \frac{1}{6283} \times 0.4300\,\text{rad} = 68.4\,\mu\text{s}$$

$$t_{\text{fall}} = \frac{1}{2000} - 68.4\,\mu\text{s} = 500 - 68.4 = 431.6\,\mu\text{s}$$

The output is a **non-symmetric square wave** at 1 kHz that switches low at $t \approx 68.4\,\mu\text{s}$ and switches high at $t \approx 431.6\,\mu\text{s}$, with the high-time significantly longer than the low-time because the input sine wave spends more time above $+2.1\,\text{V}$ than it takes to traverse from $+2.1$ to $-2.1$ (and back). This illustrates **pulse-width modulation by amplitude variation** — an important concept for KTU viva questions.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This Before the Exam**

- **Basic Comparator:** Single threshold = $V_{ref}$; no feedback; output chatters in presence of noise.
- **Inverting Schmitt:** Input on (−) terminal; UTP/LTP depend on $V_{out}$ state via positive feedback; $V_{UTP} = +\beta V_{sat}$, $V_{LTP} = -\beta V_{sat}$ when $V_{ref} = 0$, with $\beta = \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$.
- **Non-Inverting Schmitt:** Input on (+) terminal; thresholds are $\pm \dfrac{R_2}{R_1} V_{sat}$ in the symmetric case; loop traversed in opposite rotational direction.
- **Hysteresis Width:** $V_H = 2\beta V_{sat}$ (inverting) or $V_H = 2\dfrac{R_2}{R_1} V_{sat}$ (non-inverting).
- **Saturation Voltage:** $V_{sat} \approx V_{CC} - 1.5\,\text{V}$ for 741 — never use the full rail voltage in design calculations.
- **Zero-Crossing Detector:** Special case of comparator with $V_{ref} = 0\,\text{V}$.
- **Window Comparator:** Uses two op-amps + AND gate to detect when input lies *between* two reference voltages.
- **Noise Immunity Rule:** Choose $V_H$ greater than the peak-to-peak noise amplitude on the input signal.
- **Decoupling Capacitor:** Always place $0.1\,\mu\text{F}$ between $V_{CC}$ and $V_{EE}$ pins of the 741, close to the IC.
- **Output High Time vs Low Time:** With an asymmetric input (e.g., sine), the high and low portions of the comparator output are not equal — used for pulse-width modulation.
- **Common Board Mistake:** Forgetting to mark both UTP and LTP on the X-axis of the transfer curve (only one is drawn). Examiners specifically check this.
- **KTU Viva Favorite:** "What happens if you remove the feedback resistor in a Schmitt trigger?" — Answer: It becomes a basic comparator and loses noise immunity.
- **741 vs Dedicated Comparators (LM311):** 741 has propagation delay $\sim 20\,\mu\text{s}$; LM311 has $\sim 200\,\text{ns}$. For high-speed applications, use a dedicated comparator IC.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of Waveform Generators (Triangular and Sawtooth)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICL408 - ANALOG CIRCUITS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Design of Waveform Generators (Triangular and Sawtooth)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Waveform Generator** is an electronic circuit that produces a periodic, non-sinusoidal output signal (such as square, triangular, or sawtooth waves) without requiring any external input signal other than a DC power supply. In the context of the KTU 2024 Analog Circuits Lab, the **Triangular Wave Generator** and the **Sawtooth Wave Generator** are constructed using two cascaded op-amp stages: a **Schmitt Trigger (regenerative comparator)** followed by an **Integrator**.

- A **Triangular Wave Generator** produces a symmetric output waveform whose amplitude rises and falls linearly at equal rates, yielding equal positive and negative ramp durations.
- A **Sawtooth Wave Generator** produces an *asymmetric* triangular waveform, where the rising and falling ramps occur at significantly different rates, producing a slow ramp followed by a fast flyback (or vice versa).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Concept:** Both circuits are essentially the *same fundamental topology*. The Sawtooth generator is simply a **modified** Triangular generator in which the charging and discharging paths of the integrator capacitor are made deliberately unequal — typically by introducing a **diode steering network** or a **transistor shunt** across the integrator's input resistor.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of waveform generation like **filling and emptying a bucket of water**:

- A **triangular wave** is like a bucket being filled at a perfectly steady rate until it reaches a high mark, then emptied at the *same steady rate* down to a low mark, then filled again — symmetric in time.
- A **sawtooth wave** is like a bucket that fills very slowly for a long time, but the moment it hits the high mark, someone **tips it over instantly** and it empties in a flash — the up-time is much longer than the down-time.

The Schmitt Trigger acts as the **"judge"** that monitors the water level and flips a switch the instant the level crosses a threshold, and the Integrator acts as the **"filling mechanism"** that raises or lowers the level linearly with time.

## 1.3 Standard Operating Parameters (KTU Bench Setup)

> [!NOTE]
> **Typical Lab Bench Constants for the Design Exercise:**
> - **Op-amp IC:** µA741 / LM741 (single supply dual ±12 V or ±15 V)
> - **Saturation Voltage (Vsat):** ≈ **±13 V** for ±15 V supply; ≈ **±10 V** for ±12 V supply
> - **Standard Component Tolerances:** Resistors 5 % (E24 series), Capacitors 10 % (ceramic/electrolytic)
> - **Required Frequency Range:** 100 Hz to 10 kHz (typical KTU 2024 module prescription)
> - **Output Amplitude:** 4 V to 8 V peak-to-peak

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Circuit Topology — The Building Blocks

The waveform generator is constructed using **two op-amp sub-circuits** connected in a closed loop:

### Sub-Circuit 1: Non-Inverting Schmitt Trigger (Comparator with Hysteresis)

- An op-amp configured as a **regenerative comparator** (positive feedback through resistor R₂).
- The output is a **square wave** that swings between +Vsat and −Vsat.
- It has **two threshold (trigger) voltages**, defined as:
   - **Upper Threshold (VT+)** = +β · Vsat
   - **Lower Threshold (VT−)** = −β · Vsat
- where **β** is the **positive feedback fraction**, given by **β = R₁ / (R₁ + R₂)**.
- This creates a **hysteresis window** of width **2β·Vsat**, which prevents false triggering due to noise.

### Sub-Circuit 2: Miller Integrator (Lossy / Ideal)

- An op-amp integrator that converts the square wave from the Schmitt trigger into a linear ramp.
- For an **ideal integrator** (no R across the capacitor), the output is a perfect ramp.
- The integrator's output is fed back to the Schmitt trigger's input, **closing the regenerative loop**.

> [!NOTE]
> **The fundamental closed-loop principle:** The Schmitt trigger *decides* when to flip; the integrator *executes* the linear ramp between flips. The two thresholds of the Schmitt trigger set the **peak amplitude** of the triangle, while the integrator's **R·C time constant** sets the **frequency**.

## 2.2 Why Both Waveforms Coexist

Because the Schmitt trigger produces a square wave and the integrator produces a triangle wave, the circuit is actually a **dual-output function generator** — you get:
- **Square wave** at the output of the Schmitt trigger (Op-amp 1 output).
- **Triangular wave** at the output of the Integrator (Op-amp 2 output).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Symbol | Formula | Unit / Notes |
|---|-----------|--------|---------|--------------|
| 1 | Feedback fraction | $\beta$ | $\beta = \dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}$ | Dimensionless, 0 < β < 1 |
| 2 | Upper threshold | $V_{T+}$ | $V_{T+} = +\beta \cdot V_{sat}$ | Volts (V) |
| 3 | Lower threshold | $V_{T-}$ | $V_{T-} = -\beta \cdot V_{sat}$ | Volts (V) |
| 4 | Peak triangular amplitude | $V_{p}$ | $V_{p} = \beta \cdot V_{sat}$ | Volts (V) |
| 5 | Period of triangular wave | $T$ | $T = \dfrac{4 R C R_1}{R_2}$ | Seconds (s) |
| 6 | Frequency of triangular wave | $f$ | $f = \dfrac{R_2}{4 R C R_1}$ | Hertz (Hz) |
| 7 | Symmetric triangular condition | — | If $R_1 = R_2$, then $f = \dfrac{1}{4RC}$ | Hz |
| 8 | Integrator slope (rising) | $\dfrac{dV_o}{dt}$ | $\dfrac{dV_o}{dt} = \dfrac{V_{sat}}{R \cdot C}$ | V/s |
| 9 | Sawtooth asymmetry factor | $k$ | $k = \dfrac{T_{ramp}}{T_{flyback}} = \dfrac{R_{charge}}{R_{discharge}}$ | Dimensionless |
| 10 | Sawtooth frequency | $f_{saw}$ | $f_{saw} = \dfrac{1}{T_{ramp} + T_{flyback}}$ | Hz |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MNEMONIC FOR EXAM:** **"Beta sets the peaks; R·C sets the speed"** — the feedback fraction β dictates the **amplitude** of the triangular wave, while the integrator's R·C time constant dictates the **frequency**.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Function Generators (Lab Equipment):** Every commercial function generator (Tektronix, Keysight, GW Instek) uses this exact op-amp topology as the analog front-end before digital synthesis.
- **Time-Base Generators in Oscilloscopes / CRTs:** Sawtooth waves drive the horizontal sweep in older CRT displays and time-base circuits.
- **Pulse-Width Modulation (PWM):** Sawtooth carriers are compared with message signals to generate PWM in Class-D audio amplifiers and motor drives.
- **Music Synthesis:** The sawtooth is one of the most harmonic-rich waveforms and is used as a building block in analog synthesizers (Moog, ARP).
- **ADC / Sample-and-Hold Circuits:** Triangular waves are used in dual-slope integrating ADCs for high-resolution digital voltmeters.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Frequency Formula (Symmetric Triangular Wave)

Let the Schmitt trigger output be $V_{sq}(t)$, which alternates between $+V_{sat}$ and $-V_{sat}$.

The integrator output $V_o(t)$ is given by the standard inverting integrator equation:

$$V_o(t) = -\dfrac{1}{R C} \int V_{sq}(t) \, dt$$

**Step 1 — Assume the Schmitt output is at $+V_{sat}$ for the first half-period $T/2$.**

The integrator output ramps downward (inverting) at a constant slope:

$$\dfrac{dV_o}{dt} = -\dfrac{V_{sat}}{R C}$$

**Step 2 — During this half-period, the output moves from $+V_{T+}$ to $-V_{T-}$.**

The total voltage swing is:

$$\Delta V_o = V_{T+} - V_{T-} = 2 \beta V_{sat}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the kinematic relationship $\Delta V_o = \text{slope} \times \text{time}$.**

$$2 \beta V_{sat} = \dfrac{V_{sat}}{R C} \cdot \dfrac{T}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Cancel $V_{sat}$ from both sides and solve for $T$.**

$$2 \beta = \dfrac{T}{2 R C}$$

$$T = 4 \beta R C = 4 \cdot \dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} \cdot R C$$

**Step 5 — Convert to frequency.**

$$f = \dfrac{1}{T} = \dfrac{R_1 + R_2}{4 R C R_1}$$

> **Refinement using standard notation:** In most KTU textbooks the Schmitt resistors are labelled $R_1$ (to ground) and $R_2$ (in feedback). The formula is then equivalently written as:
> 
> $$f = \dfrac{R_2}{4 R C R_1}$$
> 
> where $R_1$ is connected from the non-inverting input to ground, and $R_2$ is the feedback resistor from output to non-inverting input.

## 3.2 Complete Worked Design Example (KTU Lab Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Problem (from KTU 2024 Lab Manual — typical exercise):**
> Design a triangular wave generator to produce a **1 kHz** signal with an **output amplitude of 5 V peak** using a **741 op-amp with ±15 V supply** (Vsat ≈ 13 V).

### Step 1: Compute the Required Feedback Fraction β

The peak amplitude of the triangular wave equals $\beta \cdot V_{sat}$:

$$\beta = \dfrac{V_p}{V_{sat}} = \dfrac{5 \text{ V}}{13 \text{ V}} \approx 0.385$$

### Step 2: Choose Schmitt Resistor Ratio

Let $R_1 = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$. Then:

$$\beta = \dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} \Rightarrow R_2 = R_1 \left(\dfrac{1}{\beta} - 1\right)$$

$$R_2 = 10 \text{ k}\Omega \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{0.385} - 1\right) \approx 10 \text{ k}\Omega \cdot 1.597 \approx 15.97 \text{ k}\Omega$$

Use the nearest E24 standard value: **$R_2 = 16 \text{ k}\Omega$**.

### Step 3: Choose Integrator R and C for f = 1 kHz

Using $f = \dfrac{1}{4RC}$ (symmetric choice $R_1 = R_2$ would re-tune β; here we keep the asymmetric resistor choice):

$$R C = \dfrac{1}{4 f} \cdot \dfrac{R_1 + R_2}{R_2} = \dfrac{1}{4 \cdot 1000} \cdot \dfrac{10\text{k} + 16\text{k}}{16\text{k}}$$

$$R C = 2.5 \times 10^{-4} \cdot 1.625 = 4.0625 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s}$$

### Step 4: Pick Standard Capacitor and Compute Resistor

Choose $C = 0.1 \mu\text{F} = 10^{-7} \text{ F}$ (a common lab value). Then:

$$R = \dfrac{4.0625 \times 10^{-4}}{10^{-7}} = 4062.5 \Omega$$

Use the nearest E24 standard value: **$R \approx 3.9 \text{ k}\Omega$** or **$4.7 \text{ k}\Omega$** (with 5 % tolerance — verify with the bench).

### Final Component List (Lab Bill of Materials)

| Component | Calculated Value | Selected (E24) | Purpose |
|-----------|------------------|----------------|---------|
| $R_1$ (Schmitt to GND) | 10 kΩ | **10 kΩ** | Sets feedback fraction |
| $R_2$ (Schmitt feedback) | 15.97 kΩ | **16 kΩ** | Sets feedback fraction |
| $R$ (Integrator input) | 4.06 kΩ | **3.9 kΩ** | Sets RC time constant |
| $C$ (Integrator feedback) | 0.1 µF | **0.1 µF** | Integrator capacitor |
| Op-amp 1 | 741 | µA741 / LM741 | Schmitt trigger |
| Op-amp 2 | 741 | µA741 / LM741 | Integrator |
| Supply | ±15 V | Dual DC | Powers both op-amps |

## 3.3 Sawtooth Wave Generator — Modification Analysis

To convert a symmetric triangular generator into a **sawtooth generator**, we introduce a **diode-resistor network** in parallel with the integrator resistor $R$.

### Method: Diode Steering

- Place a **diode D1 in series with a resistor R_d** across the integrator's input resistor R.
- During the **positive half of the square wave**, one diode conducts and the effective resistance is $R \parallel R_{d1}$ (small → fast slope).
- During the **negative half**, the diode is reverse-biased and only R conducts (large → slow slope).

### Resulting Waveform

- **Slow ramp** during one half (long charging time).
- **Fast flyback** during the other half (short discharging time).
- The ratio of the two times = ratio of the two effective R values.

$$T_{ramp} = 2 \beta R_{slow} C$$
$$T_{flyback} = 2 \beta R_{fast} C$$
$$f_{saw} = \dfrac{1}{T_{ramp} + T_{flyback}} = \dfrac{1}{2 \beta C (R_{slow} + R_{fast})}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Alternative Method (Transistor-based):** Replace the diode with a **BJT (e.g., BC547)** acting as a switch. The base is driven by the Schmitt output, so the transistor shorts out part of the integrator resistor during one half-cycle. This is preferred when finer control of the duty cycle is required (used in modern PWM chips like the SG3525).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow (Triangular Wave Generator)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[DC Supply plus or minus 15V] --> nodeB[Op-Amp 1: Non-Inverting Schmitt Trigger]
    nodeB -- Square Wave --> nodeC[Op-Amp 2: Miller Integrator]
    nodeC -- Triangular Wave --> nodeD[Output 2: Triangle Wave Terminal]
    nodeB -- Square Wave --> nodeE[Output 1: Square Wave Terminal]
    nodeC -- Feedback to Schmitt input --> nodeB
    nodeF[Input Reference: Ground 0V] --> nodeB
    nodeG[Resistor R1 to Ground] --> nodeB
    nodeH[Resistor R2 Feedback] --> nodeB
    nodeI[Resistor R Integrator Input] --> nodeC
    nodeJ[Capacitor C Integrator Feedback] --> nodeC
```

## 4.2 Closed-Loop Signal Flow & State Transition Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> StateHigh: Power ON Schmitt output is plus Vsat
    StateHigh: State A: Square wave is plus Vsat, Integrator ramps DOWN from plus Vp to minus Vp
    StateHigh --> StateLow: Vtri reaches minus Vp, lower threshold crossed
    StateLow: State B: Square wave is minus Vsat, Integrator ramps UP from minus Vp to plus Vp
    StateLow --> StateHigh: Vtri reaches plus Vp, upper threshold crossed
    StateLow --> [*]: Power OFF
```

## 4.3 Sawtooth Modification — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Op-Amp 1: Schmitt Trigger] -->|Square Wave| nodeB[Diode D1 plus Resistor Rd1 in parallel]
    nodeB -->|Fast path| nodeC[Op-Amp 2: Integrator with Capacitor C]
    nodeA -->|Square Wave| nodeD[Diode D2 plus Resistor Rd2 in parallel]
    nodeD -->|Slow path| nodeC
    nodeC -->|Sawtooth Wave| nodeE[Output: Sawtooth Terminal]
    nodeC -->|Feedback Path| nodeA
    nodeF[DC Supply plus or minus 15V] --> nodeA
    nodeF --> nodeC
```

## 4.4 Pin-Level Wiring Reference (µA741 8-Pin DIP)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeP1[Pin 1: Offset Null] --> nodeP2[Pin 2: Inverting Input A]
    nodeP3[Pin 3: Non-Inverting Input A] --> nodeP4[Pin 4: V minus 15V]
    nodeP5[Pin 5: Offset Null] --> nodeP6[Pin 6: Output A]
    nodeP7[Pin 7: V plus 15V] --> nodeP8[Pin 8: NC]
    nodeP2 -. connects to .- nodeB[Schmitt R2 feedback node]
    nodeP3 -. connects to .- nodeC[R1 to ground node]
    nodeP6 -. is square wave output .- nodeX[Drives integrator]
    nodeX --> nodeP2b[Pin 2 of Op-Amp 2 Integrator]
    nodeP3b[Pin 3 of Op-Amp 2 to ground via R]
    nodeP6b[Pin 6 of Op-Amp 2 is triangular output]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Pin 8 of µA741 is NC (No Connection)** — students often mistakenly wire it to ground or supply, which is harmless but pollutes the schematic. Examiners will deduct marks for this.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, May 2024]**
**Define a triangular wave generator. With a neat functional block diagram, explain how a triangular waveform is generated using op-amps.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A triangular wave generator is an oscillator circuit that produces a periodic output whose voltage varies linearly with time between two equal positive and negative peak levels.

It consists of two op-amp stages connected in a closed loop:
1. **Schmitt Trigger (Comparator with hysteresis):** Generates a square wave at its output, swinging between +Vsat and −Vsat. The threshold voltages are ±β·Vsat where β = R₁/(R₁+R₂).
2. **Integrator:** The square wave is applied to the input of an inverting op-amp integrator (with resistor R and capacitor C). The output of the integrator is a **linear ramp**.

The integrator output is fed back to the Schmitt trigger input. As the triangle crosses the upper threshold, the Schmitt output flips polarity, reversing the ramp direction. This produces a continuous **symmetric triangular waveform**. The output frequency is given by **f = (R₁+R₂)/(4RCR₁)**.

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark for block diagram, 1 mark for Schmitt function, 1 mark for integrator function and frequency equation.)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between a triangular wave generator and a sawtooth wave generator. How is the sawtooth waveform obtained from the basic triangular generator?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Triangular Generator | Sawtooth Generator |
|---------|----------------------|---------------------|
| Waveform symmetry | Symmetric (equal positive and negative ramp times) | Asymmetric (slow ramp + fast flyback) |
| Charging vs. discharging time | Equal | Unequal |
| Modification | None (basic circuit) | Diode or transistor network added |

The **sawtooth waveform** is obtained by modifying the integrator of the triangular generator so that its **time constant is different during the positive and negative half-cycles of the square wave input**. This is typically done by:
- Adding a **diode-resistor steering network** in parallel with the integrator's input resistor.
- The diode conducts during one half-cycle, producing a **fast slope** (low effective R), and is reverse-biased during the other half, producing a **slow slope** (high R).

The result is a waveform with a **gradual rise and a sudden drop** (or vice versa), which is the classic sawtooth shape used in time-base circuits and PWM.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Model, May 2024]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, **explain the working of a triangular wave generator using op-amps**. Derive the expression for its output frequency. (7 marks)

**(b)** **Design a triangular wave generator** to produce a **2 kHz** symmetric triangular wave with a **peak amplitude of 6 V** using **±15 V supply (Vsat = 13 V)** and a **µA741 op-amp**. Specify all component values. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Working Principle:**

The circuit consists of two op-amps (A1 and A2):
- **A1** is a **non-inverting Schmitt trigger** with resistors R₁ (to ground) and R₂ (feedback).
- **A2** is an **inverting integrator** with input resistor R and feedback capacitor C.

**Step 1 — Schmitt Trigger Action:**
The output of A1 is a square wave at ±Vsat. The non-inverting input sees a fraction β = R₁/(R₁+R₂) of this output, plus a contribution from the triangle output V_tri. The thresholds are:
$$V_{T+} = +\beta \cdot V_{sat}, \quad V_{T-} = -\beta \cdot V_{sat}$$

**Step 2 — Integrator Action:**
When V_sq = +Vsat, the integrator output ramps **down** with slope $-V_{sat}/(RC)$. When V_sq = −Vsat, it ramps **up** with slope $+V_{sat}/(RC)$.

**Step 3 — Loop Closure:**
The triangle output is fed back to the Schmitt input. As the triangle crosses the upper threshold, the Schmitt output flips, reversing the ramp direction, producing a continuous **triangular wave** at the output of A2.

**Derivation of Frequency:**

During half a period T/2, the triangle swings from +V_{T+} to −V_{T-}, a total change of $2\beta V_{sat}$. The slope magnitude is $V_{sat}/(RC)$. Equating:

$$2\beta V_{sat} = \dfrac{V_{sat}}{RC} \cdot \dfrac{T}{2}$$

$$T = 4\beta RC = 4 \cdot \dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} \cdot RC$$

$$\boxed{f = \dfrac{1}{T} = \dfrac{R_1 + R_2}{4 R C R_1}}$$

*(Mark split: 2 marks circuit, 2 marks working, 3 marks derivation.)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** f = 2 kHz, V_p = 6 V, V_sat = 13 V.

**Step 1: Compute β**

$$\beta = \dfrac{V_p}{V_{sat}} = \dfrac{6}{13} \approx 0.4615$$

**Step 2: Choose R₁ and compute R₂**

Select $R_1 = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$ (standard). Then:

$$R_2 = R_1 \left(\dfrac{1}{\beta} - 1\right) = 10 \text{ k}\Omega \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{0.4615} - 1\right) \approx 11.67 \text{ k}\Omega$$

Use standard: **R₂ = 12 kΩ**.

**Step 3: Compute RC for f = 2 kHz**

$$R C = \dfrac{1}{4 f} \cdot \dfrac{R_1 + R_2}{R_2} = \dfrac{1}{8000} \cdot \dfrac{10\text{k} + 12\text{k}}{12\text{k}}$$

$$R C = 1.25 \times 10^{-4} \cdot 1.833 = 2.2917 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s}$$

**Step 4: Choose C and compute R**

Choose $C = 0.01 \mu F = 10^{-8} \text{ F}$. Then:

$$R = \dfrac{2.2917 \times 10^{-4}}{10^{-8}} = 22.917 \text{ k}\Omega$$

Use standard: **R = 22 kΩ** (or 24 kΩ for slight safety margin).

**Final Design Values:**

| Component | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| R₁ | 10 kΩ |
| R₂ | 12 kΩ |
| R | 22 kΩ |
| C | 0.01 µF |
| Op-amp | µA741 |
| Supply | ±15 V |

*(Mark split: 1 mark β calculation, 2 marks R₂, 2 marks R·C, 1 mark C, 1 mark R.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Model, Dec 2023]**

**(a)** With the help of a circuit diagram, **explain the working of a sawtooth wave generator** using op-amps and a diode network. Derive the expressions for ramp time and flyback time. (7 marks)

**(b)** **Design a sawtooth wave generator** to produce an output with a **total period of 500 µs** and a **duty cycle (ramp time) of 80 %** using a 741 op-amp with ±12 V supply. The peak output amplitude must be **4 V**. Calculate all required component values. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Circuit Description:**

A sawtooth wave generator is a modification of the triangular wave generator. The integrator's input resistor R is **shunted by a diode D in series with a resistor R_d** (a steering network).

**Working:**

- When the Schmitt output is **+Vsat**, diode D is **forward-biased** (conducting). The effective input resistance becomes $R \parallel R_d$ (smaller), so the integrator charges/discharges the capacitor **quickly** → **fast ramp** (flyback).
- When the Schmitt output is **−Vsat**, diode D is **reverse-biased** (non-conducting). The integrator's input resistance is just R (larger), so the capacitor charges/discharges **slowly** → **slow ramp**.

**Ramp Time (Slow Slope):**

$$T_{ramp} = \dfrac{2 \beta V_{sat} \cdot R \cdot C}{V_{sat}} = 2 \beta R C$$

**Flyback Time (Fast Slope):**

$$T_{flyback} = 2 \beta (R \parallel R_d) \cdot C$$

**Period and Frequency:**

$$T = T_{ramp} + T_{flyback} = 2 \beta C \left[R + (R \parallel R_d)\right]$$

$$f_{saw} = \dfrac{1}{T}$$

**Duty Cycle:**

$$\text{Duty Cycle} = \dfrac{T_{ramp}}{T} \times 100 \% = \dfrac{R}{R + (R \parallel R_d)} \times 100 \%$$

*(Mark split: 2 marks circuit, 2 marks working, 3 marks derivations.)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** T = 500 µs, Duty cycle = 80 %, V_p = 4 V, V_sat = 10 V (for ±12 V supply).

**Step 1: Identify T_ramp and T_flyback**

$$T_{ramp} = 0.80 \times 500 \mu s = 400 \mu s$$
$$T_{flyback} = 0.20 \times 500 \mu s = 100 \mu s$$

**Step 2: Compute β**

$$\beta = \dfrac{V_p}{V_{sat}} = \dfrac{4}{10} = 0.4$$

**Step 3: Choose R₁ and R₂ for β = 0.4**

Let $R_1 = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$. Then:

$$R_2 = R_1 \left(\dfrac{1}{\beta} - 1\right) = 10 \text{ k}\Omega \cdot (2.5 - 1) = 15 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 4: Compute C and R for T_ramp**

$$T_{ramp} = 2 \beta R C \Rightarrow R C = \dfrac{T_{ramp}}{2 \beta} = \dfrac{400 \times 10^{-6}}{2 \times 0.4} = 5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s}$$

Choose $C = 0.1 \mu F = 10^{-7} \text{ F}$. Then:

$$R = \dfrac{5 \times 10^{-4}}{10^{-7}} = 5 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 5: Compute R_d for T_flyback**

$$T_{flyback} = 2 \beta (R \parallel R_d) C$$
$$R \parallel R_d = \dfrac{T_{flyback}}{2 \beta C} = \dfrac{100 \times 10^{-6}}{2 \times 0.4 \times 10^{-7}} = 1250 \Omega$$

Solve for R_d:

$$\dfrac{R \cdot R_d}{R + R_d} = 1250 \Rightarrow \dfrac{5000 \cdot R_d}{5000 + R_d} = 1250$$
$$5000 R_d = 1250 (5000 + R_d) = 6.25 \times 10^6 + 1250 R_d$$
$$3750 R_d = 6.25 \times 10^6 \Rightarrow R_d \approx 1666.67 \Omega$$

Use standard: **R_d = 1.5 kΩ** or **1.8 kΩ**.

**Final Component Values:**

| Component | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| R₁ | 10 kΩ |
| R₂ | 15 kΩ |
| R | 5 kΩ |
| C | 0.1 µF |
| R_d | 1.5 kΩ (or 1.8 kΩ) |
| Diode D | 1N4148 (signal diode) |
| Op-amp | µA741 |
| Supply | ±12 V |

*(Mark split: 1 mark time split, 1 mark β, 2 marks R₁/R₂, 2 marks R & C, 1 mark R_d.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting Vsat vs. supply voltage distinction:** Students write Vsat = 15 V for a ±15 V supply, but the 741 saturates at ≈ 13 V. This propagates into β and gives the wrong amplitude.
> 2. **Wrong feedback fraction formula:** Using $\beta = R_2/(R_1+R_2)$ instead of $R_1/(R_1+R_2)$ — verify the topology from your circuit diagram every time.
> 3. **Mislabeling Schmitt vs. integrator components:** R, C in the formula are the **integrator's** R and C, NOT the Schmitt resistors. A common, costly mistake.
> 4. **Using non-standard component values:** Examiners accept only E12/E24 series (10, 12, 15, 18, 22, 27, 33, ...). A value like "3.7 kΩ" will be marked down.
> 5. **No pin diagram or supply rails:** A KTU board answer without explicit +V and −V supply connections to pins 7 and 4 of the 741 is considered incomplete.
> 6. **Not mentioning that the square wave is also available as a free output** — this loses the 1-mark "dual output" highlight in the KTU 2024 marking scheme.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Core formula for symmetric triangular wave:** $f = \dfrac{1}{4RC}$ when $R_1 = R_2$.
- **General formula:** $f = \dfrac{R_2}{4 R C R_1}$ (or $\dfrac{R_1+R_2}{4RCR_1}$ — both are equivalent and depend on resistor labelling).
- **Peak amplitude:** $V_p = \beta \cdot V_{sat}$, where $\beta = \dfrac{R_1}{R_1+R_2}$.
- **Period:** $T = 4\beta RC$.
- **Two simultaneous outputs:** Square wave (Schmitt output) and triangular wave (Integrator output).
- **Sawtooth modification:** Add a **diode + resistor** in parallel with the integrator's R to make the charging/discharging rates unequal.
- **Sawtooth formula:** $T_{ramp} = 2\beta R C$ and $T_{flyback} = 2\beta (R \parallel R_d) C$.
- **Duty cycle of sawtooth:** $\dfrac{R}{R + (R \parallel R_d)} \times 100\%$.
- **Op-amp used in lab:** µA741 / LM741 with ±12 V or ±15 V dual supply.
- **741 Pin Map (memorize for the exam):** Pin 2 = Inverting input, Pin 3 = Non-inverting input, Pin 4 = V−, Pin 6 = Output, Pin 7 = V+, Pin 8 = NC.
- **Vsat value to use:** For ±15 V supply use **Vsat ≈ 13 V**; for ±12 V supply use **Vsat ≈ 10 V**. Never use the supply rail directly.
- **Standard component series to use in designs:** Resistors — E24 (5 % tolerance); Capacitors — sub-family of E24.
- **Output frequency range for the lab exercise:** 100 Hz to 10 kHz is typical; design accordingly.
- **Why feedback fraction matters:** β controls the **peak amplitude** of the triangle; R·C controls the **frequency** — they are **independent** design parameters (a key insight examiners test).
- **Astable multivibrator (using 555 timer) is NOT the same** as the op-amp triangular generator. The op-amp version offers better linearity, tunable symmetry, and dual outputs.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of Wien Bridge Oscillator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICL408 - ANALOG CIRCUITS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Design of Wien Bridge Oscillator

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Wien Bridge Oscillator** is a sinusoidal, low-frequency (audio frequency range) electronic oscillator that generates stable, low-distortion sine waves using a **non-inverting operational amplifier** configuration with a frequency-selective positive feedback network known as the **Wien Bridge**.

The feedback network is composed of a **series RC arm** and a **parallel RC arm**, while a **resistive voltage divider** forms the negative feedback loop. The frequency of oscillation is governed by the equality of the time constants of the series and parallel arms.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** A Wien Bridge Oscillator is an audio frequency oscillator using an op-amp (typically µA741) with two RC networks — a series RC and a parallel RC — connected in a bridge configuration. It satisfies the **Barkhausen Criterion** for sustained sinusoidal oscillations.

## 1.2 Frequency Range and Applications

| Parameter | Standard Value |
|---|---|
| Operating frequency range | **1 Hz to 1 MHz** |
| Typical laboratory design frequency | **1 kHz to 10 kHz** |
| Total Harmonic Distortion (THD) | Less than **0.1%** with amplitude stabilization |
| Output amplitude stability | Determined by non-linear gain control element |

## 1.3 Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are **pushing a child on a swing** in a playground. The swing oscillates back and forth at a particular natural rhythm. To keep the swing moving without dying out, you must give a small push **at exactly the right moment** (matching the swing's natural frequency) and with **exactly the right amount of energy** (matching the energy lost to friction).

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy Breakdown:**
> - The **swing** = The **Wien Bridge network** (it has a natural "preferred" frequency of oscillation)
> - The **periodic push** = The **op-amp's positive feedback** (must be in-phase with the output)
> - The **energy lost to friction** = Resistive losses in the circuit (must be replenished by amplifier gain)
> - **Pushing too hard** = Gain > 3 → waveform **clips** at the supply rails (distortion)
> - **Pushing too softly** = Gain < 3 → oscillations **die out** (damped decay)
> - **Pushing perfectly** = Gain = 3 → sustained, pure sine wave

In essence, the Wien Bridge Oscillator is a **"swing that pushes itself"** — the op-amp provides exactly enough energy, at exactly the right phase, to compensate for losses and keep the oscillation alive forever.

## 1.4 Classification of the Oscillator

- **Type:** Sinusoidal, RC-feedback oscillator (NOT LC-type)
- **Feedback topology:** Positive feedback for oscillation, Negative feedback for gain control
- **Quadrature component:** None (purely RC, no inductors needed)
- **Frequency stability:** Moderate (sufficient for audio applications)
- **Output waveform:** Sinusoidal, with low distortion when amplitude stabilization is used

## 1.5 Why a "Bridge"?

The name "Wien Bridge" originates from the **Max Wien** configuration (1938), which is a four-arm AC bridge:
- **Arm 1:** Series RC (Z₁)
- **Arm 2:** Parallel RC (Z₂)
- **Arm 3:** Pure resistor (R₁ in feedback)
- **Arm 4:** Pure resistor (R₂ in feedback)

When the bridge is **balanced** (Z₁·R₁ = Z₂·R₂), the output is at a null. In oscillator application, the bridge is operated **slightly off-balance** to provide a feedback factor β = 1/3 at the desired frequency.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Circuit Architecture and Block-Level Operation

The Wien Bridge Oscillator consists of two fundamental sub-blocks:

**Block 1 — Frequency-Selective Positive Feedback Network (Wien Bridge):**
- A series RC combination in one arm
- A parallel RC combination in the adjacent arm
- Together they form a **band-pass filter** that has maximum transmission (β_max = 1/3) at a single frequency f₀

**Block 2 — Non-Inverting Amplifier Stage:**
- Op-amp (µA741 or equivalent)
- Two feedback resistors R_f and R_1
- Closed-loop voltage gain A_CL = 1 + R_f/R_1
- The gain must be set to **A_CL ≥ 3** to start and sustain oscillations

## 2.2 Barkhausen Criterion — The Heart of Oscillation

For any sinusoidal oscillator to produce sustained oscillations, two conditions must be simultaneously satisfied:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Barkhausen Criterion (1921):**
> 1. **Loop Gain Magnitude Condition:** |A·β| = 1 (the magnitude of the loop gain must equal exactly unity)
> 2. **Loop Phase Condition:** ∠(A·β) = 0° or integer multiples of 360° (the total phase shift around the loop must be zero, ensuring positive feedback)

Where:
- **A** = Voltage gain of the amplifier stage
- **β** = Feedback factor of the frequency-selective network

## 2.3 Derivation of the Feedback Factor β

The Wien network consists of two impedances:

**Series arm impedance Z_s:**

$$Z_s = R + \frac{1}{j\omega C} = \frac{1 + j\omega RC}{j\omega C}$$

**Parallel arm impedance Z_p:**

$$Z_p = \frac{R \cdot \frac{1}{j\omega C}}{R + \frac{1}{j\omega C}} = \frac{R}{1 + j\omega RC}$$

The feedback factor (voltage divider ratio) is:

$$\beta = \frac{V_f}{V_o} = \frac{Z_p}{Z_s + Z_p}$$

Substituting and simplifying:

$$\beta(j\omega) = \frac{1}{3 + j\left(\omega RC - \frac{1}{\omega RC}\right)}$$

## 2.4 The Resonant / Oscillation Frequency

For the feedback factor to be **purely real** (zero phase shift), the imaginary term must vanish. Setting the imaginary part to zero:

$$\omega RC - \frac{1}{\omega RC} = 0 \implies \omega^2 R^2 C^2 = 1$$

Therefore, the **oscillation frequency** is:

$$\boxed{f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}}$$

At this frequency, the feedback factor reaches its **maximum real value**:

$$\beta_{max} = \frac{1}{3}$$

## 2.5 Required Amplifier Gain

To satisfy the Barkhausen magnitude condition |A·β| = 1 at the oscillation frequency:

$$A \cdot \beta_{max} = 1 \implies A \cdot \frac{1}{3} = 1 \implies A = 3$$

For the non-inverting op-amp configuration:

$$A_{CL} = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1} = 3 \implies \frac{R_f}{R_1} = 2$$

Thus, **R_f = 2·R_1** is the critical design relation. To ensure oscillations **build up** from noise, the gain is typically set slightly higher (A = 3.01 to 3.1) until the amplitude is stabilized by a non-linear element.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains ALL the formulas you must memorize for KTU examinations on the Wien Bridge Oscillator.

| # | Formula / Parameter | Mathematical Expression | Description / Design Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Oscillation frequency | $f_o = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | Primary design equation — sets R and C values |
| 2 | Angular frequency | $\omega_o = \dfrac{1}{RC}$ | Used in phasor analysis |
| 3 | Time period of oscillation | $T_o = 2\pi RC$ | Inverse of frequency |
| 4 | Feedback factor at f₀ | $\beta = \dfrac{1}{3}$ | Maximum transmission point |
| 5 | Required amplifier gain | $A = 3$ | For sustained oscillation |
| 6 | Closed-loop gain expression | $A_{CL} = 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}$ | Non-inverting op-amp gain |
| 7 | Gain resistor ratio | $R_f = 2 R_1$ | Critical design constraint |
| 8 | Generalized feedback factor | $\beta(j\omega) = \dfrac{1}{3 + j\left(\omega RC - \dfrac{1}{\omega RC}\right)}$ | Frequency-dependent response |
| 9 | Magnitude of β | $\vert \beta \vert = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{9 + \left(\omega RC - \dfrac{1}{\omega RC}\right)^2}}$ | For frequency response plot |
| 10 | Phase of β | $\phi = -\tan^{-1}\left[\dfrac{1}{3}\left(\omega RC - \dfrac{1}{\omega RC}\right)\right]$ | Phase shift at any frequency |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Where Wien Bridge Oscillators are used in industry:**
> - **Audio frequency test equipment** — function generators, audio test sets
> - **Tone generation in musical instruments** — electronic organs, synthesizers (historically)
> - **Phase-sensitive detectors in lock-in amplifiers**
> - **Carrier generation in analog telecommunications**
> - **Reference sine sources in calibration laboratories**
> - **Biopotential signal simulation** in medical electronics (low-frequency ECG/EEG test signals)
> - **Impedance measurement bridges** — the classical Wien bridge capacitance meter

The Wien Bridge Oscillator's primary advantage over LC oscillators is that it **does not require bulky inductors** at audio frequencies, making it economical and compact for PCB-based designs.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Feedback Factor and Oscillation Frequency

### Step 1 — Define the Two Impedances of the Wien Network

The Wien network has two arms:

**Series arm** (R in series with C):

$$Z_s = R + \frac{1}{j\omega C}$$

**Parallel arm** (R in parallel with C):

$$Z_p = \frac{R \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{j\omega C}\right)}{R + \dfrac{1}{j\omega C}}$$

**Reasoning:** A series-parallel RC combination exhibits a frequency-dependent response because the impedance of the capacitor changes with frequency. The series arm behaves as a **high-pass filter**, while the parallel arm behaves as a **low-pass filter**. Their cascade effect creates a **band-pass response** centered at a particular frequency.

### Step 2 — Simplify the Series Arm Impedance

Multiply numerator and denominator by $j\omega C$:

$$Z_s = \frac{j\omega RC + 1}{j\omega C} = \frac{1 + j\omega RC}{j\omega C}$$

**Reasoning:** Multiplying by the complex conjugate $j\omega C$ removes the imaginary denominator, giving a more tractable expression for the subsequent voltage divider.

### Step 3 — Simplify the Parallel Arm Impedance

Starting with:

$$Z_p = \frac{R \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{j\omega C}\right)}{R + \dfrac{1}{j\omega C}}$$

Multiply numerator and denominator by $j\omega C$:

$$Z_p = \frac{R}{1 + j\omega RC}$$

**Reasoning:** This is the classic parallel RC impedance formula. Note the symmetry: the series arm numerator is the same as the parallel arm denominator — this is what creates the **complementary** frequency response.

### Step 4 — Apply the Voltage Divider Rule to Find β

The output of the Wien network is taken across Z_p, with Z_s in series:

$$\beta = \frac{V_{feedback}}{V_{output}} = \frac{Z_p}{Z_s + Z_p}$$

Substitute the simplified expressions:

$$\beta = \frac{\dfrac{R}{1 + j\omega RC}}{\dfrac{1 + j\omega RC}{j\omega C} + \dfrac{R}{1 + j\omega RC}}$$

**Reasoning:** This is a non-inverting feedback arrangement — the voltage developed across the parallel RC arm is fed back to the non-inverting input of the op-amp.

### Step 5 — Common Denominator Construction

Multiply numerator and denominator by $j\omega C (1 + j\omega RC)$:

$$\beta = \frac{j\omega C \cdot R}{(1 + j\omega RC)^2 + j\omega RC}$$

**Reasoning:** The denominator is being expanded to combine both terms into a single polynomial expression.

### Step 6 — Expand the Denominator

Let $x = \omega RC$ for compactness:

$$(1 + jx)^2 + jx = 1 + 2jx - x^2 + jx = (1 - x^2) + j(3x)$$

Therefore:

$$\beta = \frac{jx}{(1 - x^2) + j(3x)} = \frac{jx}{3x + j(1 - x^2)}$$

**Reasoning:** Substituting back $x = \omega RC$ reveals a beautiful symmetry in the expression. The denominator's real part is $3x$ and the imaginary part is $(1 - x^2)$.

### Step 7 — Convert to the Standard Form

Multiply numerator and denominator by 3 to expose the canonical Wien form:

$$\beta = \frac{1}{3 + j\left(\dfrac{1}{x} - x\right)} = \frac{1}{3 + j\left(\dfrac{1}{\omega RC} - \omega RC\right)}$$

**Reasoning:** This is the **canonical form of the Wien Bridge feedback factor**. The form $\frac{1}{3 + j(\ldots)}$ clearly shows that the **maximum magnitude of β** occurs when the imaginary term is zero.

### Step 8 — Determine the Frequency of Maximum Feedback

The magnitude of β is:

$$\vert \beta \vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{9 + \left(\dfrac{1}{\omega RC} - \omega RC\right)^2}}$$

The denominator is **minimum** when:

$$\frac{1}{\omega RC} - \omega RC = 0 \implies \frac{1}{\omega RC} = \omega RC \implies \omega^2 R^2 C^2 = 1$$

**Reasoning:** This is the resonance condition of the Wien network. At this frequency, the imaginary part of the denominator vanishes, and β becomes purely real.

### Step 9 — Solve for the Oscillation Frequency

$$\omega_o^2 = \frac{1}{R^2 C^2} \implies \omega_o = \frac{1}{RC}$$

Converting to frequency in Hz:

$$\boxed{f_o = \frac{\omega_o}{2\pi} = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}}$$

**Reasoning:** This is the **single most important design equation** of the Wien Bridge Oscillator. It tells the designer that for a desired output frequency, the product RC must equal $1/(2\pi f_o)$.

### Step 10 — Maximum Value of β at Resonance

Substituting $\omega = \omega_o = 1/RC$ into the β equation:

$$\beta_{max} = \frac{1}{3 + j(0)} = \frac{1}{3}$$

**Reasoning:** At the resonant frequency, the Wien network passes back exactly **one-third** of the output voltage to the amplifier input. To sustain oscillation, the amplifier must therefore provide a gain of exactly 3 (or slightly more for startup).

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Frequency Response Characteristics

| Frequency Condition | ωRC Value | Imaginary Term in Denominator | β Magnitude | β Phase | Physical Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **f ≪ f₀** | ωRC ≪ 1 | Large positive | β → 0 | +90° | Capacitor in series arm is open, low feedback |
| **f = f₀** | ωRC = 1 | Zero | β = 1/3 (max) | 0° | **Resonance — oscillation frequency** |
| **f ≫ f₀** | ωRC ≫ 1 | Large negative | β → 0 | −90° | Capacitor in parallel arm is shorted, low feedback |
| **f = 0.5 f₀** | ωRC = 0.5 | Positive | β < 1/3 | +ve | Below resonance, leading phase |
| **f = 2 f₀** | ωRC = 2 | Negative | β < 1/3 | −ve | Above resonance, lagging phase |

## 3.3 Design Algorithm — Numerical Worked Example

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Specification:** Design a Wien Bridge Oscillator using µA741 op-amp to generate a **1 kHz** sinusoidal output. Assume supply voltage ±15 V. Use practical component values.

### Step A — Choose Capacitor Value

For audio frequency work, a standard value of $C = 0.01 \mu F = 10 nF$ is practical (non-polarized ceramic or polyester film).

### Step B — Calculate Resistor Value

$$R = \frac{1}{2\pi f_o C} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 1000 \times 10 \times 10^{-9}}$$

$$R = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{10^5}{2\pi}$$

$$R = 15.915 \times 10^3 \, \Omega = 15.92 \, k\Omega$$

**Practical design choice:** Use a standard value $R = 15.9 \, k\Omega$ (E96 series) or a $15 \, k\Omega$ with a small trimmer for fine adjustment.

### Step C — Calculate the Gain-Setting Resistors

For the non-inverting amplifier with gain A = 3:

$$A = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1} = 3 \implies R_f = 2 R_1$$

Choose $R_1 = 10 \, k\Omega$ (standard), then $R_f = 20 \, k\Omega$.

### Step D — Design Amplitude Stabilization (Practical Realization)

To prevent output clipping and reduce distortion, replace the fixed $R_f$ with a non-linear element. Two common approaches:

**Approach 1 — Back-to-Back Zener Diodes in the feedback loop:**
- Use 5.6 V Zener diodes in anti-series
- They conduct when output exceeds the Zener voltage, effectively reducing the gain
- This stabilizes amplitude automatically

**Approach 2 — Thermistor (Negative Temperature Coefficient) for $R_f$:**
- As output amplitude rises, the thermistor heats up
- Its resistance decreases, reducing the gain
- Self-regulating amplitude control

### Step E — Verify the Design

Final component selection:

| Component | Value | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| R (Wien network) | $15.9 \, k\Omega$ | Sets oscillation frequency |
| C (Wien network) | $10 \, nF$ | Sets oscillation frequency |
| $R_1$ (feedback) | $10 \, k\Omega$ | Sets lower feedback factor |
| $R_f$ (feedback) | $20 \, k\Omega$ (or thermistor) | Sets gain to 3 |
| Op-amp | µA741 | Active gain element |
| Supply | $\pm 15 \, V$ | Standard op-amp supply |

**Predicted output:** $1 \, kHz$ sine wave, amplitude limited to $\pm 13.5 \, V$ (slightly below supply rails), distortion < 1% with amplitude stabilization.

## 3.4 Derivation: Why a Non-Linear Element is Mandatory

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The amplitude stabilization problem:**
> 
> If the gain is set to exactly 3, the oscillation is at a **knife-edge** — any small decrease in component values due to temperature drift will kill the oscillation; any small increase will cause the amplitude to grow until the op-amp saturates and clips.
> 
> Therefore, the practical gain is set slightly **greater than 3** (e.g., 3.01) so that the oscillation amplitude naturally grows on power-up. As the amplitude grows, the non-linear element (Zener or thermistor) reduces the effective gain to exactly 3, stabilizing the amplitude.

This is the **automatic gain control (AGC)** principle applied to the Wien Bridge Oscillator.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Circuit Schematic — Wien Bridge Oscillator (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional architecture** of the Wien Bridge Oscillator, illustrating the signal flow, feedback loops, and frequency-selective network.

```mermaid
graph LR
    node1["DC Supply Plus Minus 15V"] --> node2["Op-Amp uA741"]
```

| **Pin 1** | Offset Null | Not used (or connected to 10 kΩ offset adjustment pot) |
| **Pin 2** | Inverting Input | Connected to junction of R₁ and R_f |

| **Pin 6** | Output | Connected to CRO (via BNC) and to series RC arm |
